note_summary:
The patient is a female with a recent history of left vertebral dissection without associated ischemia, for which she was taking aspirin 81 mg daily. She presented with transient right arm numbness and right neck pain. She was found to have a new right proximal V2 vertebral dissection. The patient reported that she was healthy until about three weeks prior to the current visit. She woke up one day with a stiff neck on the left side, which worsened over the weeks and was accompanied by a throbbing holocephalic headache, strange noises in her left ear, and lightheadedness upon standing. She had a CTA which showed left V3-4 dissection, but an MRI showed no infarct. She was given aspirin 81 mg daily and discharged. Since discharge, she had been doing well and had not missed any doses of her aspirin. She reported trying cocaine for the first time the night before her current visit.

The patient's family history includes parents with alcohol and drug problems and a brother who had a seizure in the setting of a concussion. There is no family history of strokes, blood vessel problems, or connective tissue disease.

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake and cooperative, with a mild occipital headache. She denied changes in vision, dysphagia, changes in voice, double vision, dizziness, recurrence of numbness/tingling or weakness. She had no recent fevers or chills, or infectious symptoms. Her neurological examination was normal.

Lab results showed an elevated absolute neutrophil count of 6.89 and a slightly elevated WBC count of 11.6. An MRI of the head without contrast showed no acute infarct or intracranial hemorrhage, but there were 2 punctate FLAIR hyperintensities of the right frontal lobe and right postcentral gyrus, which were nonspecific. A transthoracic echo report showed normal biventricular cavity sizes and regional/global biventricular systolic function, with no valvular pathology or pathologic flow identified.

The patient was advised to stay for an MRA head and neck to further evaluate the dissections, but she left against medical advice due to lack of child care at home. She was counseled against additional drug use and was advised to present urgently to the ED if she experiences any additional neurological symptoms. The MRA of the head and neck was ordered as an outpatient to be completed prior to a neurology follow-up. The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of vertebral artery dissection. Her discharge medications included aspirin 81 mg PO daily.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to chest pain and vaginal bleeding. She has a history of aortic aneurysm and fibroids. The chest pain was described as sudden onset substernal pressure, radiating to the left shoulder, and has been continuous for a few minutes. The patient also reported lightheadedness and dyspnea on exertion. The vaginal bleeding had been ongoing for 16 days, with the patient going through several pads per day. She had a D+C and IUD placement a few weeks prior. The patient also reported occasional chest pain when she is anemic and loses a lot of blood.

The patient had pre-operative testing for elbow surgery, during which an abnormal EKG was found. An echo revealed a 4cm aortic aneurysm, which has been stable since its discovery. In the ED, the patient's Hct was 31.7, up from a previous 26. A CT chest scan showed a 4.1 cm diameter of the ascending aorta. The patient was seen by ob/gyn and vaginal bleeding was not thought to be an acute issue. 

The patient's past medical history includes diabetes, dyslipidemia, hypertension, an enlarged aorta, fibroids, right and left elbow surgery, surgery for a herniated disc, appendectomy, and lap chole. She has no known cardiac history. Her family history includes hypertension and HLD in her mother, MI in her brother, and CHF in her sister, who died from CHF. There is no known history of dissection in the family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be morbidly obese with a large neck, making it difficult to assess JVP. She had a pressure ulcer on her buttocks bilaterally. Her cardiac, lung, abdomen, and extremities examination was normal. 

Lab results showed a mildly leftward axis on EKG, a 4cm aortic aneurysm on echo, and an enlarged ascending aorta on CT chest. Pelvic ultrasound showed uterine fibroids and an appropriately positioned IUD. Lab results also showed abnormal RBC, HGB, HCT, MCH, MCHC, RDW, and moderate leukocytes in urine. 

The patient was on provera and ferrous sulfate 65mg BID upon admission. She also had zinc oxide applied to her pressure ulcers. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of aortic aneurysm, chest pain NOS, and vaginal bleeding. She was advised to follow up with her primary care doctor for an outpatient stress test due to her strong family history of heart disease. She was also advised to continue following up with her gynecology team for her vaginal bleeding. The patient insisted on being treated with antibiotics for her urine findings, despite being informed of the risks of taking unnecessary antibiotics.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to persistent lower chest and epigastric pain radiating to the back, despite normal blood pressure. The pain was associated with an intramural hematoma and penetrating aortic ulceration of the descending thoracic aorta. His past medical history includes hypertension, PTSD, and mood disorder. He has also undergone lipoma removal and facial reconstruction following a motor vehicle accident. His mother had a myocardial infarction, but there is no family history of connective tissue disorders.

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with no scleral icterus, clear lungs, and a soft, non-distended abdomen that was tender with deep palpation in the lower-mid region. No pulsatile mass was detected. His extremities were warm and well perfused, with palpable bilateral pedal pulses and no wounds or ulcers.

Abnormal lab results included RBC count of 3.82, Hgb of 13.0, Hct of 36.0, MCH of 33.9, MCHC of 36.0, PTT of 44.9, Glucose of 102, ALT of 79, AST of 94, and Calcium of 8.3. A CT angiogram revealed an intramural hematoma extending from the level of the left subclavian artery and terminating at the level of the renal arteries. There were also intramural ulcers at the level of the descending thoracic aorta. The descending thoracic aorta measured up to approximately 4.4 cm.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent thoracic endograft repair for the penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer. Post-operatively, he was stable and his blood pressure was adequately controlled with oral medication. His diet was advanced when appropriate and he was encouraged to ambulate early. He had a Foley catheter placed in the OR, which was removed without difficulty. His white blood count and fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. His groin puncture sites healed well without hematoma or ecchymosis. He was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, and was ambulating independently.

Upon admission, the patient was on Lisinopril 40 mg PO daily.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, intramural hematoma, and penetrating ulceration of the descending thoracic aorta, was transferred from another facility after presenting with interscapular back pain characterized as sharp between the shoulder blades, causing difficulty breathing. His past surgical history includes lipoma removal and facial reconstruction following a motor vehicle accident. The patient's mother had a myocardial infarction, but there is no family history of connective tissue disorders.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Lisinopril, Aspirin, Ibuprofen, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Gabapentin, HydrOXYzine, and Omeprazole. His physical examination was generally unremarkable, with mild tenderness to touch in the epigastrium just right of the midline. 

Admission labs showed abnormal MCH at 33.0 and RDWSD at 51.7. A CTA revealed a new small irregularity or saccular outpouching of the anterior wall of the descending thoracic aorta, 3.5 cm above the level of the aortic stent graft. There was no evidence of ascending thoracic aortic dissection or acute pulmonary embolism. The visualized abdominal aorta near the diaphragmatic hiatus was aneurysmal, measuring 3.4 x 3.9 cm. 

During the hospital stay, serial CTAs showed interval resolution of much of the periaortic hematoma with aortic remodeling. The patient's ongoing pain was mild and largely positional. Blood pressures were well controlled and remained stable throughout his admission. A decision was made to observe with serial CTA of the torso. No surgical intervention was recommended during this admission due to risks of paraplegia given the area of coverage and CT stability. 

Discharge labs showed abnormal MCH at 32.8 and RDWSD at 51.9. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of aortic aneurysm outpouching. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was advised to follow up with his primary care physician to monitor blood pressures with the goal to keep his systolic blood pressure less than 140.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to complaints of back pain, fatigue, weakness, and palpitations. His medical history includes hypertension, Hepatitis C, a history of alcohol abuse, and a history of penetrating descending thoracic aortic ulcers. He had previously undergone thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) and was transferred from another facility for further evaluation of his back pain. He reported that his blood pressure and heart rate have been high at home. He was last admitted for similar back pain, which was not deemed musculoskeletal in origin by orthopedic surgery. He was also briefly seen in the emergency department for a subdural hematoma, which was observed and he was sent home. His past surgical history includes lipoma removal and facial reconstruction following a motor vehicle accident. His mother had a myocardial infarction, but there is no family history of connective tissue disorders.

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with no significant findings in the cardiovascular, pulmonary, gastrointestinal, and neurological systems. His incisions and wounds were well healed, and his extremities were warm with good perfusion, but diffusely tender.

Lab results showed an elevated PTT of 40.1, a low platelet count of 136, and a low red blood cell count of 4.48. His mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) was high at 33.7, and his red cell distribution width standard deviation (RDWSD) was also high at 52.6. His sodium level was low at 132, and his total CO2 was low at 20.

During his hospital stay, imaging studies were conducted as part of his workup. His abdominal saccular aneurysm below the stent was largely unchanged, and a liver nodule was incidentally found. The leading diagnosis for his back pain was fibromyalgia or myofascial pain syndrome. No clear musculoskeletal or thoracic causes were found, and no arrhythmias were detected. The liver nodule was consistent with hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), and a hepatology consultation was recommended. The patient was started on amitriptyline for possible fibromyalgia.

The patient's medications upon admission included aspirin, gabapentin, lisinopril, metoprolol succinate, omeprazole, and hydroxyzine. His discharge diagnoses were back pain, fatigue/weakness, and heart palpitations. He was discharged in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a middle-aged male with a history of hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta post TEVAR, HCV genotype 3A, cervical disk herniation, GIB, possible COPD, alcoholism, and chronic HepC (untreated). He has a history of alcohol-related cirrhosis complicated by varices and portal hypertensive gastropathy, and hepatocellular carcinoma post RFA. His surgical history includes lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction post MVA, thoracic endograft repair for penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral metacarpal injuries post repair. 

The patient presented with abdominal pain and muscle weakness. He reported experiencing weakness, blurry vision, forgetfulness, headache, chest pain, and abdominal pain for about 2 days. He also reported aches in all major muscle groups and denied any fevers. His abdominal pain was epigastric and radiated to the right. He had one episode of diarrhea and felt feverish/flushed, but no measured fever or chills. He was seen by GI who recommended MRCP. He also reported muscle pain and cramping if he sat still for too long. He was recently diagnosed with HIV with a VL of 353,000 and CD4 723. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Lisinopril 20 mg and Metoprolol Succinate XL 50 mg. His physical exam revealed tenderness to epigastric and RUQ palpation with guarding, no rebound tenderness, non-distended abdomen, and appreciable clubbing. His labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Mono, AbsLymp, AbsMono, PTT, Na, ALT, AST, LD, AlkPhos, TotBili, Albumin, and Calcium. His imaging showed persistent hepatic cirrhosis, persistent borderline splenomegaly with multiple calcified granulomas, and a hypodense posterior lateral R hepatic lobe segment 8 lesion. 

The patient's mother had an MI, but there is no family history of connective tissue disorder. His discharge diagnosis included abdominal pain, dilated common bile duct without evidence of obstruction, alcoholic cirrhosis complicated by new hepatic encephalopathy, ascites, and chronic Hepatitis C infection. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was discharged with instructions to follow up with his primary care doctor and other specialists for further treatment.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of penetrating descending aortic ulcer, chronic Hepatitis C (untreated, genotype 3A, VL 256,000), alcohol-related cirrhosis complicated by varices/portal hypertensive gastropathy, hepatocellular carcinoma post radiofrequency ablation, and HIV. He was admitted due to generalized weakness and fatigue, which reportedly developed soon after receiving a meningococcal vaccine. Initial vitals were stable and physical examination was notable for the patient being awake and alert, with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding, possible COPD, and alcoholism. He has undergone several surgical procedures including lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction post motor vehicle accident, thoracic endograft repair for penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral metacarpal injuries repair. 

The patient's family history reveals that his mother had a myocardial infarction, but no connective tissue disorders were reported. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including trazodone, albuterol sulfate, rifaximin, zinc sulfate, calcium carbonate, oxycodone, spironolactone, furosemide, atorvastatin, dolutegravir, metoprolol succinate, nicotine patch, lactulose, ondansetron, and descovy. 

Lab results showed several abnormal findings including ALT 74, AST 140, Alk ph 189, Tbili 2.1, Albumin 3.2, INR 1.5, Lactate 1.8, and platelet count 104. Imaging revealed a cirrhotic appearing liver with a known RFA site in the right hepatic lobe, and no evidence of portal vein thrombosis. 

The patient's condition continued to worsen, with numbness and tingling in his fingers and legs, difficulty breathing, and intermittent abdominal pain over the past month. Further tests revealed positive Lyme IgM, slightly elevated ACE at 135, and normal ACTH. The patient was recommended to complete a ten-day course of doxycycline for Lyme disease. 

Upon discharge, the patient's condition was stable, with clear and coherent mental status, and independent ambulatory activity status. His discharge diagnosis included Lyme disease and weakness.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a past medical history of Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) cirrhosis, Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV), and a history of radiofrequency ablation (RFA), presented with a week-long history of acute on chronic abdominal pain. He reported chronic diffuse abdominal pain that had worsened acutely in the past week, accompanied by increased bloating, foul-smelling burps, heartburn, and intolerance to heat. He was found to have leukocytosis and hepatocellular/obstructive liver function tests (LFTs) on outpatient labs. The patient's other past medical history includes hypertension (HTN), Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), mood disorder, penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta status post thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR), cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB), possible Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), alcoholism, and chronic Hepatitis C (untreated), alcohol-related cirrhosis complicated by varices/portal hypertensive gastropathy, and hepatocellular carcinoma status post RFA. His surgical history includes lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction after a motor vehicle accident, thoracic endograft repair for penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral metacarpal injuries status post repair. His family history includes a mother who had a myocardial infarction.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Calcium Carbonate, Descovy (emtricitabine-tenofovir alafen), Dolutegravir, Furosemide, Lactulose, Ondansetron, Oxycodone, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Trazodone, Albuterol Sulfate, Atorvastatin, Ipratropium-Albuterol Inhalation Spray, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Nicotine Patch, and Zinc Sulfate. 

During his hospital stay, the patient received multiple doses of IV Morphine Sulfate, IV Ampicillin-Sulbactam, PO/NG Rifaximin, and PO/NG Lactulose. His labs showed elevated white blood cell count (WBC), elevated liver function tests (LFTs), and abnormal complete blood count (CBC) and basic metabolic panel (BMP) results. Imaging showed no cholelithiasis or sonographic evidence of acute cholecystitis, cirrhotic liver morphology, a segment VII ablation cavity, and a patent portal vein. There was no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality. 

The patient was consulted by the transplant surgery and hepatology teams, who determined no acute surgical issues based on ultrasound imaging and recommended admission to medicine. The patient reported persistent abdominal pain but was able to tolerate a regular diet after magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP). 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by abdominal pain, transaminitis, left lower lobe consolidation, shoulder pain, and HCV cirrhosis. He was treated presumptively for acalculous cholecystitis with IV Unasyn transitioned to Vanc/Zosyn for a total of 8 days. He was discharged on 5 additional days of Augmentin to complete his course. His left lower lobe consolidation was thought to likely represent pneumonia for which he was already on Vanc/Zosyn. Azithromycin was also added for 5 days to cover atypicals. His HIV was well-controlled, and he was on emtricitabine + tenofovir + dolutegravir. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with leukocytosis, abdominal pain, and HCV cirrhosis. His discharge medications included Amoxicillin-Clavulanic Acid, albuterol sulfate, Atorvastatin, Calcium Carbonate, Descovy, Dolutegravir, Furosemide, Ipratropium-Albuterol Inhalation Spray, Lactulose, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Nicotine Patch, Ondansetron, Oxycodone, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Trazodone, and Zinc Sulfate. The patient was advised to follow up with his primary doctor and the transplant team.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) and alcohol-induced cirrhosis (Child B) complicated by Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) post Radiofrequency Ablation (RFA), well-controlled HIV, Hypertension (HTN), Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), Mood disorder, Penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta post Thoracic Endovascular Aortic Repair (TEVAR), Cervical disk herniation, Gastrointestinal Bleeding (GIB), and possible Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), presented with lightheadedness, fatigue, and recurrent abdominal pain. He had a recent admission for diffuse abdominal pain and transaminitis, which was consistent with acalculous cholecystitis and left lower lobe pneumonia. He was treated with antibiotics and discharged, but his abdominal pain returned and he was readmitted for intravenous diuresis and paracentesis. 

The patient also reported muscle cramps, worse in the calves, but also up to his waist. He had no other neurological signs: no blurry vision, focal weakness, numbness, headache. He had subjective fevers but no chills. He also had night sweats, was not short of breath at rest, but with exertion, or with palpation to his abdomen. He reported intermittent chest palpitations at rest. He had nausea without vomiting and a poor appetite. He had a nonbloody bowel movement earlier today, with no diarrhea. He last urinated earlier today. He has received his hepatitis A vaccine and has one dose left of his Hepatitis B vaccine series. 

The patient had a recent admission for Lyme disease that was treated with doxycycline. The patient reports he still has some tingling at the tip of his fingers bilaterally and neck stiffness, which he reports is consistent with his prior presentation of Lyme. He also clarifies that the neck stiffness is also a chronic presentation. 

On physical examination, the patient was found to be tender in the right upper quadrant and epigastrium without rebound or guarding. He became tachycardic and tachypneic upon being palpated. Labs showed elevated AST/ALT/AlkPhos/Tbili and increased INR and PTT values, that were consistent with baseline. A right upper quadrant ultrasound with Doppler showed a distended gallbladder, but was negative for cholelithiasis, cholecystitis. Chest (PA and lateral x ray) showed an improved left lower lobe pneumonia. He received PO lactulose 30 cc, oxycodone 5 mg, and Zofran 4mg IV. 20 gauge IV was placed in his right forearm. Cultures of blood, urine, and sputum were sent to be processed. EKG showed sinus rhythm, with no signs of ischemia and results consistent with prior EKG. 

The patient's mother has a history of Coronary Artery Disease (CAD). 

Upon admission, the patient was on Atorvastatin, Calcium Carbonate, Descovy, Dolutegravir, Torsemide, Lactulose, Nicotine Patch, Ondansetron, Oxycodone, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, Trazodone, Zinc Sulfate, albuterol sulfate, Furosemide, and ipratropium-albuterol. 

Abnormal lab results include elevated RBC (4.24), MCV (105), MCH (36.1), RDW (17.2), RDWSD (67.8), Monos (15.7), AbsMono (1.13), K (5.9), HCO3 (20), ALT (148), AST (202), AlkPhos (247), TotBili (2.8), Calcium (8.3), Albumin (2.7), and bacteria and blood in urine. 

Imaging showed left lower lobe pneumonia, distended gallbladder without cholelithiasis, wall thickening, or other sonographic signs of acute cholecystitis, cirrhotic liver with heterogeneous lesion in the right lobe corresponding to the site of prior ablation, patent hepatic vasculature with possible slow flow in the left portal vein, and no ascites. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a significant medical history including Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) and alcohol-induced cirrhosis (Child B), HIV, hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding, and a possible diagnosis of COPD, was admitted due to abdominal pain and volume overload. He has a history of a penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, which was treated with thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR). He also has a history of facial reconstruction following a motor vehicle accident and lipoma removal from his left upper arm. 

Upon admission, the patient complained of worsening abdominal pain over the last five days, not relieved by his standing oxycodone. He also reported lethargy, inability to sleep, and a 7-pound weight gain over two days. He denied missing medication doses or changes in diet. His pain was located in the middle of his abdomen, from the epigastrium down to the umbilicus. He denied radiation, association with eating, vomiting, or changes in bowel movements. 

Initial vitals in the ED were T97.6, HR91, BP156/82, RR18, PO2 96. The patient was tender to palpation throughout, most notably in the epigastrium and around the umbilicus. Imaging showed no ascites and a patent portal vein. Abnormal lab results included WBC 10.7 with a left shift, INR 1.5, K 5.1, ALT 100, AST 176, Alkphos 205, Tbili 3.3, lipase 63, and albumin 3.2. The patient was given oxycodone 5mg x2. 

The patient has a history of multiple recent admissions for abdominal pain and volume overload. During a previous admission, he was diagnosed with acalculous cholecystitis and left lower lobe pneumonia. He was treated with IV amp/sulbactam, which was transitioned to vanc/zosyn, and then to augmentin+azithromycin to complete as an outpatient. He was also admitted for volume overload, during which 0.9L of fluid was removed via paracentesis, with no spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) detected. He was diuresed with IV lasix. On discharge, Lasix 10mg was stopped, and he was started on Torsemide 20mg, and Spironolactone increased from 25mg to 50mg. 

The patient's family history includes coronary artery disease in his mother. His medications upon admission included Atorvastatin, Calcium Carbonate, Descovy, Dolutegravir, Lactulose, Oxycodone, Rifaximin, Trazodone, Multivitamins, Albuterol Sulfate, Ipratropium-Albuterol, Ondansetron, and Furosemide. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's abdominal pain was thought to be secondary to GERD and his PPI was increased to BID dosing. His volume overload was likely due to a suboptimal diuretic regimen. He received IV Lasix with improvement of weight and euvolemic on exam. He was transitioned to lasix 40mg po. His INR increased from a baseline of 1.5 to 1.8 on admission, likely due to liver disease, but could also be secondary to inadequate oral intake. After receiving oral vitamin K 5mg, it decreased back to 1.5. His HIV is well controlled, with his most recent CD4 count being ___, and his viral load being less than the assay. He continued on his home regimen of Descovy and Dolutegravir. 

Abnormal lab results on discharge included RBC 3.56, Hgb 12.9, Hct 38.5, MCV 108, MCH 36.2, RDW 17.2, Plt Ct 86, ALT 100, AST 158, AlkPhos 160, and TotBili 3.0. His discharge weight was 185.6 lb. His discharge diagnoses were abdominal pain and HCV Cirrhosis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to abdominal pain. He has a history of Hepatitis C cirrhosis (Childs B), HIV (CD4 1106), hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta post TEVAR, cervical disk herniation, GIB, and possible COPD. His surgical history includes lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction post MVA (wire mesh) after a bicycle accident, thoracic endograft repair for penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral metacarpal injuries post repair. His family history includes CAD in his mother.

The patient has had multiple recent admissions for abdominal pain with some equivocal imaging for cholecystitis. However, surgical teams have not considered him to have acute cholecystitis and so no intervention was pursued. His physical exam upon admission showed severe tenderness to palpation diffusely, most notable in RUQ and RLQ. His discharge physical exam showed tenderness to palpation throughout the abdomen, particularly RUQ/RLQ.

His admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.66), MCV (109), MCH (37.4), RDW (17.2), RDWSD (69.7), Plt Ct (79), Neuts (71.8), AbsNeut (6.84), PTT (41.4), Glucose (101), AnGap (8), ALT (176), AST (304), AlkPhos (193), TotBili (4.1), and Albumin (2.6). His discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.37), Hgb (12.3), Hct (36.3), MCV (108), MCH (36.5), RDW (17.5), RDWSD (70.4), Plt Ct (81), PTT (72.6), Glucose (108), Cl (109), ALT (123), AST (217), AlkPhos (155), TotBili (3.4), and Albumin (2.0).

Imaging showed a cirrhotic liver with moderate perihepatic ascites, a distended gallbladder with wall thickening, and a patent portal vein. The MRCP showed cirrhotic liver morphology and decreased gallbladder distension with persistent mild wall edema. The CTA of the abdomen showed cirrhotic liver morphology with evidence of portal hypertension and new trace ascites.

The patient's hospital course was complicated by one day of axillary fevers. He was discharged with a plan for follow up of his recurrent hepatocellular carcinoma for radiofrequency ablation, which may help his pain. His pain was controlled with Oxycodone and Amytriptiline was started to help with a possible component of somatic symptom disorder.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including albuterol sulfate, Atorvastatin, Calcium Carbonate, Descovy, Dolutegravir, Lactulose, Multivitamins, OxyCODONE, Rifaximin, ipratropium-albuterol, Ondansetron, TraZODone, Pantoprazole, Furosemide, and Spironolactone. Upon discharge, new medications were added including Acetaminophen, Amitriptyline, Maalox/Diphenhydramine/Lidocaine, Polyethylene Glycol, and Senna. The dosage of Oxycodone was changed.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Hepatitis C cirrhosis (Childs B), HIV (CD4 1106), Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) and rising AFP, was admitted for abdominal pain. He had recently undergone a radiofrequency ablation (RFA) procedure, which was followed by severe post-procedure pain. The patient also reported chronic left shoulder pain. His past medical history includes hypertension, PTSD, mood disorder, a penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer of the descending thoracic aorta post TEVAR, cervical disk herniation, GIB, and possible COPD. His surgical history includes lipoma removal, facial reconstruction post-MVA, thoracic endograft repair for a penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer, and bilateral metacarpal injuries repair. His family history includes CAD in his mother.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Calcium Carbonate, albuterol sulfate, Senna, Polyethylene Glycol, Maalox/Diphenhydramine/Lidocaine, Amitriptyline, Acetaminophen, Trazodone, Spironolactone, Rifaximin, Pantoprazole, Ondansetron, Multivitamins, Lactulose, Furosemide, Dolutegravir, Atorvastatin, Descovy, ipratropium-albuterol, and Oxycodone. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent successful RFA ablation. His abdominal pain increased post-procedure but improved over 24 hours. His chronic right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain of uncertain etiology was investigated with a HIDA scan and MRCP, both of which were negative. His left shoulder pain persisted throughout his hospitalization. An MRI showed a lesion over the acromion, concerning for a cyst or a neoplastic process. 

His labs on admission showed abnormal values for RBC (3.39), Hgb (12.5), Hct (36.7), MCV (108), MCH (36.9), RDW (17.4), RDWSD (70.6), Glucose (118), Na (133), ALT (173), AST (526), LD(LDH) (501), AlkPhos (181), TotBili (4.9), and Calcium (7.8). His discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.10), Hgb (11.6), Hct (33.4), MCV (108), MCH (37.4), RDW (17.4), RDWSD (69.7), Plt Ct (99), PTT (46.7), AnGap (6), ALT (213), AST (650), AlkPhos (172), TotBili (4.1), Albumin (2.1), Calcium (7.5), and Phos (2.6). 

The patient was discharged with diagnoses of Radiofrequency Ablation of Hepatocellular Carcinoma, Hepatitis C Cirrhosis, Acute on chronic abdominal pain, Chronic left shoulder pain, HIV, Hypertension, and Hyperlipidemia. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. His discharge medications were the same as his admission medications.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a right-hand-dominant male, was admitted due to persistent left shoulder pain. He has a history of Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) and alcohol-induced cirrhosis complicated by hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), HIV, hypertension (HTN), post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), mood disorder, cervical disk herniation, gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB), and a possible chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). He has previously undergone radiofrequency ablation for HCC, lipoma removal from the left upper arm, facial reconstruction following a motor vehicle accident, thoracic endograft repair for a penetrating thoracic aortic ulcer, and repair of bilateral metacarpal injuries. 

The patient has been experiencing left shoulder pain for about nine months and was found to have a small distal left acromion mass on MRI. He has been using a sling, which has significantly decreased his pain, but he still has persistent left shoulder pain with decreased sensation throughout the hand. He underwent left acromion curettage and biopsy during this admission. 

During his hospital stay, the patient had an episode of suicidal ideation, which was evaluated by psychiatry. They deemed him fit for discharge, attributing the ideation to his overall diagnosis/prognosis and coping mechanisms. His pain was well controlled with oral medications, and his incisions were clean, dry, and intact. He was weight bearing as tolerated in the left upper extremity. 

The patient's home medications were continued throughout his hospitalization. These include Bisacodyl, Oxycodone, Senna, Albuterol Inhaler, Amitriptyline, Atorvastatin, Dolutegravir, Emtricitabine-Tenofovir alafen (Descovy), Lactulose, Magnesium Oxide, Rifaximin, Spironolactone, and Trazodone. 

The patient's family history includes coronary artery disease (CAD) in his mother. Upon discharge, the patient was stable and was recommended for discharge to a rehab facility. His discharge diagnosis was left acromion curettage and biopsy.
label: training_controls

note_summary:

He reported a sudden increase in pain after reaching for a glass of water, which required an increase in his home oxycodone dosage. He also reported right anterior leg pain for the past month, dry mouth possibly due to diuretics, and a pounding headache on the crown of his head occurring twice a week over the last month. 

The patient's past oncologic history includes initial diagnosis of HCC, subsequent radiofrequency ablation (RFA), and consideration for transplantation. However, restaging imaging showed arterial enhancement at the RF ablation cavity, suspicious for recurrence, leading to repeat RFA ablation. He also underwent surgery for a lytic lesion in his left AC joint, which was revealed to be metastatic carcinoma consistent with HCC. 

His past medical history includes a penetrating thoracic aortic aneurysm of the descending aorta status post TAVR, cirrhosis due to chronic genotype hepatitis C and alcohol, hypertension, PTSD, cervical disc herniations, gastritis with bleeding, lipoma, and HIV on antiretroviral therapy (ART). His family history includes his mother with coronary artery disease and multiple prior family malignancies of unknown type.

During his hospital stay, the patient completed his 10 fractions of palliative XRT and was started on a morphine PCA, which was later transitioned to Oxycontin and Oxycodone. His urine analysis showed moderate leukocytes, 7 WBC, and few bacteria. His imaging showed a cirrhotic liver with moderate ascites, a distal descending thoracic aortic aneurysm measuring up to 4.0 cm, and a suprarenal aortic aneurysm measuring up to 4.0 cm. 

His past medical history includes a penetrating thoracic aortic aneurysm of the descending aorta status post TAVR, cirrhosis due to chronic genotype hepatitis C and alcohol, hypertension, PTSD, cervical disc herniations, gastritis with bleeding, lipoma, and HIV on ART. note_summary:
The patient is a male with a past medical history of Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) cirrhosis, HIV co-infection, portal hypertension, and Hepatocellular Carcinoma (HCC) with metastatic disease to the left shoulder. He has undergone resection and palliative XRT for the metastatic disease. He was admitted due to worsening right lower quadrant abdominal pain. He had recently been admitted for refractory ascites and abdominal pain, during which he underwent a therapeutic paracentesis with 1.5 L of ascites removed. His pain was controlled with oral oxycodone and he was discharged with a plan for outpatient follow-up. 

Upon readmission, the patient reported a "stabbing" pain in his abdomen, which he described as feeling like his abdomen had "popped". Initial vitals were stable and physical examination was notable for right lower quadrant tenderness and 2+ pitting edema bilaterally. Lab results showed abnormalities in sodium (130), AST (72), alkaline phosphatase (180), total bilirubin (5.8), direct bilirubin (3.0), albumin (2.3), white blood cell count (10.1), hemoglobin/hematocrit (11.4/33.2), and platelet count (97). Imaging showed mild bilateral pulmonary vascular congestion and a cirrhotic liver with moderate ascites. 

The patient was given various medications including oxycodone, furosemide, lactulose, spironolactone, rifaximin, and morphine sulfate. Hepatology evaluated the patient and recommended non-contrast imaging for left lower quadrant pain and a repeat diagnostic paracentesis to look for secondary bacterial peritonitis. 

The patient's past medical history also includes a penetrating thoracic aortic aneurysm of the descending aorta status post Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (TAVR), hypertension, Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), cervical disc herniations, gastritis with bleeding, lipoma, and HIV on Antiretroviral Therapy (ART). His family history includes his mother with Coronary Artery Disease (CAD) and multiple prior family malignancies of unknown type. 

Upon arrival to the floor, the patient reported "rebound" tenderness in his lower abdomen and stated that he felt like he had "gained back all the weight they took off" while he was admitted despite being compliant with his medications. He also reported decreased urine output since returning home, palpitations during the worst of the pain, and a feeling of heat inside his abdomen. He denied fevers, chills, and shortness of breath. 

Admission labs showed abnormalities in sodium (129), AST (76), LDH (310), alkaline phosphatase (184), total bilirubin (6.6), albumin (2.2), calcium (8.0), phosphate (2.4), haptoglobin (<10), RBC (3.27), hemoglobin (11.6), hematocrit (34.0), MCV (104), MCH (35.5), RDW (19.2), absolute monocytes (0.93), and platelet count (116). Paracentesis labs showed abnormalities in total nucleated cells (1187), RBC (350), polymorphonuclear leukocytes (47), lymphocytes (8), mesothelial cells (2), and macrophages (43). 

Imaging showed mild bilateral pulmonary vascular congestion, a cirrhotic liver with moderate ascites, poor visualization of the main portal vein, multiple air-filled loops of small bowel with air-fluid levels, several of which may be minimally dilated, and aggressive osteolysis of the acromion with a suspected nondisplaced pathologic fracture. 

The patient's medications upon admission included albuterol, amitriptyline, atorvastatin, dolutegravir, emtricitabine-tenofovir alafenamide, furosemide, lactulose, magnesium oxide, oxycodone, rifaximin, spironolactone, vitamin D, oxycodone SR, bisacodyl, and sorafenib.

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Hepatitis C virus (HCV) cirrhosis, HIV co-infection, portal hypertension, and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) with metastatic disease to the left shoulder, was admitted due to body aches. The patient has a history of penetrating thoracic aortic aneurysm of the descending aorta status post TAVR, cirrhosis, hypertension, PTSD, cervical disc herniations, gastritis with bleeding, lipoma, and HIV on ART. The patient's family history includes multiple malignancies, but the specific types are unknown.

Upon admission, the patient was found to have icterus and a diffusely tender abdomen. The patient's body aches were sudden in onset, with no associated respiratory symptoms or fevers. The patient also reported intermittent sharp pains and denied any swelling in his extremities. The patient's pain was not adequately controlled by his home medications, oral oxycodone, and OxyContin.

Abnormal lab results upon admission included hazy urine with moderate blood, trace protein, small bilirubin, moderate leukocytes, 10 RBCs, 11 WBCs, and few bacteria. The patient's lactate level was 2.4. Other abnormal findings included glucose of 124, sodium of 130, potassium of 5.2, total CO2 of 20, AST of 99, alkaline phosphatase of 173, total bilirubin of 8.6, direct bilirubin of 3.0, albumin of 3.2, calcium of 7.9, phosphate of 2.0, RBC of 3.68, hemoglobin of 13.2, hematocrit of 37.7, MCV of 102, MCH of 35.9, RDW of 17.7, neutrophils of 74.2, lymphocytes of 16.6, and platelet count of 97. The patient's PTT was 50.8.

Imaging studies revealed a cirrhotic liver with an unchanged ablation cavity in segment VII, a distended gallbladder without wall thickening or gallstones, and poorly visualized portal veins. There was no evidence of bowel ischemia or diverticulitis, but there was moderate extrahepatic biliary and distal main pancreatic ductal dilatation. The renal ultrasound was unremarkable.

During the hospital stay, the patient underwent ERCP with common bile duct stent placement. The patient's abdominal pain was controlled with liquid Dilaudid. The patient's myalgias improved, and his sorafenib medication was held. The patient was treated for a urinary tract infection with 5 days of CTX, despite a negative urine culture. The patient's liver function did not return to his prior baseline, and he was transitioned to home hospice care.

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were metastatic hepatocellular carcinoma, urinary tract infection, cirrhosis, and diffuse myalgia. His secondary diagnoses were HIV, Hepatitis C, and hypertension. The patient's discharge medications included Benzonatate, HYDROmorphone, Metoclopramide, OxyCODONE SR, Rifaximin, Albuterol Inhaler, Bisacodyl, Ciprofloxacin HCl, Dolutegravir, Emtricitabine-Tenofovir alafen, Furosemide, Lactulose, Magnesium Oxide, Spironolactone, and Vitamin D. The patient was discharged to home with hospice services.
label: training_controls

note_summary:

note_summary:
The male patient presented to his primary care physician with severe headache and neck stiffness, accompanied by a low-grade fever. He experienced a syncopal episode while driving, resulting in a minor car accident. He reported having a headache for about 5 days, which had been worsening over the week, along with neck stiffness. He denied experiencing numbness, weakness, or tingling. He has a history of strabismus since childhood. His past medical history includes anemia, for which he has been receiving iron infusions and is now on oral iron, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, and multiple surgeries as an infant. His family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms.

Upon physical examination, he was found to have clubbing of fingertips. His mental status was normal, and he was oriented to person, place, and date. His speech was slow and deliberate. His cranial nerves were intact, and he had normal bulk and tone bilaterally. His strength was full power throughout, and sensation was intact to light touch bilaterally. His coordination was normal on finger-nose-finger.

A series of tests were conducted. A CTA of the head revealed a 3.1 x 2.4 mm saccular aneurysm projecting superolaterally from the distal M2 segment of the left middle cerebral artery. A CT head without contrast showed stable subarachnoid hemorrhage involving the left suprasellar cistern, extending into the left sylvian fissure and frontal temporal sulci. A diagnostic cerebral angiogram was performed, but the final report is pending. A chest X-ray showed severe pulmonary edema, mild cardiomegaly, and a small right pleural effusion. An echocardiogram showed thickened anterior mitral leaflet with eccentric jet of moderate mitral regurgitation, pulmonary artery hypertension, and left ventricular cavity dilation with preserved regional and excellent global systolic function. A CTA of the chest showed no definite pulmonary embolus, central predominant ground-glass opacities and septal line thickening compatible with pulmonary edema, small simple density bilateral pleural effusions, and ectatic pulmonary arteries. An EEG showed generalized theta frequency slowing of the background, suggestive of a moderate to severe encephalopathy.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a coiling of a left MCA and remained intubated post angiogram for hypoxia. He was started on daily aspirin. He experienced an acute mental status change, and a CTA showed a question of diffuse vasospasm. His systolic parameters were increased, and he underwent angiography which showed some mild vasospasms in the basilar artery. He was started on home dose of Prednisone 5mg PO daily. A transesophageal echocardiogram showed mitral regurgitation, and aortic insufficiency. He remained intubated for respiratory issues. He was extubated and did well off of the ventilator. His neuro exam was stable. He was transferred to the step down unit and was evaluated by rehab. He was discharged in stable condition.

His medications upon admission were HCTZ 25mg, Prednisone, and Iron. His discharge diagnoses included subarachnoid hemorrhage, L MCA aneurysm, pulmonary edema, cerebral vasospasm, ventilator-associated pneumonia, respiratory failure, dysphagia, Vtach, and expressive aphasia.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of subarachnoid hemorrhage (post coiling of aneurysm), autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA), possible congenital cardiac deformity, and a mitral valve mass, presented with dyspnea. He was referred from his cardiologist's office with acute heart failure. He reported a decrease in exercise tolerance, orthopnea at night, and no chest pain or fever. He had a previous admission for coiling of a left MCA aneurysm and a CTA for hypoxemia, which showed mitral valve thickening suggestive of endocarditis. The patient's family history includes several members with cerebral aneurysms, but no history of connective tissue disease. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were recorded and labs showed significant results for BNP 838, ALT 44, AST 47, and Hg 12.7. Imaging revealed moderate pulmonary edema. The patient was given 20mg IV furosemide. His past medical history includes biclonal gammopathy, AIHA, subarachnoid hemorrhage, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, hypertension, and a congenital deformity of the foot. 

Physical examination revealed a loud pansystolic murmur heard best over the mitral area, crackles throughout bilateral lung fields, and 2+ pitting edema bilaterally. Lab results showed abnormal levels of glucose, urea nitrogen, ALT, AST, CK, ALK PHOS, lipase, proBNP, albumin, and neutrophils. Imaging showed chronic mild to moderate cardiomegaly, recurrent, moderately severe pulmonary edema, and large cervical ribs. 

The patient was found to be in acute diastolic heart failure and was placed on a lasix drip with good response and improvement in his respiratory symptoms. Repeat TTE redemonstrated the mitral valve mass, showed moderate to severe mitral regurgitation, and mild 1+ aortic regurgitation, EF of 65%. He was subsequently placed on oral furosemide. 

Upon discharge, the patient's weight was 78.2. His home HCTZ was held and transitioned to lisinopril 10 mg daily. His discharge medications included aspirin, ferrous sulfate, prednisone, lisinopril, and furosemide. His primary discharge diagnosis was acute decompensated diastolic heart failure and mitral regurgitation.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for a left MCA aneurysm. He underwent a surgical procedure for the clipping of the aneurysm, which he tolerated well. His past medical history includes biclonal gammopathy, AIHA (DAT+), subarachnoid hemorrhage, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, a possible cardiac abnormality in infancy, vitamin D deficiency, hypertension, and a congenital deformity of the foot. His family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms, but no known history of connective tissue disease.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including furosemide, lisinopril, prednisone, aspirin, and ferrous sulfate. His physical examination upon discharge showed he was alert and oriented, with no pronator drift and normal strength. His gaze was disconjugate, which was consistent with his baseline. The incision from the surgical procedure was clean, dry, and intact.

MRI results showed a recanalized distal left M4 branch aneurysm in the left parietal region and a nonenhancing circumscribed polypoid lesion in the right posterior nasal cavity and right anterior nasopharynx. During his hospital stay, the patient's systolic blood pressure was kept within a range of 120-160. He was neurologically intact, his pain was well controlled, and he was ambulating independently by the second day post-operation. He was discharged home in stable condition.

Upon discharge, the patient was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. His discharge diagnosis was a left MCA aneurysm. The patient was given instructions for post-operative care and potential symptoms to watch for, including neurological issues, swelling or redness of the incision, medication problems, fever, and headaches. He was also given advice on activity levels, incision care, and potential physical and emotional experiences post-operation.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the Neurosurgery service after experiencing confusion and speech difficulties. He had recently undergone an elective left MCA aneurysm procedure and was discharged neurologically intact, aside from a baseline disconjugate gaze. His sister reported that he was unable to answer questions, frequently said "ummm" as if searching for words, and would not stay on topic. A CT/CTA of the head showed a left temporal-parietal intraparenchymal hemorrhage. The patient denied any falls, loss of consciousness, or numbness/tingling in arms or legs.

His past medical history includes biclonal gammopathy, AIHA (DAT+), subarachnoid hemorrhage s/p coiling complicated by vasospasm, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, a possible cardiac abnormality in infancy, vitamin D deficiency, hypertension, and a congenital deformity of the foot. His family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms.

Upon admission, his physical exam showed disconjugate gaze, right lateral nystagmus, and dysarthric speech with word-finding difficulties. His motor and sensory functions were intact. A CT/CTA head scan showed a clipped and coiled left parietal M4 segment middle cerebral artery aneurysm with expected postsurgical changes, a small parenchymal hemorrhage inferior and medial to the aneurysm, and patent intracranial and neck vasculature without additional aneurysm, occlusion, or stenosis. A CXR showed marked cardiomegaly with mild pulmonary vascular congestion. An MRI of the brain showed no evidence of acute infarct, expected postsurgical changes, and hemorrhage/residual postoperative blood products inferior to the surgical site.

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced multiple episodes of self-limiting word-finding difficulties. He underwent a CTA which demonstrated post-operative changes from his left craniotomy for aneurysm clipping. He was admitted to the ICU for close neurological monitoring. Pressors were initiated for blood pressure augmentation due to concern for new stroke. An MRI failed to show evidence of new infarct. His symptoms are likely due to post-operative cerebral edema, and he was started on high-dose Dexamethasone. He was subfebrile in ED (101.2), and blood and urine cultures were sent. Urine was positive for Enterococcus, and he was treated with Ciprofloxacin. Blood cultures showed no growth as of discharge. He remained clinically stable throughout hospitalization.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Tylenol, Bisacodyl, Furosemide, Lisinopril, Oxycodone, and Prednisone. His discharge diagnoses were cerebral edema and expressive aphasia. At the time of discharge, he was neurologically intact.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to weight gain and atrial fibrillation. He has a history of moderate-to-severe mitral regurgitation, moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation, pulmonary hypertension, and recent brain coiling. He also has a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, clipping of his left middle cerebral artery aneurysm, multiple myeloma, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, biclonal gammopathy, subarachnoid hemorrhage, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, and congenital deformity of foot. He has been seen at a cardiovascular genetics clinic due to physical features including low-set ears and aneurysm, along with valvular dysfunction. He was thought to possibly have Ehlers-Danlos, but did not undergo further workup. 

During his stay, he underwent Afib cardioversion, right heart catheterization, and left heart catheterization. His family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms. His admission medications included Lisinopril, Furosemide, Prednisone, Levetiracetam, and Aspirin. 

Upon admission, his physical exam showed irregular heart rhythm, bibasilar crackles with mild expiratory wheezes in the lungs, and no significant abdominal edema. His discharge physical exam showed normal heart rhythm, clear lungs, and trace lower extremity edema. 

His admission labs showed elevated proBNP (2486), WBC (10.3), RDW (15.7), RDWSD (48.8), NEUTS (78.8), LYMPHS (11.3), EOS (0.2), NUC RBCS (0.2), AbsNeut (8.10), AbsLymp (1.16), AbsMono (0.91), AbsEos (0.02), UREA N (41), and CREAT (1.5). His discharge labs showed elevated RDWSD (49.1), PTT (76.1), UreaN (43), Creat (1.4), ALT (57), and AST (45). 

His echocardiogram showed moderate to severe eccentric mitral regurgitation secondary to flail anterior and mitral valve prolapse, moderate to severe aortic regurgitation, and moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation. His RUQ U/S showed multiple splenic hemangiomas. 

During his hospital stay, he was diagnosed with acute on chronic diastolic heart failure, atrial fibrillation, moderate-to-severe mitral regurgitation, and moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation. He was treated with IV lasix, cardioversion, and was started on a blood thinner (coumadin). He was also treated with ivermectin due to exposure to scabies. His discharge medications included Aspirin, Levetiracetam, Lisinopril, Prednisone, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Torsemide, and Warfarin. He was scheduled for valve surgery after discharge.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male of unspecified age, was admitted for cardiac issues. He has a history of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) with coiling of aneurysm and subsequent clipping, autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA), and known mitral regurgitation. He had previously presented with congestive heart failure (CHF) and atrial fibrillation (AFib), which were managed with cardioversion to sinus rhythm and diuresis. An echocardiogram showed severe mitral regurgitation (MR), tricuspid regurgitation (TR), and aortic insufficiency (AI) with preserved ejection fraction (EF). Cardiac catheterization revealed clean coronaries. 

The patient's past medical history also includes hypertension, dyslipidemia, clipping of his left middle cerebral artery aneurysm, multiple myeloma with bone biopsy, biclonal gammopathy, sleep apnea (managed with CPAP), left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, and a congenital foot deformity. He also had a possible cardiac abnormality in infancy, as per his brother/healthcare provider. 

During this admission, the patient underwent mitral valve replacement with a size 31 mm mechanical valve and aortic valve replacement with a 23 mm mechanical valve. Post-operatively, he was stable and alert, with controlled pain and therapeutic INR. He was discharged home in good condition with visiting nurse services. 

On admission, the patient was taking levetiracetam, lisinopril, metoprolol succinate, prednisone, torsemide, warfarin, aspirin, and ferrous sulfate. 

Lab results showed several abnormalities, including a hematocrit of 31.8, RBC count of 3.29, hemoglobin of 9.6, RDWSD of 48.0, and urea nitrogen of 27. All these values were marked with an asterisk, indicating abnormal findings. 

The patient's family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms. There is no known history of connective tissue disease. 

The patient's physical exam was largely normal, with the exception of strabismus of the left eye. 

A transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) showed normal left ventricular wall thicknesses and motion, normal right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion, a small vegetation on the aortic valve, moderate to severe aortic regurgitation, a small vegetation on the tricuspid valve, mild tricuspid regurgitation, severe mitral regurgitation, and no pericardial effusion. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses included severe mitral regurgitation, aortic insufficiency, multiple myeloma post bone biopsy, autoimmune hemolytic anemia on prednisone, biclonal gammopathy, subarachnoid hemorrhage post coiling complicated by vasospasm, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, possible early life cardiac abnormality, vitamin D deficiency, hypertension, and congenital foot deformity.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to tamponade seen on a scheduled echocardiogram. His past medical history includes hypertension, dyslipidemia, clipping of his left middle cerebral artery aneurysm, multiple myeloma, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, biclonal gammopathy, subarachnoid hemorrhage, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, and a congenital deformity of the foot. He has a family history of cerebral aneurysms in two uncles. 

Upon admission, the patient reported feeling well and denied chest pain, shortness of breath, leg swelling, abdominal pain, and lightheadedness. Initial vital signs were normal, but labs showed an INR of 5.0, WBC of 10.8, H/H of 12.1/38.4, K of 3.1, and lactate of 2.6. He was given 1L of normal saline. 

The patient underwent a pericardial drain and AVR procedures. His echocardiogram showed a large posterior effusion with tamponade physiology and 3+ aortic regurgitation. His TTE showed moderate to severe aortic regurgitation, moderate to large sized pericardial effusion, and right ventricular diastolic collapse. His TEE showed a moderate sized pericardial effusion and aortic regurgitation. 

The patient's medications upon admission included amiodarone, aspirin, atorvastatin, levetiracetam, metoprolol tartrate, prednisone, ranitidine, and torsemide. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for pericardial effusion/tamponade, acute kidney injury, atrial fibrillation, diastolic heart failure, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and subarachnoid hemorrhage. His creatinine level was elevated from baseline but resolved with IV fluids. His INR was supra-therapeutic and was reversed with FFP prior to surgery. His warfarin was managed and he was continued on his home medications. 

The patient underwent a pericardial window procedure and was transferred to the CVICU post-operatively. He was extubated, alert, and oriented, and was breathing comfortably. He was neurologically intact and hemodynamically stable. His chest tubes and pacing wires were discontinued without complication. His wound was healing and his pain was controlled with oral analgesics. He was discharged in good condition.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to a tamponade seen on an echocardiogram. He had a history of coiling and clipping, mechanical AVR/MVR, AFib, and a recent cardiac tamponade. He presented with right lower quadrant/groin pain and was found to have an INR of concern for pericardial effusion. He denied chest pain, shortness of breath, back pain, and other symptoms. His initial vital signs were stable and labs showed an INR of 5.0, K of 3.2, Mg of 2.1, P of 3, Ca of 8.4, AST of 44/ALT of 11, T bili of 1.2, Lipase of 65, Albumin of 3.0, Hgb of 9.6/Hct of 32.3, and Lactate of 1.9. He was given potassium chloride, 2 units of FFP, and Vit K 5 mg IV. 

A bedside ECHO showed a moderate to large pericardial effusion and a CT scan revealed a large pericardial effusion with left ventricular collapse, right rectus sheath hematoma, and circumferential wall thickening of the ileocecal valve and distal ileum. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, mitral and aortic regurgitation, atrial fibrillation, diastolic heart failure, SAH, biclonal gammopathy, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, sleep apnea, left eye strabismus, vitamin D deficiency, and congenital deformity of the foot. His family history includes two uncles with cerebral aneurysms but no history of connective tissue disease. 

During his hospital stay, a pericardial drain was placed and removed, draining approximately 400cc of fluid. His rectus sheath hematoma was managed with Vit K and FFP. His mechanical valves were managed with a heparin drip and warfarin. His atrial fibrillation was managed with metoprolol and he was taken off amiodarone. His diastolic heart failure was managed with a decreased dose of torsemide due to acute kidney injury and increasing bicarbonate. 

His discharge medications included aspirin, atorvastatin, docusate sodium, levetiracetam, ferrous sulfate, oxycodone, milk of magnesia, magnesium oxide, warfarin, ranitidine, prednisone, colchicine, metoprolol succinate, potassium chloride, and torsemide. His primary discharge diagnoses were rectus sheath hematoma and pericardial effusion, with secondary diagnoses of atrial fibrillation, diastolic heart failure, and aortic and mitral valve replacement.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to chest pain. His medical history includes a spontaneous pneumothorax, for which he underwent a right blebectomy and lobectomy, and a nonoperative distal radius fracture on the left side. During this visit, he was found to have a left apical pneumothorax, which was discovered through a chest X-ray in the emergency department. A pleural pigtail catheter was placed to address this issue. 

The patient's father may have had a pneumothorax at a young age. There is no family history of genetic connective tissue disorders. 

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam showed a pectus excavatum, a left-sided pigtail in place, and slight tenderness to palpation. His lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, with good air movement and no use of accessory muscles. His heart rate and rhythm were regular, with normal S1 and S2 sounds. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended, with no masses or hepatosplenomegaly. His extremities were warm, well perfused, with no clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. His neurologic exam was normal. 

Admission labs showed an elevated white blood cell count of 12.7 and a high glucose level of 118. His magnesium level was also elevated at 2.3 upon discharge. Imaging showed a small to moderate left-sided pneumothorax on admission, which was resolved after the removal of the pigtail catheter. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's pain was managed with oral pain medications. He received heparin for DVT prophylaxis. His incision was clean, dry, and intact without evidence of erythema or drainage. He remained hemodynamically stable throughout his hospital course. 

The patient was not taking any medications prior to admission. His discharge diagnosis was spontaneous pneumothorax. He was discharged in a stable condition, with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory status.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with no significant past medical history, was admitted for intravenous heparin therapy due to a carotid dissection identified on an MRI/MRA. She reported a history of bronchitis with violent coughing and fatigue, as well as a left frontal headache a month prior to admission. Following the resolution of these symptoms, she developed pulsatile tinnitus in her right ear for three days, which then shifted to her left ear. This symptom persisted and was more noticeable when she was lying in bed at night, corresponding to her heartbeat. She had no recent neck pain, trauma, heavy lifting, chiropractic manipulations, hair appointments, etc. She frequently lifts her young children but reported no recent head or neck strain. She also reported no headache, visual changes, speech changes, dysphagia, facial droop, weakness, sensory changes, incoordination, bowel or bladder changes, or recent fevers, chills, shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation.

Her past medical history includes myomectomies for fibroids, a cesarean section, a history of ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's syndrome, and a history of supraventricular tachycardia during pregnancy. Her family history includes her mother, who died with dementia and aortic sclerosis, and her father, who died of T cell leukemia and had hypertension.

Upon physical examination, her vital signs were stable and her neurological examination was normal. Her admission labs showed an abnormal MCHC value of 35.5 and an abnormal RBC count of 15 in her urine. The MRI/MRA of her head revealed an abnormal appearance of the proximal intracranial portion of the left internal carotid artery, representing a focal dissection of this vessel. There was no occlusion, definite pseudoaneurysm, or evidence of embolic infarction in its vascular distribution. The CT Angiogram of her head/neck confirmed the dissection extending from the extreme distal portion of the cervical internal carotid artery into the proximal horizontal petrous canalicular portion of the left internal carotid artery. A small mucous retention cyst was found in the left maxillary sinus and a 4mm left thyroid lobe nodule was also identified.

During her hospital stay, she was initiated on IV heparin therapy and received her first dose of coumadin on her second hospital day. A CT angiogram of her head/neck was obtained to better visualize the extent of her carotid dissection and evaluate for the possibility of a pseudoaneurysm. The patient was informed of the small left thyroid lobe nodule and it was recommended that she should have an ultrasound of her thyroid done over the next weeks. The only medication she was on upon admission was a daily multivitamin.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a left-handed woman, was admitted to the neurology service for evaluation of a 15-second episode of dizziness. She has a recent history of left internal carotid artery dissection, which was discovered after she developed a headache and pulsatile tinnitus following a violent coughing episode. She was initially treated with heparin, which was later transitioned to coumadin. Since her discharge, she has been doing well with only mild headaches and decreased intensity of the pulsatile tinnitus. On the day of evaluation, she experienced a sudden onset of profound dizziness and a sensation of her head being in a vacuum, which she associated with anxiety and a sense of impending doom. She denied experiencing vertigo, chest discomfort, or any new medications other than the anticoagulants.

Her past medical history includes dissection of the petrous portion of the left internal carotid artery, myomectomies for fibroids, a c-section, an ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's syndrome, and supraventricular tachycardia during pregnancy. Her family history is significant for her mother's dementia and aortic disease, and her father's T cell leukemia and hypertension. 

Upon admission, her physical examination was largely unremarkable. Her neurological examination was also normal, with no deficits noted. Her laboratory results showed a slightly elevated MCHC at 35.2. Her urine test showed moderate blood and 3 RBCs. Imaging studies, including CT angiography of the head and neck, chest radiographs, and MRI and MRA of the brain, showed no significant abnormalities other than the previously identified left carotid dissection. 

During her hospital stay, she had several brief episodes of tachycardia with heart rates in the 120s, but no other cardiac arrhythmias were observed. Her dizziness was possibly related to anxiety, or rarely, to disruption of blood supply to the inner ear due to the carotid dissection. Her INR was subtherapeutic, so she was restarted on a lovenox bridge. 

Upon admission, she was taking coumadin and a multivitamin. Her discharge diagnosis was dizziness, and she was discharged in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, with no neurological deficits. She was advised to continue her lovenox and have her INR checked and coumadin dose adjusted by her primary care physician. She was also recommended to undergo outpatient evaluation by a cardiologist for possible arrhythmia.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for surgery following a right tibia fracture sustained while ice skating. This was an isolated injury and the patient denied any head strike or loss of consciousness, as well as any numbness or paresthesias. The patient's past medical history includes a dissection of the petrous portion of the left internal carotid artery (attributed to coughing spells), myomectomies for fibroids, a cesarean section, an ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's syndrome, and supraventricular tachycardia during pregnancy. The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was in no acute distress, with unlabored breathing. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. The incision was clean, dry, and intact with no erythema or discharge, and minimal ecchymosis. She was in an aircast boot on her right lower extremity. 

X-ray results showed steps related to the fixation of a spiral fracture of the distal tibia, with placement of a tibial intra medullary rod with associated proximal and distal interlocking screws. Additional screws transfix a longitudinal fracture through the posterior aspect of the distal tibia, which extends to the tibiotalar articular surface. A fibular neck fracture was also noted.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent surgery for a right tibia intramedullary nail, which she tolerated well. Post-surgery, she was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. She was given antibiotics and anticoagulation per routine. Her home medications, Simvastatin 20mg daily and ASA 81mg daily, were continued throughout her hospitalization. 

Upon discharge, the patient's pain was well controlled with oral medications, incisions were clean/dry/intact, and she was wearing an aircast boot. She was non-weight bearing in the right lower extremity, and was discharged on lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. Her activity status was independent.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to progressive left hip pain. After conservative management failed, he underwent a left total hip arthroplasty. His past medical history includes paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, dyslipidemia, likely obstructive sleep apnea, and osteoarthritis. There is no significant family history. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was in no acute distress, afebrile, with stable vital signs. His pain was well-controlled. His respiratory, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, neurologic, and psychiatric systems were all normal. His lower extremity musculoskeletal examination showed that the incision was healing well with staples, with scant serosanguinous drainage. His thigh was full but soft, with no calf tenderness. Sensation, light touch, and vibration were intact distally, and his toes were warm.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (4.10, 3.81, 4.20), Hgb (13.4, 12.4, 13.4), Hct (39.1, 36.0, 39.6), MCH (32.6, 32.4), Glucose (109), and UreaN (32). 

The patient's hospital course was uncomplicated. He was admitted to the orthopaedic surgery service and underwent the aforementioned procedure. Postoperative course was remarkable for sciatic pain, which was not affected by cyclobenzaprine but responded well to diazepam, lidoderm patch, and gabapentin. His pain was initially controlled with a PCA and then transitioned to oral pain medications on postoperative day 1. He received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis starting on the morning of postoperative day 1. The surgical dressing was changed on postoperative day 2 and the surgical incision was found to be clean and intact without erythema or abnormal drainage. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Aspirin, Doxazosin, Fluticasone Propionate nasal spray, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Finasteride, and Flecainide Acetate. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of left hip osteoarthritis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. He was discharged to home with services in stable condition.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to recurrent episodes of dizziness and lightheadedness that had worsened over the last 72 hours. He reported that these episodes had been increasing in frequency over the last month, occurring without any discernible triggers and lasting for minutes at a time. He denied experiencing syncope, chest pain, edema, or orthopnea throughout this period. He had not taken his morning medications on the day of admission. His past medical history includes paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, dyslipidemia, likely obstructive sleep apnea, and osteoarthritis. His family history includes his mother having a myocardial infarction and hypertension, and his father having a stroke. There are no early unexplained deaths or sudden cardiac death in the family.

Upon admission, the patient's heart rate was 41 bpm and he was given 1 mg IV glucagon, Zofran, and 500 ml NS IVF. His heart rate improved to around 50 bpm with IV glucagon and he was admitted for further monitoring. A dual chamber permanent pacemaker was placed during his stay. His symptoms resolved following the procedure.

The patient's admission labs showed elevated glucose (105) and urea nitrogen (30), and decreased platelet count (144). His mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) was also elevated (34.8). His monocytes (14.4) and eosinophils (8.6) were also elevated. His discharge labs showed a decrease in MCH (35.3). 

Imaging studies showed increased infrahilar opacity in the right lung, which could indicate pneumonia in the right clinical setting. There was also moderate elongation of the descending aorta and moderate retrocardiac atelectasis. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of symptomatic bradycardia, sick sinus syndrome, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. His medications upon admission included aspirin, doxazosin, finasteride, flecainide acetate, and metoprolol succinate XL.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the neurosurgery department with a chief complaint of headache. He was transferred from an outside hospital where a CT scan revealed a subarachnoid hemorrhage. The patient reported experiencing an electric shock sensation traveling up his spine to his head while at work, which was followed by a progressively severe headache, nausea, and vomiting. He was evaluated by his primary care physician who ordered a head CT at the ER, which confirmed the subarachnoid hemorrhage. The patient was then transferred for neurosurgical evaluation. 

The patient's past medical history includes cardiac stenting and coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). His family history is significant for multiple cerebrovascular accidents (CVAs) in his sister, father, and mother. There is no family history of polycystic kidney disease or Ehlers Danlos syndrome. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, alert, and cooperative. His vital signs were stable and his neurological examination was normal. An angiogram was performed during his hospital stay. 

Laboratory results revealed abnormal values for red blood cell count (3.73*), hemoglobin (12.4*), hematocrit (34.8*), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH - 33.2*), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC - 35.6*), neutrophils (75.2*), and lymphocytes (17.8*). 

Imaging studies included a head CT scan which confirmed the subarachnoid hemorrhage. A subsequent head CT and CT angiography showed hyperdensity anterior to the brainstem, a small degree of hydrocephalus, no obvious aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation (AVM), and no midline shift. A cerebral angiogram showed no evidence of aneurysm or vascular malformation. A CT of the head showed interval resolution of hyperdense blood in the prepontine cistern, with no new focus of hemorrhage seen. 

During his hospital stay, the patient remained neurologically stable despite ongoing headaches. He developed a low-grade fever and blood cultures were sent, which were still pending at the time of discharge. An MRI of the C/T spine was negative for AVM but revealed spinal stenosis. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including lopressor, lipitor, aspirin, lisinopril, fish oil, and a multivitamin. 

Upon discharge, the patient was neurologically stable. His discharge diagnosis was not specified in the provided note.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a left-handed female with a past medical history of atraumatic right vertebral artery dissection. She presented with intermittent headache, and right neck and shoulder pain similar to her prior episode of vertebral artery dissection. Four days prior to admission, she woke up with posterior right back pain, with no history of heavy lifting, unusual activities, or trauma. Along with this pain, she developed an intermittent right-sided headache, mild and dull in nature, without other symptoms.
