As the days progressed, her back/shoulder pain progressed up to her right trapezius, and then up to her right neck and paraspinals. She also experienced a transient episode of right eye blurring/fuzziness while working on the computer. She presented due to increasing neck discomfort, mild nausea, and limitation in range of motion. 

Her previous episode was very similar, with onset of a mild right headache and right neck pain and stiffness with limitation in range of motion. She was admitted and neurologically non-focal, and was discharged on Coumadin, which has since been discontinued. She denies diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, tinnitus or hearing difficulty, difficulties producing or comprehending speech, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, and difficulty with gait. She also denies recent fever or chills, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain or tightness, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation or abdominal pain, recent change in bowel or bladder habits, dysuria, arthralgias or myalgias, and rash.

The patient has no family history of arterial dissection, but her maternal grandfather had multiple strokes at an old age. There is no family history of connective tissue disease. Upon admission, she was taking ibuprofen for her discomfort. Her physical exam revealed tenderness to palpation along right paraspinals, trapezius, and scapula, very limited head turn to right, and significant muscle tension in right trapezius. She was unable to touch chin to chest. Her neurologic exam was normal. 

Her lab results showed abnormal RBC count of 3.64 and Eosinophils of 0.9. Her MRI and MRA showed no evidence of hemorrhage, edema, masses or infarction, and her ventricles and sulci were normal in caliber and configuration. Her MRA brain showed that the intracranial vertebral and internal carotid arteries and their major branches appear normal without evidence of stenosis, occlusion, or aneurysm formation. Her MRA neck showed that the common, internal and external carotid arteries appear normal. Her CT and CTA showed no evidence of hemorrhage, edema, masses, mass effect, or infarction, and her ventricles and sulci were normal in caliber and configuration. There were no findings to suggest dissection. 

During her hospital stay, her neck and shoulder pain was likely muscular in origin so pain was managed with valium and tizanidine. She improved to discharge home. Her discharge diagnosis was history of atraumatic vertebral dissection and musculoskeletal neck and shoulder pain.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a white male, was admitted due to pain between his shoulder blades during exertion. A CT scan revealed a thoracic aortic aneurysm, and he was referred for further evaluation of his dilated aortic root. He has a history of hypertension and is a current smoker. He was started on Losartan. His past medical history includes hypercholesterolemia, thoracic aortic aneurysm, hypertension, and erectile dysfunction. His family history is significant for his grandfather who died from a ruptured aneurysm.

Upon admission, the patient was anxious and occasionally coughing. His blood pressure was elevated, and he had trace edema on his left extremity. His physical exam was otherwise unremarkable. He underwent a valve-sparing aortic root replacement procedure. Prebypass results showed a moderately dilated aortic root and ascending aorta, with simple atheroma in the aortic arch. No thoracic aortic dissection was seen. Postbypass results showed a graft in the ascending aorta and root with smooth contours, and the native aortic valve was in situ without regurgitation or stenosis.

Abnormal lab results included low RBC count (2.90*), low hemoglobin (9.4*), low hematocrit (26.5*), high MCH (32.5*), high MCHC (35.6*), and low potassium (3.2*). The patient was transferred to the CVICU in stable condition post-surgery. He was extubated and neurologically intact on postoperative day 1. He developed a fever, which resolved with an Imodium tablet. His white blood cell count remained normal and his sternal incision showed no signs of infection. By the time of discharge, the patient was ambulating freely, his wound was healing, and his pain was controlled with oral analgesics.

Upon admission, the patient was on losartan, toprol XL, cialis, and ambien. He was discharged home in good condition with appropriate follow-up instructions. His discharge diagnosis included aortic root aneurysm, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and erectile dysfunction.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female of unspecified age, was admitted with the chief complaint of pleuritic pain. She has a history of intermittent chest tightness for the past year, which was found to be due to a moderate-sized right pneumothorax. The patient has had five episodes of chest discomfort, usually right-sided, accompanied by a feeling of tightness/muscle spasm, with pain upon inspiration occasionally associated with wheezing and dyspnea. The discomfort is typically on the front of the right chest, though it radiates to the back at times. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions.

The patient's past medical history includes left shoulder pain, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), dysmenorrhea, female infertility, elevated prolactin level, visual changes, bilateral retinal lattice degeneration, and vitreous syneresis. Her family history includes her father having a stroke and her mother having a thyroid disorder.

Upon admission, the patient was not taking any preadmission medications. During her hospital stay, she underwent a right pleural chest tube placement and VATS right upper lobe and right lower lobe superior segment wedge resections, diaphragmatic biopsy with primary repair, and mechanical and chemical 0.5 g doxycycline pleurodesis.

Lab results showed a WBC of 7.2, RBC of 4.07, HGB of 12.1, HCT of 36.3, MCV of 89, MCH of 29.7, MCHC of 33.3, RDW of 13.1, RDWSD of 42.6, PLT COUNT of 334, PTT of 29.3, GLUCOSE of 88, UREA N of 14, CREAT of 0.8, SODIUM of 139, POTASSIUM of 5.3, CHLORIDE of 105, TOTAL CO2 of 23, and ANION GAP of 11. A CTA chest showed a moderate right pneumothorax with moderate right middle lobe atelectasis, with no underlying mechanism identified. There was no CT evidence for tension pneumothorax.

The patient was evaluated by the Interventional Pulmonary service and a catamenial pneumothorax was suspected given clinical history and recent IVF treatment. The Thoracic Surgery service was consulted for surgical repair and biopsy given the possible endometrial implants in the pleural space. After an uncomplicated recovery, she was discharged home and will follow up in the Thoracic Clinic in 2 weeks. Her discharge diagnosis was spontaneous right pneumothorax.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to shortness of breath. She has a history of left shoulder pain, irritable bowel syndrome, dysmenorrhea, female infertility, elevated prolactin level, visual changes, bilateral retinal lattice degeneration, and vitreous syneresis. Her family history includes her father having a stroke and her mother having a thyroid disorder. Upon admission, she was taking Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg PO TID as needed for spasms.

The patient had previously undergone VATS RUL and RLL wedge and mechanical and chemical pleurodesis with 500mg doxycycline for spontaneous pneumothorax, and biopsy of some pigmented diaphragmatic lesions. She presented with recurrent right basilar PTX. She developed right-sided lower pleuritic type pain, worse on inspiration, and noted some shortness of breath. Pathology from the VATS had demonstrated fibroelastotic scarring and fibrin/hemorrhage suggestive of prior pneumothorax, with the diaphragmatic lesions consistent with hemosiderin deposition. There was some concern of pulmonary endometriosis given the temporal association with the patient's menses, however, this was not demonstrated on biopsy.

During her hospital stay, she underwent CT guided placement of right pleural pigtail catheter, intrapleural Doxycycline pleurodesis, and R VATS pleurodesis, blebectomy, diaphragmatic repair. Post-procedure, her pain was controlled with an epidural catheter and dPCA. Her diet was advanced to regular with no issues. Her PCA was discontinued on POD2 and her chest tubes were removed later that day. Repeat chest x-ray after chest tube removal revealed only a trace pneumothorax. Her epidural catheter was subsequently discontinued and she was transitioned to oral pain medications.

Her admission labs showed MCHC at 31.6* and Phos at 4.7*. Discharge labs showed RBC at 3.71*, Hgb at 11.0*, MCHC at 31.9*, Glucose at 105*, and AnGap at 9*. Radiographs showed a significant interval reduction in the right pneumothorax, now small, following insertion right basal pigtail pleural drainage catheter. However, repeat CXR revealed worsening of the pneumothorax which now had a fluid component. After chest tube removal, only a trace pneumothorax was revealed.

She was discharged with a diagnosis of recurrent right pneumothorax, in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a right-handed female who presented for further evaluation of recurrent nocturnal events. These episodes began a year ago, following a systemic, viral-like illness with low-grade fevers and malaise. The patient described waking from sleep with a sensation of muscle tension and "discharging excess energy from her fingers." These episodes typically last 3 seconds and occur multiple times per night. The patient denies experiencing any auras, loss of consciousness, tongue biting, urinary incontinence, or postictal symptoms such as confusion with these episodes. She has not had a generalized convulsion. The longest episode-free interval was a 6-week period.

The patient has a past medical history of scoliosis, hyperlipidemia, mild aortic dilation, and sinus problems post-surgical repair of "impaction". She has undergone an MRI of her spine, which demonstrated chronic spinal disease and scoliosis. An EEG, PSH, and brain MRI were reportedly normal. A sleep study was also conducted but was not diagnostic. The patient's family history includes a son with a seizure disorder, but no other history of neurologic disease in the family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in no acute distress, lying in bed comfortably. No obvious dysmorphology was noted. Neurological examination was largely normal, with the patient being awake, alert, and oriented. No deficits were noted in cranial nerves, motor function, sensory function, reflexes, coordination, or gait.

Lab results showed a slightly low RBC count at 4.04. All other lab results, including WBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, PLT COUNT, GLUCOSE, UREA N, CREAT, SODIUM, POTASSIUM, CHLORIDE, TOTAL CO2, ANION GAP, CALCIUM, PHOSPHATE, MAGNESIUM, ALT(SGPT), AST(SGOT), and ALK PHOS, were within normal ranges.

The patient was admitted to the epilepsy service for EEG LTM. Her interictal EEG demonstrated intermittent left greater than right temporal/parietal theta slowing, as well as frequent left temporal/parietal sharp transients, likely epileptiform in nature. During an event, she had multiple myoclonic jerks, primarily of the right shoulder/arm although at times involving the entire body, and at times associated with dystonic posturing of the right greater than left arms. The EEG during this event just showed an arousal pattern. However, due to the stereotyped nature of the events and the abnormal interictal EEG, it was felt that these events are likely ictal in origin.

Upon admission, the patient was on Lipitor 80 mg QHS and Metoprolol 6.25 mg BID. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy, severe anxiety, depression, scoliosis, hyperlipidemia, mild aortic dilation, sinus problems post surgical repair of "impaction", episcleritis, and nephrolithiasis, was referred to the emergency department due to worsening nocturnal events, increasing anxiety, and severe sleep difficulties over the past two weeks. Her epilepsy symptoms began several years ago and were characterized by recurrent nocturnal events involving arousal from sleep, a poorly characterized energy sensation, core tightening, and bilateral tonic extension of the arms and legs lasting several seconds, as well as daytime right shoulder myoclonic jerks. An EEG demonstrated left temporal/parietal slowing and frequent interictal epileptiform discharges in the same region. An MRI showed some nonspecific subcortical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, but no concerning lesions. She has undergone trials of carbamazepine and clonazepam, with no clear improvement in her symptoms, and is now off both. 

In the past, she had worsening and severe anxiety that led to inpatient psychiatric admission where she was started on Gabapentin (now at 200mg BID) and Nortriptyline 25mg BID. However, her anxiety worsened again recently. In terms of seizure control, her event frequency had decreased significantly, but she developed a new event that has been occurring nightly: sudden awakening from sleep, followed by irresistible urge to move/rock her legs back and forth. This is associated with intense sensation of fear and dread. 

The patient's family history includes a son with developmental disabilities and a seizure disorder, and a maternal cousin with nocturnal events beginning with leg twitches. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Gabapentin 200mg TID, Fluvoxamine 200mg daily, Nortriptyline 25mg BID (tapering down), and Atorvastatin 80mg daily. Her physical exam was largely unremarkable. Lab results showed abnormal RBC count of 3.95, MCH of 32.1, and Eosinophils of 5.5. 

During her hospital stay, the patient had several episodes of waking from sleep usually in the morning hours, with feeling anxious and variably being impelled to move her feet. There were no EEG changes associated with these episodes. After discussion with her outpatient neurologist, Lamotrigine 12.5mg po BID was started, without any rashes noted. If her events are precipitated by anxiety, the low dose will likely be helpful, but if the episodes continue, the dose may be increased to anticonvulsant dosing as an outpatient. Gabapentin was continued, and may be tapered as outpatient.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, presented with persistent and worsening neck pain, which began upon waking up. He also reported a change in tongue sensation, specifically an inability to feel cold when drinking from a water cooler. He was unsure if this sensation was localized to one side of the tongue or involved the entire tongue, and whether this sensation persisted to the present day. The patient had a history of a remote gymnastic-related neck injury, which occurred more than a few years ago, and did not result in any neurological deficits. He also reported a recent hiking incident where he slipped slightly, but denied any significant fall or whiplash event. 

The patient's past surgical history includes hydrocele and varicocele repair, wisdom teeth removal, tonsillectomy, and hernia repair as an infant. He has no known allergies except for iodinated contrast media - IV dye. His family history is positive for diabetes and hypertension, but negative for any connective tissue disease or genetic diseases involving stretchy skin or hypermobile joints.

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, cooperative, and showed no signs of distress. His vital signs were stable and within normal limits. His neurological examination was largely unremarkable, with normal mental status, cranial nerve function, motor function, sensory function, deep tendon reflexes, coordination, and gait. 

Laboratory results showed a serum and urine drug screen negative for any substances. His hemoglobin was 12.2, PTT 28.5, and INR 1.1. Radiologic data from an MRI/MRA of the head and neck showed no acute intracranial abnormality or acute infarct, and a normal MRA of the brain. However, there was a short segment of high signal surrounding the V3 segment of the left vertebral artery, suggestive of a focal dissection.

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with a left vertebral artery dissection, likely due to a traumatic event. No brainstem or posterior circulation ischemia was seen. His tongue symptoms resolved and he was placed on a heparin drip in the emergency department, which was later transitioned to aspirin 81mg monotherapy. The patient was discharged in a stable condition with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory status.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a history of depression since her early years. She has been on several medications, none of which have been effective. She has been under the care of various psychiatrists over the years. A few months ago, she was recommended Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT) due to her refractory depression. However, her parents were hesitant until she had more medication trials. The patient has been feeling more depressed over the last few years, and more acutely over the last month. She was put on Parnate, but did not respond well. She has been unable to work for the past month and has been feeling more sensitive and irritable. She has a supportive family and denies any suicidal ideation.

The patient has a past medical history of hypothyroidism and was evaluated for temporal lobe epilepsy as a child, but no strong conclusion was made that her psychiatric problems were neurologically based. She has a history of substance abuse, including alcohol and tobacco, but denies drug use. She has a family history of severe depression and alcoholism in her paternal grandfather, a paternal uncle who led an isolated life, and ADHD in her mother and brother. Her father has mild depression.

Upon physical examination, her temperature was 96.4, blood pressure was 121/76, heart rate was 125, and respiratory rate was 17. Her mental status exam showed a depressed mood, sad affect, and limited/fair insight and judgment. She was alert and oriented, and her cognitive assessment was within normal limits.

The patient was admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit. Her outpatient psychiatrist was contacted, and in coordination with his interests, a trial of Remeron was started, and Parnate was tapered and eventually discontinued. After being cleared by medicine, ECT was started. She received 2 treatments during this stay and will continue these treatments as an outpatient after discharge. On admission, she reported multiple crying spells on a daily basis in conjunction with a depressed mood and declining ability to function. After starting ECT, she reported improvement in her mood and her crying spells abated. She exhibited no cognitive impairments during her hospital course. She attended groups regularly, and had no behavioral issues.

Upon admission, she was on Lorazepam 1mg twice a day and Tranylcypromine (Parnate) 10mg twice a day. She was prescribed three times a day but couldn't tolerate it. Her discharge diagnosis included Major Depression and hypothyroidism. Her condition was stable with improvement in mood since admission.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to leakage of fluid. She reported mild cramping but no painful contractions. She had no fever, chills, dysuria, or abdominal trauma. She underwent a normal vaginal delivery of twins. Her postpartum course was uneventful.

Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism and a workup for temporal lobe epilepsy during her childhood, which did not conclusively link her psychiatric problems to neurological causes. She has been tested for two syndromes in the past, both of which were negative. 

Her family history includes a paternal grandfather with severe depression and alcoholism, a paternal uncle who led an isolated life, a mother and brother with ADHD, and a father with mild depression.

Upon physical examination, her vital signs were recorded as 98.5, 105, 20, 133/85. Her abdomen was soft and gravid, with no tenderness. Fetal heart tones for twin A were 170 with moderate variability and positive accelerations, and intermittent variables. For twin B, the heart tones were 160 with moderate variability and positive accelerations, with no decelerations. The TOCO was recorded every few minutes. The sterile speculum exam showed grossly ruptured membranes, pooling of clear fluid, and a positive nitrazine test.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC at 3.41, Hgb at 11.0, Hct at 31.9 and 29.0, and MCH at 32.3. Her WBC count was 7.7.

Upon admission, she was on Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO DAILY and Venlafaxine XR 450 mg PO DAILY. 

Upon discharge, her condition was stable. She was instructed to call or return if she experienced fever, excessive bleeding, pain, or depressive symptoms. She was also advised not to insert anything into her vagina for 4 weeks.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, was admitted for prenatal care at 33 weeks and 6 days gestation due to elevated blood pressure readings during a clinic visit. She reported significant fatigue but denied any loss of fluid, vaginal bleeding, contractions, headache, visual changes, shortness of breath, chest pain, epigastric pain, and abdominal pain. She was overall asymptomatic except for the elevated blood pressure. She was also experiencing constipation and was advised to take Colace 100 mg twice daily, Miralax as needed, prune juice, increase dietary fiber intake, and follow a bisacodyl suppository regimen. 

Her past medical history includes a prior preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM) and preterm delivery of twins at 34-35 weeks, depression and anxiety for which she is on multiple medications and has a history of electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), temporal lobe epilepsy managed with lamictal, hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine, and a question of connective tissue disorder/mitral valve prolapse. She also has a history of heavy menses, abnormal pap smear results (LGSIL, CIN1 on colposcopy) with normal results since, and denies history of HIV, syphilis, gonorrhea, chlamydia, or HSV. She denies fibroids, cysts, and heavy bleeding. Her initial blood pressure readings were elevated (134/92, 139/88) with no history of chronic hypertension. She has a long history of depression/anxiety and has been on medications since an undisclosed age. She has a history of temporal lobe epilepsy with a diagnosis of complex seizures at an undisclosed age. She also has Raynaud's and hypothyroidism. She had a prior evaluation for a connective tissue disorder with a negative work-up. 

Her family history includes depression, alcoholism, ADHD, hypertension (both parents), and a connective tissue disorder (father, sister). She denies any family history of aneurysms, uterine or organ rupture. 

Upon physical examination, her vitals were within normal range and her abdomen was soft and non-tender with no edema in the extremities. Lab results showed abnormal RBC count at 3.66* and 3.47*, Hgb at 10.8*, and Hct at 32.3*. 

During her hospital stay, she was evaluated for preeclampsia due to elevated blood pressures. Her pregnancy-induced hypertension labs, urine protein:creatinine ratio, and 24-hour urine protein were within normal limits. She remained asymptomatic, without severe range blood pressures, headache, visual changes, chest pain, shortness of breath, or right upper quadrant pain. She was diagnosed with gestational hypertension and was discharged home with precautions and close follow-up. 

Her medications upon admission included Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg, Venlafaxine XR 450 mg, LamoTRIgine 400 mg, BuPROPion 100 mg, Prenatal Vitamins, FoLIC Acid 4 mg, Aspirin 81 mg, and Acetaminophen 1000 mg. She also had PRN medications including Docusate Sodium 100 mg, Acetaminophen 650 mg, Calcium Carbonate 500 mg, Milk of Magnesia, and Bisacodyl. She has no known allergies. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was chronic hypertension. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, was admitted to the Obstetrics/Gynecology service due to elevated blood pressure readings in the clinic. She was asymptomatic at the time of her prenatal visit, denying any loss of fluid, vaginal bleeding, contractions, fetal movement, headaches, visual changes, shortness of breath, chest pain, epigastric pain, or abdominal pain. However, she reported elevated home blood pressure readings, with diastolic blood pressure in the range of 100s-110. She has a known diagnosis of gestational hypertension.

Her past medical history includes a prior preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM) and preterm delivery of twins, depression and anxiety managed with multiple medications and a history of electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), temporal lobe epilepsy managed with lamotrigine, hypothyroidism, and a question of connective tissue disorder/mitral valve prolapse. She also has a history of heavy menses and an abnormal pap smear (LGSIL, CIN1 on colposcopy), but has had normal pap smears since. She denies any history of HIV, syphilis, gonorrhea, chlamydia, HSV, fibroids, cysts, or heavy bleeding. She has a long history of depression/anxiety, for which she has been on medications since an undisclosed age. She has also been diagnosed with Raynaud's and hypothyroidism, for which she is on levothyroxine. She has previously been evaluated for a connective tissue disorder, with negative work-up results.

Her family history includes depression, alcoholism, ADHD, hypertension (both parents), and a connective tissue disorder (father, sister). She denies any family history of aneurysms, uterine or organ rupture.

Upon admission, her blood pressure readings were elevated, and her BMI was 23.7. Her physical examination was largely unremarkable, with no edema noted bilaterally. Her blood work showed abnormal RBC count of 3.84* and 3.69*, Hgb of 11.0*, Hct of 33.8*, and PTT of 23.0*. Her magnesium level was also elevated at 5.2*.

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with preeclampsia with severe blood pressure readings. She developed severe range blood pressures on hospital day 2, requiring short-acting antihypertensive medications. She agreed to induction of labor, and her 24-hour urine protein was >300 mg. Serum labs showed no evidence of end-organ damage. She had a spontaneous vaginal delivery of a liveborn female infant. She received IV magnesium throughout her intrapartum course, continuing for 24 hours postpartum. She started labetalol 200 mg every 8 hours for blood pressure control.

Her medications upon admission included acetaminophen, bupropion (sustained release), lamotrigine, levothyroxine sodium, and venlafaxine XR. She continued on venlafaxine, bupropion, and lamictal for depression, and levothyroxine for hypothyroidism during her hospital stay.

She was discharged with a diagnosis of preeclampsia with severe features at 35 weeks gestation. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was able to ambulate independently.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of bipolar disorder and hepatitis C. He was admitted with symptoms of meningismus and was evaluated for possible intracranial hemorrhage. He presented with a fever of 103, photophobia, fatigue, and a diffuse headache. His blood cultures grew MSSA, and his antibiotics were switched to Oxacillin. He underwent a thorough endocarditis work-up, which was negative. A CT scan showed inflammation of the R lower quadrant mesentary and trace free fluid without free air, a 1.8 cm indeterminant lesion in the spleen, and possible parietal hyperdensities. The patient complained of severe headache and was emergently intubated and transferred for further work-up. A subsequent CT scan showed a left subdural hematoma and two areas of intracranial hemorrhage in the occipital lobe.

The patient's past medical history includes bipolar disorder, hepatitis C, and a possible coarctation of the aorta. His family history is unknown. Upon admission, he was on Lithium 30 mg PO BID and Paxil 30 mg PO daily. 

Abnormal lab results include WBC-11.3*, RBC-4.59*, Hgb-13.2*, Hct-38.4*, Neuts-89*, Lymphs-4*, ALT-142*, AST-89*, LD(LDH)-398*, CK(CPK)-404*, cTropnT-0.06*, Triglyc-212*, K-2.8*, ESR-58*, and CRP-103.3*. 

Imaging studies showed bilateral frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage, left subdural hemorrhage with 1.1 cm of midline shift, and effacement of the basal cisterns. There was no definite tonsillar herniation. The aortic arch was partially visualized, and was tortuous, buckled, and located high in the mediastinum, with a possible coarctation or aortic pseudo-coarctation. There was significant midline shifting towards the right, approximately 9 mm of midline shifting deviation, causing narrowing of the perimesencephalic cisterns. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was found to have MSSA bacteremia with positive blood cultures. He was intubated for protection of airway, and hypotension required aggressive fluid resuscitation via CVL. He was initially started on dilantin for seizure prophylaxis and then transitioned to keppra 750mg BID. He was also found to have an aortic aneurysm of 4.7 cm by CT scan/ TEE. He was evaluated by cardiothoracic surgery who recommended semiurgent repair as an outpatient. He was also found to have 3+ aortic regurgitation on TEE. He was noted to have an erythematous, nonpainful blanching rash with superimposed vesicles on dependent areas of body, which was thought to represent milliaria crystallina. His mental status changes were thought to be secondary to combined DI picture from long term lithium as well as SIADH from acute intracranial process.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted with a chief complaint of pneumothorax. He has a history of mild asthma and panic attacks. He presented with right-sided chest pain that occurred while walking, which was pleuritic in nature and associated with shortness of breath. The pain resolved spontaneously after a few hours but recurred, becoming worse when bending down to tie his shoes. The patient had a chest x-ray at his primary care physician's office which showed a small apical pneumothorax. He has not taken any pain medication and has no history of pneumothorax in the past. He last smoked marijuana two days prior to admission and there was no trauma reported. 

The patient's family history includes his grandmother's history of coronary artery disease and his grandfather's history of stroke. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with normal vital signs and no signs of distress. 

Lab results showed an abnormal MCHC value of 35.1. All other lab results, including WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDW, Plt, Glucose, UreaN, Creat, Na, K, Cl, HCO3, and AnGap were within normal ranges. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's pneumothorax remained stable and he was not in respiratory distress, so no chest tube or pigtail was inserted. His pain was controlled with oral medication. He was deemed appropriate for discharge with instructions to return to the emergency room if he felt short of breath or experienced an increase in pain. 

Upon admission, the patient was not on any medications. His discharge diagnosis was spontaneous pneumothorax. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory at the time of discharge.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to chest pain. He has a medical history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), mild asthma, and a previous spontaneous pneumothorax on the right side that was treated conservatively. During this visit, he reported a 3-hour history of chest pain and a chest x-ray revealed a right-sided apical pneumothorax. He experienced pain with inspiration and some shortness of breath but denied difficulty breathing. Since his last admission, he has been evaluated by pulmonology for his dyspnea on exertion, with normal Pulmonary Function Tests (PFT's) and Cardiopulmonary Exercise Test (CPET).

Upon physical examination, decreased breath sounds were noted in the right upper chest and a soft systolic murmur was detected over the left intercostal space. The patient's temperature was 97.6, heart rate was 81, blood pressure was 139/87, respiratory rate was 18, and oxygen saturation was 100% on room air. 

The patient's chest x-rays showed an increasing right pneumothorax, now moderate in size without evidence of tension. After the placement of a right chest tube, a small residual right apical pneumothorax was observed. A subsequent x-ray showed the chest tube in place with a small apical pneumothorax, but no evidence of acute pneumonia or vascular congestion.

During his hospital stay, the patient was evaluated by the Thoracic Surgery service. Given that this was his second pneumothorax on the right side, surgery was recommended. However, the patient preferred to wait on surgery. A pleural catheter was placed in the Emergency Room after a repeat chest x-ray showed an enlarging pneumothorax. Following transfer to the Surgical floor, the patient progressed well. His chest tube was placed on suction, without any air leak, and following a waterseal trial the next day, he had a tiny right apical pneumothorax. His tube was removed and his post pull film showed a stable right apical pneumothorax. His room air saturations were 96% and his pain was minimal, controlled with Tylenol.

Upon admission, the patient was on Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q8H as needed for anxiety. There is no known family history of pulmonary disease, pneumothorax, or any genetic connective tissue disorders. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of spontaneous right pneumothorax. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to left hip osteoarthritis (OA). He underwent a left total hip arthroplasty (L THA) without complications. His past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD), for which he had a coronary artery bypass grafting (CABGx4) procedure, percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA) of the left circumflex artery (LCx) and posterior descending artery (PDA) graft, and intracoronary angiogenesis factor. He also has a history of hyperlipidemia, depression, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with severe esophagitis and gastritis, possible fibromyalgia, erectile dysfunction, right knee surgery, multiple fractures of both elbows, and a left patellar fracture. 

The patient's family history is positive for heart disease, pulmonary fibrosis, and stomach cancer. Upon admission, the patient was on Plavix 75, Nexium BID, Dilaudid, Lisinopril 20, and Metoprolol 100. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's blood work showed abnormal results, including low red blood cell (RBC) count, low hemoglobin (Hgb), low hematocrit (Hct), and high glucose levels. Specifically, his RBC was 2.96, Hgb was 9.3, Hct was 26.6, and glucose was 135. 

The patient was seen daily by physical therapy and labs were checked throughout the hospital course and repleted accordingly. At the time of discharge, the patient was tolerating a regular diet and feeling well. He was afebrile with stable vital signs. His hematocrit was stable, and his pain was adequately controlled on a PO regimen. The operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. The patient was discharged to home with services or rehabilitation in a stable condition. His weight-bearing status was 50% partial weight bearing (PWB). 

The patient was instructed to return to the emergency department or notify his doctor if he experiences severe pain not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers over 101.5, shaking chills, redness or drainage from the incision site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns. He was also advised to follow up with his primary care physician regarding this admission and any new medications and refills.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to left hip pain. His past medical history includes coronary artery disease, for which he underwent coronary artery bypass grafting four times. He also had percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty of the left circumflex artery and posterior descending artery graft, with no changes noted in subsequent catheterizations. He has a history of hyperlipidemia, which caused joint pains with Lipitor, Zocor, and Lescol. He has been diagnosed with depression and gastroesophageal reflux disease, which led to severe esophagitis and gastritis. He has also experienced erectile dysfunction. He has had right knee surgery and multiple fractures of both elbows, as well as a left patellar fracture. 

During this admission, he underwent a left total hip arthroplasty. Postoperatively, he was treated with a patient-controlled analgesia and then oral pain medications. He received intravenous antibiotics for 24 hours postoperatively and lovenox for deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis. His surgical incision was found to be clean, dry, and intact without erythema or purulent drainage. He had one episode of chest pain, which was managed with aspirin and nitroglycerin. His hematocrit was 28.6. 

His lab results showed abnormal values for red blood cell count (3.46), hemoglobin (10.6), hematocrit (30.6), and glucose (143). His family history is positive for heart disease, pulmonary fibrosis, and stomach cancer. 

Upon admission, he was taking Tylenol, Aspirin, Lisinopril 20, Metoprolol 100 tid, Nexium 40, nitroglycerin spray and patch, Plavix 75, Zantac 150, and Zocor. 

His condition was stable upon discharge, with the diagnosis of left hip osteoarthritis. His discharge instructions included pain management, wound care, activity restrictions, and follow-up appointments. He was advised to return to the emergency department or notify his doctor if he experienced severe pain not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers over 101.5, shaking chills, redness or drainage from the incision site, chest pain, shortness of breath, or any other concerns.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, impaired fasting glucose, severe coronary artery disease (with a history of myocardial infarction, coronary artery bypass grafting, and multiple percutaneous coronary interventions), and distal length-dependent axonal sensory polyneuropathy. He was transferred from an outside hospital after presenting with sudden onset visual changes, chest pain radiating to the left shoulder and jaw, and difficulty speaking, all of which have since resolved. He reported similar episodes in the past, the most recent of which occurred two weeks prior. He also reported dyspnea on exertion and fatigue, which have worsened over the past two months. 

The patient's other medical history includes peripheral vascular disease, depression, possible fibromyalgia, erectile dysfunction, right knee surgery, multiple fractures of both elbows, left patellar fracture, bilateral hip replacements, skin burns on his left leg, and a history of violent outbursts while in the hospital. He has a family history of renal failure in his mother and coronary artery disease and a seizure disorder in his father. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Plavix, Nexium, Ranitidine, Simvastatin, Carvedilol, Aspirin, Calcium with Vitamin D, a multivitamin, Nitrolingual spray, Nitro patch, and Coenzyme Q10. 

The patient's physical examination was largely unremarkable, with some decreased range of motion in the neck, increased tone in the lower extremities, and impaired stereognosis bilaterally. Neurological examination revealed decreased pinprick and temperature sensation at C3-4, impaired light touch bilaterally, and impaired vibratory sense and proprioception at the great toes bilaterally. 

Lab results showed decreased red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration. Cholesterol was elevated at 220, HbA1c was 6.6, and estimated average glucose was 143. Triglycerides were also elevated at 221. 

Imaging studies, including CT and MRI of the head, CTA of the chest, and an echocardiogram, did not reveal any acute abnormalities. An EEG was also normal. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was evaluated for possible stroke or transient ischemic attack, as well as myocardial ischemia. However, EKG and cardiac enzymes were negative for myocardial ischemia, and imaging studies did not show evidence of acute stroke or vessel abnormality. The patient's presentation was ultimately thought to be a transient ischemic attack.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease (CAD) post coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), peripheral vascular disease, depression, erectile dysfunction, and a history of multiple fractures and surgeries, was admitted due to chest pain. He has had multiple admissions for chest pain without documented acute coronary syndrome. The patient reported a gradual onset of bilateral chest tightness with some pain radiating to the left arm, non-exertional and nonpositional, occasionally associated with shortness of breath, diaphoresis, and nausea. The patient's pain was not fully relieved by sublingual nitroglycerin at home. 

Upon arrival, the patient's vital signs were stable and physical exam was unremarkable. The EKG showed normal sinus rhythm, unchanged from prior. Lab results showed a white blood cell count of 8.1, hematocrit of 37.6, creatinine of 1.0, and troponin of less than 0.01. The patient received 4mg IV morphine and sublingual nitroglycerin without resolution of pain, leading to his admission for further evaluation. 

The patient's past medical history includes a transient ischemic attack following a cardiac catheterization, multiple percutaneous coronary interventions, esophageal issues, possible fibromyalgia, and distal length-dependent severe axonal sensory polyneuropathy. The patient's family history is significant for renal failure in his mother and a strong history of CAD in his father, three brothers, and grandfather. 

Lab results showed abnormal red blood cell count of 4.18, hemoglobin of 12.9, hematocrit of 37.6, and glucose of 101. The patient underwent a Persantine stress test with nuclear imaging, which revealed atypical mild chest pain with no ischemic changes on EKG and an appropriate hemodynamic response to pharmacologic stress. The nuclear study revealed a severe fixed perfusion defect on the inferior wall, unchanged from prior study, with a calculated left ventricular ejection fraction of 50%. 

The patient's medications upon admission included carvedilol, plavix, esomeprazole, losartan, metformin, sublingual nitroglycerin, pravastatin, tramadol, ranitidine, aspirin, and a multivitamin. 

The patient's chest pain was determined to be atypical and not indicative of acute coronary syndrome. His hypertension was managed with continued losartan and carvedilol, and his diabetes was managed with metformin. His gastroesophageal reflux disease was managed with home antacids. The patient was discharged in stable condition without chest pain.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to chest pain, vision changes, and bilateral leg weakness. He reported that his symptoms began three days prior to admission, with vision changes similar to his previous transient ischemic attacks (TIAs). He also experienced left arm pain, which then traveled down to his left leg, and left-sided "crushing" chest pain which radiated into his left arm. He was concerned about the possibility of a stroke and a heart attack given his extensive cardiac history. He took a SL NTG and aspirin and presented to the fire department in his town. His symptoms resolved by the second night of his hospitalization. However, his symptoms recurred on the afternoon of admission and he was brought to the ED by ambulance.

The patient has a past medical history of coronary artery disease (CAD) with 4 coronary artery bypass grafts (CABG), myocardial infarction (MI), 15 percutaneous coronary interventions (PCIs), hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), peripheral vascular disease (PVD), depression, possible fibromyalgia, erectile dysfunction, right knee surgery, multiple fractures of both elbows, left patellar fracture, bilateral hip replacements, skin burns on his left leg, and distal length-dependent severe axonal sensory polyneuropathy. He also has a history of violent outbursts while in the hospital. 

His family history includes his father, grandfather, and two brothers who had CAD and died. His father had epilepsy and heart disease, one brother had heart disease and died, another brother had cancer and heart disease and died, and another brother had pulmonary fibrosis and possibly heart disease and died. His sister had a brain aneurysm and is now permanently disabled with left hemiplegia. His mother has renal failure and is alive.

Upon admission, his vitals were T 98.0 P 52 BP 168/98 R 18 O2 sat 98% RA. Labs were remarkable for CBC with baseline mild normocytic anemia (hgb 13.6), normal chem-10 other than mildly low phos 2.5, negative urine and serum tox screens, and troponin < 0.01 at 14:27. Head CT was negative for acute process. CXR wet read showed no acute process. Neurology was consulted since a code stroke was called. The recommended outpatient neurology follow-up and social work, as well as admission for chest pain rule-out.

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted due to dyspnea and weight gain. He has a history of paroxysmal atrial flutter and reported ventricular tachycardia (VT). His cardiac history dates back to when he was diagnosed with atrial flutter. He had an exercise tolerance test (ETT) with exercise-induced VT that was asymptomatic. He was discharged on a beta blocker, which he self-discontinued due to side effects. He had a prolonged hospitalization due to ruptured appendicitis, which was complicated by intermittent VT. He also had a knee replacement, which was again complicated by intermittent VT. He refused a recommended VT ablation. He took metoprolol for a time, but again discontinued it on his own due to side effects. He has been able to exercise regularly without symptoms until recently when he noticed dyspnea on exertion, chest tightness, a dry cough, and weight gain. He also reported abdominal distention, leg swelling, and new orthopnea. 

His past medical history includes acoustic neuroma, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis, paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, tubular adenoma of the colon, knee arthroplasty, and cervical laminectomy. His home medications include Vitamin C, Multivitamin, Turmeric, and Fish oil. His family history includes a brother with arrhythmia s/p ablation, another brother with unknown heart disease, a father with CAD/PVD, lung cancer, and COPD, and a mother with SLE, breast cancer, thyroid disorder, and CAD.

During his hospital stay, he underwent cardiac angiography and right heart catheterization. He was found to be in atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response, which responded to 5 mg of IV metoprolol. He was also given 20 mg of Lasix. An echo showed global LV hypokinesis with an EF of 15%. He was started on a nitro drip. His initial vitals in the ED were 97.6 110 96% 2L NC. His EKG showed AF with a ventricular rate of 113. His labs were notable for normal CBC, HCO3 20, otherwise normal electrolytes, and trop < 0.01. He was given ASA 324 mg, heparin gtt, and metoprolol 12.5 mg. 

His physical exam on admission showed an irregularly irregular rhythm, S3 present, a holosystolic murmur, crackles at bilateral bases, distended abdomen, and 1+ edema of bilateral extremities. His discharge exam showed a regular rhythm, S3 present, a holosystolic murmur, and no lower extremity edema. 

His lab results showed abnormal RDW-16.7*, RDWSD-53.1*, Glucose-103*, Neuts-76.6*, Lymphs-11.9*, Monos-10.3, Eos-0.8*, AbsNeut-6.60*, AbsLymp-1.03*, AbsMono-0.89*, proBNP-2479*, WBC-10.6*, RDW-16.1*, RDWSD-50.1*, UreaN-24*, ALT-61*, and TotBili-1.9*. 

Conditions and symptoms that have been ruled out include stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools, and exertional buttock or calf pain.

note_summary:

He also reported a fall eight days prior, during which he did not hit his head but experienced pain in the left lower rib cage upon deep breathing, moving, and coughing. 

Initial vitals in the emergency department (ED) showed a heart rate of 105, and an EKG revealed a rate of 104, sinus rhythm, QTc 459, and ST depression in V4. A CT scan of the chest showed an increase in the size of bilateral pleural effusions, new extensive multifocal airspace opacities in the left upper lobe, a nondisplaced fifth left rib fracture, and contrast seen only in the right heart with reflux of contrast into the inferior vena cava (IVC) and hepatic veins, likely due to poor cardiac output. 

The patient's past medical history includes acoustic neuroma, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis (OA), paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, tubular adenoma of the colon, knee arthroplasty, and cervical laminectomy. His home medications include vitamin C, multivitamin, turmeric, and fish oil. His family history includes a brother with arrhythmia status post ablation, another brother with unknown heart disease, a father with coronary artery disease (CAD), peripheral vascular disease (PVD), lung cancer, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), breast cancer, thyroid disorder, and CAD.

During his hospital stay, the patient reported not being short of breath at rest, but experiencing shortness of breath upon moving a lot in bed or trying to walk to the bathroom. He reported left-sided rib pain at the location of his fracture that worsened with coughing, and a feeling of fluid in his chest. He also reported abdominal bloating, decreased appetite, slight swelling in his legs, and a weight gain of 5 lbs since his recent discharge. He denied current dizziness, lightheadedness, shortness of breath at rest, abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, diarrhea/constipation, and pain in extremities. 

Admission labs showed elevated neutrophils (74.4), lymphocytes (11.6), monocytes (12.8), eosinophils (0.4), absolute neutrophils (8.51), absolute lymphocytes (1.32), absolute monocytes (1.46), urea nitrogen (24), and proBNP (3540). Discharge labs showed elevated RDWSD (48.6), urea nitrogen (22), and anion gap (19). 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with atrial flutter status post direct current cardioversion, paroxysmal non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, acute on chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, and a lung lesion of unknown significance. He was discharged with instructions to weigh himself every morning, call his doctor if his weight increases by more than 3 lbs in one day or 5 lbs over two days, follow up with his primary care physician for further evaluation of the area of his lungs that concerned the medical team, and take his medications as directed. His discharge medications included lisinopril 2.5 mg PO daily, furosemide 40 mg PO daily, amiodarone 400 mg PO BID, apixaban 5 mg PO BID, ascorbic acid, fish oil (Omega 3) 1000 mg PO daily, metoprolol succinate XL 25 mg PO daily, and a multivitamin.

The patient's family history includes a brother with arrhythmia and another with an unknown heart disease. His father had coronary artery disease, peripheral vascular disease, lung cancer, and COPD, while his mother had systemic lupus erythematosus, breast cancer, and a thyroid disorder.

Upon admission, the patient was experiencing worsening shortness of breath and midsternal chest tightness. He had a history of ventricular tachycardia and was discharged with a life vest. He was also on amiodarone BID and eliquius. His initial vitals in the ED showed stable tachycardia, and he was given Lasix 40mg IV. His labs showed elevated WBC, proBNP, and potassium levels, and a chest X-ray revealed mild pulmonary edema, small pleural effusions, and upper lobe opacities suggestive of multifocal pneumonia. 

During his hospital stay, the patient reported an ongoing cough, orthopnea, leg swelling, and dyspnea. He was diuresed with Lasix and transitioned to oral torsemide. His Lisinopril was discontinued, and he was started on spironolactone. He also completed a 4-day course of IV ferric gluconate. His atrial fibrillation was managed with Apixaban and Metoprolol, and his amiodarone was discontinued due to concerns of lung disease. He also had a nondisplaced rib fracture from a previous syncopal event. 

Upon discharge, his primary diagnosis was an acute on chronic exacerbation of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, and his secondary diagnoses were atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and cough. His discharge medications included Potassium Chloride, Spironolactone, Torsemide, Apixaban, Ascorbic Acid, Fish Oil, Metoprolol Succinate XL, and Multivitamins. His Amiodarone was discontinued.

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia in the 1990s, non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, paroxysmal atrial flutter/fibrillation, and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) with an ejection fraction of 10%. He also has a history of acoustic neuroma, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis, tubular adenoma of the colon, knee arthroplasty, and cervical laminectomy. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. 

The patient was admitted due to hypotension following a scheduled cavo-tricuspid isthmus (CTI) line and pulmonary vein isolation (PVI) procedure. He had three recent hospitalizations due to acute decompensated heart failure and atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. During these hospitalizations, he underwent cardiac catheterization, cardioversion, and was started on amiodarone. His Lasix dosage was increased from 20 to 40 mg daily. He also presented with chest pain, shortness of breath, and atrial fibrillation with a heart rate of 90-110 beats per minute. He was diuresed with Lasix and transitioned to Torsemide. His amiodarone was discontinued due to concerns for lung toxicity. 

During the CTI line and PVI procedure, the patient was given 160 mg of Lasix and 35 mg of Protamine. After the procedure, he became hypotensive and was given Phenylephrine IV. He was transferred to the CCU where he reported feeling well and had no complaints. 

The patient's family history includes a brother with arrhythmia status post ablation, a father with coronary artery disease, lung cancer, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus, breast cancer, and thyroid disease. 

Upon admission, the patient's labs showed elevated neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, white blood cell count, glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, and proBNP. His albumin and PO2 levels were low. His imaging studies showed multifocal opacities scattered throughout the lungs, which may represent amiodarone toxicity or multifocal pneumonia. He also had a small left pleural effusion, resolution of the right pleural effusion, and no significant change in the cardiomegaly and central pulmonary vascular congestion. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was initially hypotensive following the PVI procedure and required pressor support. He was diuresed with IV Lasix. He also had acute on chronic HFrEF and required a drip up to 20 and metolazone 5 mg. He had some systemic features concerning for a connective tissue disorder, but vascular medicine ruled this out in the absence of aortic root dilation. He also had a cough and abnormalities on lung imaging, which were concerning for amiodarone toxicity. He was started on amiodarone with close pulmonary follow-up as an outpatient. 

Upon discharge, the patient's creatinine level was 1.0 and his weight was 96.25 kg. His discharge diagnoses included acute on chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation/flutter, pulmonary scarring, and acute kidney injury. His discharge medications included Amiodarone, Lisinopril, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Torsemide, Apixaban, Ascorbic Acid, Fish Oil, and Multivitamins. Spironolactone was held and he was advised not to restart it until talking to his cardiologist.

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a history of asthma, hormonal migraines, hypothyroidism, and a previous motor vehicle accident that resulted in a cerebral contusion and small intracranial hemorrhage. She also had a prior surgery on her right thumb, which has been numb since the procedure. She presented with sudden onset right neck pain following a violent coughing episode, which was later found to be due to a right internal carotid artery (ICA) dissection and right vertebral artery narrowing. She also experienced right pulsatile tinnitus, severe sharp constant pain in her right neck radiating into her jaw and head, and a drooping right eyelid. She denied any recent traumatic injury, neck strain, or heavy lifting. 

Upon admission, her medications included ProAir HFA (albuterol sulfate) for asthma and Levothyroxine Sodium for hypothyroidism. She was found to have a right Horner's syndrome but denied any loss of vision, blurred vision, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, or hearing difficulty. She also denied any difficulties with speech, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, and difficulty with gait. 

Her lab results showed abnormal levels of potassium (7.3), total CO2 (21), PTT (53.2, 92.3, 107.9, 51.9, 41.5), and neutrophils (73.5, 6.61). Imaging revealed a right ICA dissection with a false lumen that appeared to be filled with clot and a focal smooth segment of narrowing in the right vertebral artery without occlusion. There was no evidence of acute infarcts, mass effect, or hydrocephalus. 

Her family history includes an uncle who had a stroke at a young age and another stroke later in life. He was a smoker. There is no known family history of fibromuscular dysplasia, polycystic kidney disease, or connective tissue disease. 

During her hospital stay, she was started on warfarin 5mg/day with an INR goal and bridged with IV heparin until her INR reached a therapeutic range of 2.2. Her neck pain was treated with acetaminophen and tramadol. Her EKG showed normal sinus rhythm. Upon discharge, her condition was stable with no notable changes from admission. The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the neurology department with a chief complaint of headache. He has a history of hyperlipidemia and diabetes mellitus, with possible hypertension. He was evaluated by the Emergency Department for an intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH). The patient experienced sudden onset right-sided retroorbital headache, nausea, left-sided weakness, and left facial droop. His wife noted his speech was slurred and he seemed unsettled. His blood pressure ranged between 139 - 167 systolic. A CT head revealed a large right frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage (IPH) with subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). He was treated with fosphenytoin 1g, intubated, and started on ativan. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was obtunded with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) of 5. He was unresponsive to verbal commands, had brisk flexor on the right to noxious away from stimulus and right flex on noxious to left upper extremity. His left lower extremity had increased tone compared to the right. His deep tendon reflexes were brisk in both upper extremities symmetrically. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for white blood cell count, red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, glucose, urea nitrogen, sodium, and calcium. The patient's EKG showed normal sinus rhythm with no ST/T changes. Radiologic data from a CT scan revealed a 4.5 x 3.9 x 5.0 cm right frontal hemorrhage in MCA/ACA territory, with SAH in frontal lobes with mild mass effect on right frontal horn without intraventricular hemorrhage. A cerebral angiogram showed no aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation. 

The patient's family history includes hypertension on the mother's side, but no history of hyperlipidemia, diabetes mellitus, cerebrovascular accident/transient ischemic attack, coronary artery disease/peripheral vascular disease, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformation, or connective tissue disorders. 

During his hospital stay, the patient required intubation for airway protection but was extubated without difficulty. He was started on keppra for seizure prophylaxis, which was later stopped due to a rash. He was also started on broad-spectrum antibiotics for a possible left lower lobe infiltrate. He required phenylephrine to maintain mean arterial pressure > 70 early in the hospital course but has been normotensive since extubation. His home ace-inhibitor has been resumed. He was maintained on a regular insulin sliding scale and NPH, and his home glyburide has been resumed. He developed a rash over his left arm, anterior abdominal wall, and legs, likely due to medication. He was unable to pass a speech and swallow test and underwent percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement for feeding. 

The patient's medications upon admission included glyburide, quinapril, metformin, simvastatin, and aspirin.

The patient is a right-handed male with a past medical history of hyperlipidemia, hypertension, diabetes, and a right-sided intraparenchymal hemorrhage, likely hypertensive in origin. He has also experienced seizures since the hemorrhage, which have been treated with various medications, most recently Tegretol. He presented with two seizures and was transferred from an outside hospital for further evaluation. 

The patient was recently admitted to a rehab facility after a short stay at a hospital for generalized weakness. It was found that he was hepatotoxic, likely secondary to Tegretol. He had at least two seizure events within the past few months. His wife reported that he had no obvious signs of infection and had not had any recent seizure events. However, she witnessed him having a seizure, which lasted for approximately two minutes and ended on its own. The CTA of head and neck showed no flow-limiting stenosis or aneurysm, no evidence of AVM, right frontal encephalomalacia and left frontal subarachnoid hemorrhage, atherosclerotic disease involving the aortic arch, common and proximal cervical internal carotid arteries with less than 50% stenosis by NASCET criteria, and degenerative changes in the cervical spine. note_summary:

He has also experienced seizures since the hemorrhage, which have been treated with various medications, most recently Tegretol. However, the patient had a negative reaction to Tegretol, resulting in hepatotoxicity, and was being tapered off this medication. Over the past two months, he has had two hospital admissions for seizure control and medication changes. 

The patient's family history includes hypertension and pancreatic and breast cancer on his mother's side. His father's medical history is unknown.

The patient's physical exam revealed a left facial droop and a left pronator drift postural tremor. He was unable to walk and showed signs of impaired concentration. His vitals were stable, and no other significant abnormalities were noted. 

A CT scan showed a small SAH overlying the left frontal lobe, likely secondary to amyloid angiopathy. The patient's blood pressure was well-controlled during his admission. His lab results showed a decreased red blood cell count (3.57*), hemoglobin (10.9*), and hematocrit (32.1*). 

During his stay, the patient slipped and fell while walking with a walker to the bathroom. He did not lose consciousness and fell on his bottom. He had no pain following the fall, and no bruising was observed.

note_summary:
The patient, a female school teacher, presented with a two and a half week history of headaches and left-sided numbness/tingling. The headaches began after a visit to the dentist and were not relieved by Advil. The numbness was first noticed after playing tennis and lasted for 30 minutes. The patient's primary care doctor diagnosed her with migraines and prescribed Excedrin. A head CT scan was normal. Two days prior to admission, she experienced another episode of left hand numbness, which subsided within 10 minutes. On the day of presentation, she noticed persistent numbness over the left side of her body. Her headaches were bilateral, throbbing, sharp, worse in the morning, and improved with Vicodin. 

A brain MRI showed recent infarcts in the right internal capsule and basal ganglia. An MRA showed dissection of the right internal carotid artery. She was started on aspirin and heparin. She denied any history of head or neck trauma but mentioned starting a new workout involving weight lifting a month ago. 

Her past medical history includes anxiety. She has no family history of strokes but does have a history of heart disease in both grandfathers. 

Physical examination was largely normal, with the exception of decreased sensation to fine touch and pinprick on the left side of her body. Lab results showed abnormal MCV and MCHC values. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with right internal carotid artery dissection and distal left vertebral artery dissection, as well as right occipital, basal ganglia, and internal capsule infarcts. The dissections were thought to be related to the dental procedure or possibly as a result of playing tennis. She was started on a heparin drip on admission and then transitioned to coumadin with a lovenox bridge. She continued to have mild headaches, treated with oxycodone as needed, and tingling of the left side of her body. 

Upon admission, she was taking Citalopram 40mg daily.
label: training_controls

She had a history of severe mitral regurgitation, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with hiatal hernia, osteoporosis, anxiety, scoliosis, and infertility. She had previously undergone laparoscopy for fertility workup and wisdom teeth extraction. Her family history includes lung cancer in her father, hypertension in her mother, and coronary artery disease in her sister. Post-operative echocardiogram showed preserved bi-ventricular systolic function and minimal mitral regurgitation. Her recovery was uneventful, with chest tubes and pacing wires discontinued without complication. She was evaluated by the physical therapy service for assistance with strength and mobility.

Her lab results showed several abnormal findings including hematocrit of 25.8, white blood cell count ranging from 10.1 to 13.4, red blood cell count ranging from 2.60 to 2.99, hemoglobin ranging from 7.9 to 9.2, hematocrit ranging from 24.3 to 28.4, and glucose levels ranging from 109 to 159. 

Upon discharge, she was alert and oriented, ambulating with a steady gait. Her thoracotomy incision pain was managed with oral analgesics and the incision was healing well with no erythema or drainage. She was instructed to shower daily, avoid applying lotions or creams to the incision, weigh herself each morning, and take her temperature each evening. She was advised not to drive for approximately one month and while taking narcotics.

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to long-standing left knee pain, limited range of motion, and difficulties with daily activities. He had met the clinical and radiographic indications for joint arthroplasty and opted to proceed with the procedure. Prior to admission, the patient reported feeling well with no recent illness, shortness of breath, or chest pain. He had been medically cleared for the surgical procedure. 

His past medical history includes polyarticular arthritis, for which he was on prednisone and methotrexate, a previous left high tibial osteotomy, ulcerative proctitis, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypertension. His family history was non-contributory. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, alert and oriented. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, non-distended, with positive bowel sounds. His lower extremities were fully neurovascularly intact distally with 2+ DP pulses and full strength throughout. His left knee had painful and limited range of motion. The incision was clean, dry, and intact.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a left knee total joint arthroplasty, which was well-tolerated with no complications. Postoperatively, he was placed on a PCA for pain control, IV antibiotics for prophylaxis, and Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. His range of motion was managed with a CPM machine. On postoperative day 1, the drain was removed and the patient was transitioned to oral pain medications. He received one unit of autologous blood due to a hematocrit of 27 and lightheadedness. On postoperative day 2, the Foley catheter was removed and the surgical dressing was also removed. His hematocrit was 26.1 and he was given 1 unit of packed red blood cells. He remained hemodynamically stable at all times.

Throughout his hospital stay, the patient was seen daily by physical therapy. Labs were checked post-operatively and throughout the hospital course and repleted accordingly. The patient was tolerating a regular diet and otherwise feeling well. Prior to discharge, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs. His hematocrit was stable and pain was adequately controlled on a PO regimen. The operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. A urinalysis was checked prior to discharge and was negative.

Upon admission, the patient was on Prednisone 6 mg daily, Methotrexate 2.5 mg three times per week, Omeprazole 20 mg daily, Ambien as needed, Lialda 1.2 g daily, Folate 1 gram daily, Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, and Metoprolol 12.5 mg daily. 

The patient was discharged to rehab in a stable condition with a diagnosis of left knee arthritis.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, ulcerative colitis with polyarticular arthritis, left total knee replacement, right meniscectomy followed by a right lower leg deep vein thrombosis (DVT), and left high tibial osteotomy, was admitted due to DVT and pulmonary embolism (PE). He had experienced leg numbness/swelling and chest pain and dyspnea a day prior to admission. He had a history of knee surgeries and a previous DVT after a meniscectomy. He was given a Heparin bolus and started on a gtt due to his clot burden. A retrievable Inferior Vena Cava (IVC) filter was placed. 

The patient's family history includes his mother's death from ovarian cancer, his father's death from ALS, and his brother's death from leukemia. His sister is approximately ___ years old and has no medical problems. His grandparents had diabetes mellitus. There is no history of breast, prostate or colon cancer in the family, nor any bleeding or clotting disorders.

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert, oriented, and not in acute distress. His right leg showed edema throughout. His vitals were within normal ranges except for a slightly elevated heart rate. 

Lab results showed an elevated WBC count at 13.0*# and a cTropnT level of 0.13*. A PTT test showed a value of 68.9*. An ultrasound of the lower extremities revealed occlusive clot of the right superficial femoral, deep femoral and greater saphenous veins extending into the common femoral vein. A CT angiogram showed bilateral central and segmental pulmonary emboli, a 6 mm pulmonary nodule in the lingula, a pleural-based 8-mm nodule in the right lower lobe, and right ventricular strain. The IVC filter operation report confirmed successful deployment of the filter in the infrarenal position.

The patient's medications upon admission included Prednisone 3mg qd, Omeprazole 20 mg qd, Lialda 1.2 g qd, Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg qd, Metoprolol 25 mg qd, and Mesalamine enema PR PRN. During his hospital stay, he continued his medications for ulcerative colitis, arthritis, and GERD. His hypertension remained normotensive with no change in his home regimen. 

The patient was discharged with an improved condition. His discharge diagnoses included deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, ulcerative colitis, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Ulcerative Colitis, Rheumatoid Arthritis, arrhythmia, DVT/PE (on coumadin), and GERD, presented with exertional chest pressure for the past 4 days. The patient reported severe chest pressure after walking a short distance to his car, associated with mild dyspnea. The symptoms resolved quickly with rest. He had a similar episode of chest pressure when walking to his PCP. An EKG at his PCP office was normal. The patient has a history of GERD with epigastric discomfort in the past, but this episode was different from previous GERD episodes. He also reported mild shortness of breath while doing yard work in the summer, but denies ever having any chest discomfort until this recent episode. He has no history of CAD but does have HLP and is on chronic steroids. His only family history is a grandfather with angina. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were: Temp:96.2 HR:83 BP:145/83 RR:20 POx:99%. He received SL Nitro, ASA 325. First set cardiac enzymes were negative. CXR was negative. EKG was similar to the last with more prominent t-waves laterally. 

The patient's past medical history includes Hyperlipidemia, Ulcerative colitis, GERD, Rheumatoid arthritis, and DVT/PE. He had a temporary filter placed due to clot burden on his right side and is currently on Coumadin with a diagnosis of heterozygosity for pro-thrombin gene mutation. 

The patient's family history includes a son with a dilated aorta who had been evaluated in the past for a genetic disorder (negative), a brother with leukemia, and a father with ALS. There is no family history of early MI, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death. 

Abnormal lab results upon admission include Hgb-13.9*, Neuts-74.1*, UreaN-24*, HCO3-34*, and CK(CPK)-215*. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a cardiac catheterization procedure. He was found to have 1 vessel disease with a 90% occlusion of his proximal LAD. A BMS was placed at the site. He tolerated the procedure well with no signs of bleeding. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Hydrochlorothiazide, Mesalamine, Metoprolol Tartrate, Prednisone, Warfarin, Ergocalciferol, and Omeprazole.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Prothrombin Gene Mutation, two prior Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVTs), a Pulmonary Embolism (PE), Coronary Artery Disease, Ulcerative Proctitis, Hypertension, Rheumatoid Arthritis, Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD), a possible history of arrhythmia, and Hyperlipidemia, presented with right calf heaviness. He reported experiencing sharp pain traveling upward, similar to his recent sciatica pain, but denied any swelling or tenderness in the right calf, which he had experienced with his previous DVTs. He also denied shortness of breath, dizziness, chest pain or discomfort, palpitations, visual disturbances, and sudden weakness. 

In the Emergency Department, his vital signs were stable and within normal limits. He was started on a heparin drip, but due to recent rectal bleeding and a therapeutic INR of 2.1, he was not given a heparin bolus. His lower extremity ultrasound showed a non-occlusive clot in the right thigh in the distribution of the prior clot. 

His admission labs showed abnormal Hemoglobin (HGB-13.3) and Hematocrit (HCT-38.9) levels. His lower extremity ultrasound revealed a peripheral partially occlusive deep vein thrombosis confined to the mid portion of the right superficial femoral vein. The chronicity of this clot could not be determined. His discharge labs showed abnormal RBC (4.41), Hemoglobin (Hgb-13.2), Hematocrit (Hct-37.8), and PTT (96.0) levels. 

The patient's family history includes his father who had ALS, his mother who had cancer, a son with a dilated aorta and hypercoagulable state, another son with a hypercoagulable state, and a brother who had leukemia. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Atorvastatin, Mesalamine, Metoprolol Tartrate, Nitroglycerin, Pantoprazole, Prednisone, Warfarin, Aspirin, Ergocalciferol, Co-Enzyme Q10, and Fish Oil. 

During his hospital stay, it was determined that the DVT imaged on his ultrasound was likely chronic rather than acute. He was deemed safe to discharge home. His outpatient hematologist and primary care physician were recommended to consider a slightly higher INR goal of 2.5 - 3.5.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of rheumatoid arthritis, ulcerative colitis (treated with Xeljanz), coronary artery disease (post bare-metal stent), and deep vein thrombosis/pulmonary embolism (on Coumadin), was admitted due to a presyncopal episode. He reported feeling lightheaded and as if he was going to pass out. He also noted darker stools over the past two weeks and increased fatigue. He had stopped taking his pantoprazole as his prescription could not be refilled. Upon arrival to the emergency department, he was found to have hemoccult positive black stool. His labs showed a white blood cell count of 8.5, hemoglobin/hematocrit of 8.9/28.2, platelet count of 206, INR of 3.4, sodium of 144, potassium of 4.0, phosphorus of 1.2, and troponin of less than 0.01. 

His past medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis, ulcerative colitis, DVT/PE due to a heterozygous pro-thrombin gene mutation, coronary artery disease (post bare-metal stent to left anterior descending artery), gastroesophageal reflux disease/hiatal hernia, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, left ear hearing loss, right knee arthritis, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and a left knee replacement. His family history includes his father having ALS, his mother having cancer, one son with a dilated aorta and a hypercoagulable state, another son with a hypercoagulable state, and a brother who had leukemia.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Atorvastatin, Chlorthalidone, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Prednisone, Xeljanz, Warfarin, Aspirin, Vitamin D, coenzyme Q10, Cyanocobalamin, and Tamsulosin. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) and sigmoidoscopy. The EGD revealed erosive gastritis, while the sigmoidoscopy showed normal mucosa.
