His labs upon discharge showed a white blood cell count of 7.5, red blood cell count of 2.53, hemoglobin of 7.7, hematocrit of 23.5, and red cell distribution width of 17.4. His PTT was 36.6, urea nitrogen was 27, creatinine was 0.8, phosphorus was 1.2, total iron binding capacity was 248, transferrin was 191, and C-reactive protein was 0.3. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute blood loss anemia and upper gastrointestinal bleeding from erosive gastritis. His hypertension, ulcerative colitis, coronary artery disease, DVT/PE, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and rheumatoid arthritis were managed as per his home medications. His hypophosphatemia was treated as needed. His blood pressure medications and aspirin were held until his follow-up appointment.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of recent bilateral cerebellar infarcts with bilateral vertebral artery dissections, asthma, and H. pylori, presented with new right face and torso numbness. She had been on warfarin and Lovenox for her previous conditions. She reported waking up with a stiff neck and experiencing a sense of "fogginess" and disequilibrium. Her systolic blood pressure was found to be in the 190s at her primary care doctor's office. She was referred to the emergency department where she was found to have bilateral vertebral artery dissections and bilateral cerebellar embolic appearing infarcts. After discharge, she experienced new onset bilateral blurry vision and a sense of disequilibrium. She also reported feeling anxious and having an "accelerated" heart rate. A repeat CTA and MRI of the brain showed stable dissections and no new infarcts. 

On the day prior to presentation, she reported severe right neck and shoulder pain, new right face and arm numbness, and a sensation of someone squeezing her right shoulder. A CT angiogram showed a mildly worse right vertebral artery dissection and a slightly improved left vertebral artery dissection. She denied any lightheadedness, confusion, difficulty with speech or vision, muscle weakness, or other general symptoms. 

Her physical exam showed decreased sensation to light touch in the right hemi-face, downward drift in the right arm, and decreased sensation to pinprick in the right arm and torso. She also showed some clumsiness with finger tapping bilaterally and was hesitant and took short steps when walking. 

Lab results showed normal blood cell counts, glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, electrolytes, and other blood components. Her PTT was slightly elevated. Imaging showed worsening of the right vertebral artery dissection, left cerebellar and occipital late acute infarcts, mild degenerative disc disease at C5-6, and a 14 x 16 mm proteinaceous or hemorrhagic nodule in the right lobe of the thyroid. 

The patient's hospital course included management of her bilateral vertebral dissections and cerebellar strokes, neck pain, and thyroid nodule. She was discharged with a diagnosis of cerebellar stroke and cervical radiculopathy. Her medications upon admission included warfarin, Symbicort, ProAir HFA, montelukast, and enoxaparin sodium. She denied a family history of stroke at a young age, blood clots, connective tissue disorders, or autoimmune disorders.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of bilateral vertebral dissections, multiple cerebrovascular accidents (CVAs), and asthma, was admitted due to concerns of a progressing right vertebral dissection in the context of worsening headache symptoms. She reported an acute onset of severe throbbing pain over the left posterior occiput, which resolved after an hour, followed by a similar headache over the right posterior occiput, with pain radiating down her right arm into her fingers. This pain evolved into a pressure-like sensation that intermittently spread up to her vertex. She also experienced mild intermittent left-sided chest pain and a sensation akin to "rocking on a boat". She denied any clear diplopia, room-spinning sensation, dysarthria, or dysphagia. No new focal numbness or weakness was reported. She had no recent head trauma or falls. She reported that her Warfarin was recently decreased to 10mg from 15mg due to an elevated INR of 4 last week, with follow-up levels downtrending to 0.8. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, cooperative, and in mild distress. Neurological examination revealed decreased sensation to light touch over the right forehead and right anterior shin, as well as in the right lateral forearm. Motor examination showed normal bulk and tone throughout, with no pronator drift bilaterally. The patient's deep tendon reflexes were normal and plantar response was flexor bilaterally. Coordination was intact with no intention tremor or dysdiadochokinesia noted. 

Laboratory data showed an elevated AlkPhos level of 29 and an elevated Acetaminophen level of 8. Imaging data from an MRI/MRA revealed no new strokes, and interval resolution of previously seen bilateral vertebral artery dissections when compared with prior vessel imaging. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Enoxaparin Sodium 80 mg SC BID, Warfarin 10 mg PO daily, Montelukast 10 mg PO daily, and Symbicort (budesonide-formoterol) 160-4.5 mcg/actuation inhalation BID. 

The patient's headache and neck pain were well controlled with Tylenol 1g TID and Flexeril for treatment of muscle spasm. After discussion with the patient's outpatient neurologist, the decision was made to stop anticoagulation and replace it with aspirin, given that the vertebral dissections had resolved. Therefore, heparin and warfarin were stopped. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of cervical and occipital muscle spasm. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was instructed to continue taking aspirin 81mg daily, stop Lovenox and Warfarin, manage pain with Tylenol and Flexeril as needed for muscle spasm, and continue previously arranged outpatient follow-ups. 

The patient has no known history of blood clots or connective tissue disorder, and denies a family history of stroke at a young age, blood clots, connective tissue disorders, or autoimmune disorders.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a significant history of bilateral cerebellar infarcts, including left cerebellar infarct and left occipital infarct, secondary to bilateral vertebral artery dissection. This is thought to be related to an undiagnosed connective tissue disorder, with genetic workup revealing only for SMAD3. She presented with bilateral neck pain that migrated from one side of her neck to the other, vertigo, vomiting, blurry vision, and difficulty swallowing. She was given ASA 81 mg and a CTA head and neck showed a new right V4 dissection. 

Her past medical history includes recent bilateral cerebellar infarctions with bilateral vertebral artery dissections, asthma, and H. pylori. She has no known history of blood clots or connective tissue disorder. She denies a family history of stroke at a young age, blood clots, connective tissue disorders, or autoimmune disorders. 

Upon admission, she was taking Cyclobenzaprine 5 mg PO BID. Her physical exam was generally normal, with the exception of subtle nystagmus on left gaze. Her labs showed a DM: A1c 5.1%, LDL 67, and TSH 1.0. A CTA showed a possible dissection of the right vertebral artery and an MRI showed a late acute to early subacute infarct of the right cerebellar hemisphere and right central vermis, as well as right vertebral artery dissection. There was no evidence of occlusion or aneurysm formation. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with an acute ischemic stroke in the right cerebellar hemisphere and right central vermis, most likely secondary to right vertebral artery dissection. She was started on dual antiplatelet therapy of ASA 81 and Plavix. Her stroke risk factors include presumed connective tissue disorder, DM: A1c 5.1%, LDL 67, and TSH 1.0. 

Upon discharge, her condition was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent. She was prescribed Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY and Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY. She was instructed to seek emergency medical attention if she experiences any symptoms of stroke.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, presented with palpitations, a symptom she has experienced for several years. She has a history of hypertension (HTN), sinus of valsalva aneurysm, and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). The patient has been managing her infrequent SVT episodes with beta blockers. On the day of admission, she experienced palpitations, left-sided chest pressure, shortness of breath, and mild left hand numbness. These symptoms are consistent with her previous episodes. She had not taken her usual dose of atenolol that day. Her heart rate was initially 160 but returned to normal by the time she was in a room. 

Her past medical history includes HTN, SVT, sinus of valsalva aneurysm (not seen on most recent 2 previous echos), uterine fibroids with menometrorrhagia, left breast cyst removal, and migraine headaches. Her family history is significant for hypertension, with no history of early premature coronary disease or sudden cardiac death. Her father passed away from a stroke and also had hypertension. Her mother is alive and has hypertension. She has three brothers, but their medical history is unclear. She has a paternal aunt with congestive heart failure. 

Upon physical examination, her vitals were stable and within normal limits. Her cardiac examination was normal with regular rate and rhythm, normal S1, S2, and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. 

Lab results showed an elevated CK(CPK) at 155, a slightly prolonged PTT at 19.8, and a slightly low MCV at 77 and MCHC at 37.1. Her glucose was slightly elevated at 120. Her echocardiogram showed normal left atrium size, normal left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and regional/global systolic function, and no definite sinus of Valsalva aneurysm. 

During her hospital stay, she was managed for her palpitations, which were attributed to her history of SVT and medication non-compliance. She was continued on her home doses of atenolol and hydrochlorothiazide for her hypertension. Her sinus of Valsalva aneurysm, which is most likely congenital and sometimes associated with certain syndromes, was not seen on her recent echo. This will be followed up as an outpatient with cardiology. 

Upon admission, she was on hydrochlorothiazide 25mg daily and atenolol 50mg daily. Her discharge diagnoses were palpitations, SVT in the setting of medication non-compliance, sinus of valsalva aneurysm, and hypertension. She was stable upon discharge.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Asthma, Hypertension (HTN), sinus of valsalva aneurysm, and Supraventricular Tachycardia (SVT), presented with body aches, malaise, a non-productive cough, and sinus pressure. These symptoms began two weeks prior to her visit, improved after seven days, but then worsened again. She reported elevated temperatures up to 100 degrees, mild diarrhea, and a mild headache. She also mentioned using her asthma inhaler twice since the onset of her symptoms. 

Upon admission, she was afebrile with normal blood pressure and oxygen saturation at 98% on room air. However, during her stay, she had one instance of fever at 102 degrees and desaturation to 91% on room air, requiring transient oxygen support. Her lab results showed a white blood cell count of 3.3, normal Hemoglobin and Hematocrit, and platelets, and a normal chemistry panel with a creatinine of 0.7. Her lactate was 1.2. She tested positive for Influenza A PCR. 

She was initially ordered Tamiflu, which she refused due to stomach upset. She was then started on ceftriaxone/azithromycin and later transitioned to doxycycline for presumed pneumonia, as her symptoms improved and then worsened. A chest x-ray showed a retrocardiac opacity possibly concerning for pneumonia. Other medications she received included DuoNeb's, Tylenol, metoprolol succinate 50 mg, and IV fluids.

Her past medical history includes HTN, SVT, sinus of valsalva aneurysm (not seen on most recent 2 previous echos), uterine fibroids with menometrorrhagia, left breast cyst removal, and migraine headaches. Her family history does not include early premature coronary disease or sudden cardiac death. Her father passed away from a stroke and had a history of hypertension; her mother also had HTN and passed away from a stroke. She has a paternal aunt with congestive heart failure and there is an extensive family history of hypertension.

Her physical exam upon admission showed her to be significantly congested, clammy, and appearing ill. Her cardiovascular system was regular in rate and rhythm, her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, and her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. Her discharge physical exam showed her to be sitting up in bed in no acute distress, with a regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft, non-tender, and non-distended abdomen.

Her admission labs showed a white blood cell count of 3.3*, MCV of 78*, Monos of 25.3*, Eos of 0.6*, AbsLymp of 0.68*, AbsMono of 0.84*, AbsEos of 0.02*, and a glucose level of 123*. Her discharge labs showed a white blood cell count of 3.6*, Hct of 33.3*, and MCV of 79*. Her imaging showed slightly low lung volumes, normal heart size, normal mediastinal and hilar contours, normal pulmonary vasculature, and retrocardiac opacity likely reflecting atelectasis in the setting of low lung volumes. Her microbiology results showed a positive result for Flu A PCR.

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with Influenza A and there was a concern for community-acquired pneumonia. She was also suspected to have asthma and leukopenia. Her chronic issues of SVT and HTN were managed with her home medications of metoprolol XL 50 mg and HCTZ 25 mg respectively. Her medications upon admission included Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg, Metoprolol Succinate XL 50 mg, ProAir HFA (albuterol sulfate) 90 mcg/actuation inhalation, and Vitamin D 1000 UNIT.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of diabetes, was admitted for symptomatic cholecystectomy following a month of right upper quadrant pain. An ultrasound performed at an outside hospital revealed gallstones, leading to her referral for surgery. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. Her family history includes gallstones, but this is deemed noncontributory. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Lidocaine 5% Patch, MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 1000 mg, GlipiZIDE 10 mg, Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg, Vitamin D, and Docusate Sodium 100 mg. 

The patient underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy without complications. Post-surgery, she was stable, with well-controlled pain, and was able to tolerate a progressively advanced diet. She was able to void without issue, ambulated early and frequently, and was compliant with respiratory toilet and incentive spirometry. During her stay, she received subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used. 

Upon discharge, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and her pain was well controlled. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was discharged home without services, with instructions for recovery and follow-up.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of left temporal ganglioglioma, for which he underwent a subtotal resection. He has been experiencing symptomatic epilepsy with both focal dyscognitive and likely rapidly evolving generalized seizures. His first seizure occurred while at work, and he was subsequently found to have a left frontotemporal tumor. The patient has not received any further treatments like chemotherapy, radiation, or additional resections. He has been monitored with serial MRIs initially every three months, then every six months, and then yearly without reported progression of disease. The only residual deficit from his surgery has been problems with short-term memory. 

The patient's seizure semiologies include focal dyscognitive seizures characterized by lip smacking, confusion, and slurred speech lasting about a minute, and generalized convulsions characterized by loss of consciousness, 4 extremity rhythmic shaking, then diffuse weakness. Triggers for his seizures include sleep deprivation and possibly alcohol. 

The patient's antiepileptic drug (AED) history is unclear, but he reports being trialed on multiple medications with fair control after the surgery. His current AED regimen includes Phenobarbital 90mg BID, Lamotrigine XR 300mg QHS, and Gabapentin 300mg TID. Previous AEDs that were ineffective include Levetiracetam, Pregabalin, and Lacosamide. 

The patient has had multiple emergency department visits for seizures, but no urgent hospitalizations, ICU stays, or intubations for seizures. He denies any current difficulties producing or comprehending speech, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, and difficulty with gait. 

The patient's family history does not include epilepsy or other brain tumors, but does include lung cancer, breast cancer, and a father who underwent coronary artery bypass grafting. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be awake, cooperative, and alert. His neurological exam was normal, with no evidence of apraxia or neglect. 

Abnormal lab results include a glucose level of 103*, a phenytoin level of <0.6*, and a red blood cell count of 4.28*. His lamotrigine level was also found to be low at 1.2. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was admitted for long-term monitoring (LTM) seizure capture and medication titration. Three focal dyscognitive seizures were captured off AEDs, characterized by an initial flushed feeling, then unresponsiveness, lip smacking, lasting less than 2 minutes each. These seizures were captured on EEG with seizures from the left temporal region. His home AEDs of Phenobarbital and Gabapentin were stopped upon discharge, and his home lamotrigine XR 300mg QHS was increased to lamotrigine 200mg PO BID. 

The patient's brain MRI seizure protocol was unable to be completed while inpatient, so it was arranged to be done on a later date. His medications upon admission included lamoTRIgine 300 mg oral QHS, PHENObarbital 90 mg PO BID, Gabapentin 300 mg PO TID, and Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q4H as needed for seizures.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for long-term monitoring (LTM) for medically refractory epilepsy and pre-surgical evaluation. He has a history of left frontotemporal ganglioglioma, which was surgically removed. Despite the surgery, the patient has continued to experience multiple seizure semiologies, including simple partial, complex partial, and secondary generalized. He reports an average frequency of one seizure every two weeks, but can experience up to three in one week. He occasionally bites his tongue and often reports feeling sore following episodes. 

The patient's epilepsy risk factors include an abnormal pregnancy, CNS neoplasm, a neurosurgical procedure, alcohol abuse, and drug abuse. He has no family history of seizures or epilepsy. His current antiepileptic drug (AED) regimen includes 250mg lamotrigine twice daily, Vimpat twice daily, and 1mg Lorazepam as needed for prolonged seizure or cluster. He has previously tried Phenobarbital, Gabapentin, Levetiracetam, and Pregabalin, but these were stopped due to ineffectiveness. 

On admission, the patient's blood work showed abnormal WBC count of 10.4, RBC count of 4.38, and glucose level of 114. His interictal SPECT showed decreased uptake in the left mid temporal lobe, consistent with postsurgical changes. His EEGs showed occasional left temporal spike and slow wave discharges, consistent with a prominent focus of increased cortical irritability in this region. The EEGs also showed continuous focal left temporal theta frequency slowing, suggesting the presence of subcortical dysfunction in this region. Several seizures were captured on cvEEG during his hospital stay. 

The patient's past medical history includes a left frontotemporal brain tumor, ganglioglioma, which was surgically resected, and symptomatic epilepsy. He also takes Melatonin 3mg at bedtime for insomnia. His family history does not include epilepsy or other brain tumors, but does include lung cancer, breast cancer, and a father who underwent coronary artery bypass grafting. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was monitored on cvEEG with seizure precautions. His medications were slowly weaned to off, and he experienced several generalized tonic-clonic (GTC) seizures, one of which required intervention with IV lorazepam. He was subsequently restarted on his prior home doses of lacosamide and levetiracetam, and was started newly on clobazam. His discharge diagnosis was focal symptomatic epilepsy.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted for an elective left craniotomy for temporal lobectomy due to a history of seizures. His past medical history includes a left frontotemporal brain tumor, specifically a ganglioglioma, which had been partially resected previously. He also has symptomatic epilepsy. His non-AED medication includes Melatonin 3mg qhs for insomnia. There is no family history of epilepsy or brain tumors, though there is a history of lung and breast cancer, and his father had a coronary artery bypass graft. 

Upon physical examination at discharge, the patient was alert and oriented. A small fluid collection was noted under the incision, but the incision was clean, dry, and intact. The patient's strength and sensation were intact, and a head wrap was in place. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a left craniotomy for temporal lobectomy, which he tolerated well. Post-operative CT and MRI scans showed expected changes. The patient remained stable and was transferred to the floor on post-operative day (POD) #3. A head wrap was replaced on POD #4 due to a small fluid collection around the incision. 

A neurology consult was obtained for assistance with management of antiepileptic medications post-operatively. The patient's primary epileptologist recommended no changes to his anti-epileptic medications while inpatient. 

Post-operatively, the patient's white blood cell count was elevated at 21.1, thought to be due to the steroids he received during the case. His CBC was checked daily and WBC trended down to 7.8 at discharge. 

On POD#4, the patient's pain was well controlled, he was tolerating a PO diet, ambulating independently, and urinating without signs of retention. He was discharged home in stable condition. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Clobazam 10mg BID, Vimpat 200mg BID, Lamotrigine 250mg BID, and Ativan 1mg prn for seizures. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of epilepsy. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male of undisclosed age with a history of a low-grade glioma, which was previously treated with resection. This procedure was complicated by refractory seizures despite adherence to antiepileptic drugs (AEDs), leading to a left temporal lobectomy. The patient has been on a regimen of LACOSamide 200 mg twice daily, lamotrigine 250 mg twice daily, clobazam 10 mg twice daily, and lorazepam 1 mg as needed. He also takes melatonin 3mg at bedtime for insomnia. 

The patient was admitted due to a breakthrough seizure, which was atypical as it did not begin with an aura or start with a simple etiology followed by secondary generalization. This event was concerning in the context of a new symptomatic headache and a known hygroma. Prior to admission, the patient had been found to have a post-surgical left temporal hygroma with CT imaging demonstrating possible dural communication. 

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam was largely unremarkable. Neurologically, he was alert and oriented, with fluent language, intact repetition and comprehension, and no evidence of apraxia or neglect. His motor and sensory functions were normal, with no deficits to light touch, pinprick, cold sensation, vibratory sense, or proprioception. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (11.1*), RBC (4.03*), Hgb (12.7*), Hct (37.2*), AbsLymp (3.72*), AbsMono (0.86*), and Glucose (102*). An MRI with and without contrast showed postoperative changes from the anterior left temporal lobectomy, an extracranial fluid collection with apparent connection to extra-axial space at the left middle cranial fossa, and worsened parenchymal abnormality in the anterior temporal lobe, extending into the subinsula, with cystic changes. These changes were thought to be the sequela of ischemia, less likely inflammatory or infectious process. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's breakthrough seizure was treated with an increase in the dose of his clobazam from 10 mg twice daily to 10mg in the morning and 15 mg at night. He remained seizure-free without adverse effects or notable drowsiness on this increased regimen. 

The patient has no family history of epilepsy or brain tumors, though there is a history of lung and breast cancer, and his father underwent coronary artery bypass grafting. Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a breakthrough seizure and subgaleal fluid collection. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient, with a history of refractory epilepsy, was admitted due to fluid collection and epilepsy. He had previously undergone a left temporal craniotomy for left temporal lobectomy and amygdalohippocampectomy. The patient also has a history of left frontotemporal ganglioglioma which was resected, and grand mal and complex partial seizures. During this admission, he underwent a revision of the left temporal craniotomy incision and dural opening repair due to a large fluid collection under the skin overlying the prior craniotomy site. 

The patient's family history does not include epilepsy or other brain tumors, but there is a history of lung and breast cancer, and his father had undergone coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) and later passed away from ALS. 

Upon discharge, the patient was alert and interactive, with clear and coherent mental status. His physical examination showed spontaneous eye opening, full orientation, and complex command following. His pupils were equal, round, and reactive to light, with full extraocular movements. His face was symmetric, tongue was midline, and there was no pronator drift. His speech was fluent, comprehension was intact, and sensation and proprioception were intact. His headwrap dressing was clean, dry, and intact. 

The patient's hospital course was uncomplicated. Post-operative CT showed normal post-operative changes and near total resolution of subgaleal fluid collection. He remained hemodynamically and neurologically stable, was ambulating, and tolerating oral intake prior to discharge. 

The patient was on Clobazam 10mg in the morning and 20mg in the evening, Lacosamide 200mg twice daily, Lamotrigine 250mg twice daily, Lorazepam 1mg as needed for seizures, and Melatonin 10mg at bedtime as needed for insomnia upon admission. He did not experience any seizures during this hospital admission and was continued on his home antiepileptics. 

The patient was diagnosed with subgaleal cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) collection and dural opening. He was discharged home with instructions on post-operative care, activity restrictions, medication use, potential post-operative symptoms, and when to seek medical attention.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of paranoid schizophrenia and chronic alcohol abuse, was admitted due to leg edema. He had a history of a PEA arrest and ICU course for septic shock due to pneumonia. He was reportedly living in poor conditions and was brought in by a social worker after being found with several open wounds on his legs, which were swollen and erythematous. He had been discharged from the hospital recently and was supposed to be taking antibiotics for leg cellulitis, but reportedly had not been doing so. He denied any pain, fever, chills, increased edema, other joint pain or rash. In the ED, he was given a dose of IV vancomycin and his vitals were stable.

His past medical history includes heavy alcohol abuse and frequent ED visits. He denies any family history of medical problems. Upon admission, he was not taking any preadmission medications. 

Physical examination revealed brawny edema with mild erythema in both legs, skin flaking and changes consistent with fungal infection, and wounds with eschar and prior evidence of bleeding near the left ankle. His vitals were stable and he was alert and oriented. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count (3.31), hemoglobin (9.8), hematocrit (30.7), MCHC (31.9), RDWSD (47.2), lymphocytes (18.4), and monocytes (13.5). Imaging studies showed no evidence of deep venous thrombosis in the visualized right or left lower extremity veins, but severe soft tissue edema in the calves bilaterally. Arterial and venous studies were within normal limits. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for cellulitis and lower extremity ulcers with vancomycin, ciprofloxacin, metronidazole, and wound care. He also received thiamine, folate, and multivitamins for his history of alcohol abuse. Despite recommendations for a longer course of antibiotics, the patient left against medical advice. He was transitioned to oral antibiotics (doxycycline, ciprofloxacin, and metronidazole) on the day of discharge. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses were cellulitis, chronic venous stasis ulcers, chronic paranoid schizophrenia, and hypoxic brain injury. His mental status was sometimes confused, but he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service due to severe back and leg discomfort, with right-sided sciatica and pain radiating to the right lower leg. She reported a sensation of a pinched nerve and denied any trauma. The pain was present during activity and at rest, and was exacerbated by sitting, standing, and bending. She had been experiencing these symptoms for approximately one year. 

Her past medical history includes measles, chickenpox, arthritis, and an unspecified disease. She has undergone several surgical procedures in the past, including hand surgery, right shoulder surgery, tubal ligation, and a core biopsy. Her family history is significant for cancer, lung disease, neurologic disease, and arthritis.

During her hospital stay, she underwent an anterior/posterior lumbar decompression with fusion from L3 to S1. The procedure was completed without complications and she was transferred to the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU) in a stable condition. She was given intravenous antibiotics as per standard protocol and her initial postoperative pain was managed with a patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) pump. On the second day of her hospital stay, she returned to the operating room for a scheduled L3-S1 decompression with posterior spinal interbody fusion (PSIF) as part of a staged 2-part procedure. This second surgery was also completed without complications and she was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. 

Her postoperative hemoglobin and hematocrit levels were low, and she was transfused packed red blood cells with good results. She experienced confusion on the IV pain medication and was started on low dose oxycodone. She was transitioned to oral pain medication when she was able to tolerate a regular diet. Her Foley catheter was removed on the second postoperative day. She was fitted with a lumbar brace for comfort and physical therapy was consulted for mobilization and ambulation. 

Her laboratory results showed several abnormal findings, including low red blood cell count (RBC), low hemoglobin (Hgb), low hematocrit (Hct), and high mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH). Her glucose levels were also elevated and her chloride levels were low. Her calcium and phosphorus levels were also low.

Upon admission, she was taking several medications including Carbidopa-Levodopa, Selegiline HCl, Comtan (entacapone), pramipexole, Amantadine, Gabapentin, Midodrine, Levothyroxine Sodium, and Atorvastatin. 

Her discharge diagnoses included lumbar stenosis, spondylosis, disc degeneration, acute post-op blood loss anemia, and scoliosis. She was discharged in good condition to an extended care facility.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to worsening back pain. After a thorough review of her medical history, physical examination, and radiographic studies, she was deemed a suitable candidate for XLIF and posterior T12-L2 fusion. The patient agreed to the plan and consent was obtained. 

Her past medical history includes measles, chickenpox, arthritis, and an unspecified disease. She has also undergone hand surgery, right shoulder surgery, tubal ligation, and a core biopsy in the past. Her family history includes cancer, lung disease, neurologic disease, and arthritis.

Upon physical examination, the patient was well developed, well nourished, and alert. Her range of motion in the lumbar spine was somewhat limited due to pain. She was able to ambulate well with the assistance of a walker and a TLSO brace for support. Her gross motor examination revealed good strength in the bilateral lower extremities. Sensation was intact throughout all affected dermatomes. The lateral and posterior lumbar incisions were clean, dry, and intact without signs of infection. A Foley catheter was in place.

The patient underwent XLIF T12-L2 and posterior fusion T12-L2 without complications. Postoperative pain was managed with a dilaudid PCA. She remained NPO until bowel function returned, then diet was advanced as tolerated. A bupivicaine epidural pain catheter was placed at the time of the posterior surgery and remained in place until postop day one. A hemovac drain that was placed at the time of surgery was also removed on POD#2. Physical therapy was consulted for mobilization OOB to ambulate.

Her lab results showed abnormal RBC count (2.59*), Hgb (8.3*), and Hct (24.8*). Her WBC count was 8.2. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Amantadine, Atorvastatin, Carbidopa-Levodopa, entacapone, Folic Acid, Gabapentin, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, Levothyroxine Sodium, Midodrine, pramipexole, Selegiline HCl, Calcium Carbonate, Cyanocobalamin, and Multivitamins.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of spondylosis and stenosis. At the time of discharge, she was afebrile with stable vital signs, comfortable on oral pain control, and tolerating a regular diet. Her Foley catheter remained in place upon discharge to rehab. She was advised not to lift anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks and to go for walks as part of her recovery. She was also given instructions on how to care for her wound and when to call the office.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service due to back pain. She had a history of measles, chickenpox, arthritis, and an unspecified disease. She had previously undergone hand surgery, right shoulder surgery, tubal ligation, and a core biopsy. The patient had a family history of cancer, lung disease, neurologic disease, and arthritis. 

Upon admission, the patient was well developed, well nourished, and alert and oriented. She had limited range of motion in the thoracolumbar spine due to pain but was ambulating well with the assistance of a walker and a thoracolumbar sacral orthosis (TLSO) brace for support. Sensation was intact throughout all affected dermatomes and there was no clonus present. The thoracolumbar incision was clean, dry, and intact without erythema, edema, or drainage. The patient was voiding well without a foley catheter.

The patient had presented to the doctor after a fall and was evaluated at an outside hospital. Radiographs revealed a T12 fracture following a prior T12-L2 fusion. After reviewing the patient's history, physical examination, and radiographic studies, it was determined that she would be a good candidate for revision posterior fusion. The patient agreed with the plan and consent was obtained and signed.

The patient underwent a T10 to L1 fusion, open treatment of a T12 fracture, laminotomies of T12, T11, and T10, instrumentation from T10 to L1, removal of previous instrumentation at T12, and an autograft. The surgery was performed without complications and the patient tolerated the procedure well. Postoperative pain was initially controlled with a PCA and the patient was transitioned to oral pain medication when tolerating a PO diet. A hemovac drain that was placed at the time of surgery was removed on postoperative day 2. Physical therapy was consulted for mobilization out of bed to ambulate and a TLSO brace was fitted for the patient.

Lab results revealed a white blood cell count of 8.9, red blood cell count of 2.97, hemoglobin of 9.5, hematocrit of 27.1, mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 35.1, all of which were marked as abnormal. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a T12 fracture. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. The patient was instructed not to lift anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks, to walk several times a day for a specified number of minutes as part of her recovery, and to change positions every 45 minutes when sitting or standing. She was also given a brace to wear for comfort when walking and instructions on wound care and medication management.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female under the care of Orthopaedics, was admitted due to bilateral lower extremity weakness and numbness. She has a history of Parkinson's disease, back pain, measles, chickenpox, arthritis, and an unspecified disease. She has undergone several surgical procedures including L3-S1 fusion, XLIF and fusion, and a revision of posterior laminectomy and fusion from T10-L2 due to a complete collapse of the T12 vertebra body. She also had hand surgery, right shoulder surgery, tubal ligation, and a core biopsy in the past. 

During this admission, she underwent transpedicular decompression at T10, fusion from T4 to T12, revision of instrumentation from T4 to T12, and multiple thoracic laminotomies. Post-surgery, she was ambulating well with the assistance of a walker and a TLSO brace for support. Her gross motor examination showed improvement compared to pre-surgery. Sensation was intact throughout all affected dermatomes and there was no clonus present. The thoracolumbar incision was clean, dry, and intact without erythema, edema, or drainage. A Foley catheter was in place.

The patient reported several episodes of urinary incontinence and an inability to ambulate due to the numbness and weakness in her lower extremities. She denied having fevers, chills, bowel symptoms, increasing pain, or other complaints. She also reported no recent falls or trauma. Her family history includes cancer, lung disease, neurologic disease, and arthritis.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (2.86), Hgb (8.9), Hct (26.2), and RDW (16.2). An MRI revealed severe canal narrowing at T10 and increased cord signal at T11-T12. Post-operative labs were stable and a hemovac drain placed during surgery was removed on post-operative day 2. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was a T10 fracture and spinal stenosis. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid, and her mental status was sometimes confused. She was discharged to extended care.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to abdominal pain that started 5 days prior to admission. The pain was initially gradual and dull but became sharp and intermittent in the right lower quadrant the night before presentation. The patient also experienced associated nausea and vomiting, and anorexia. She has a history of ovarian torsion and believes the current pain feels similar. In the ED, she was given subq dilaudid for pain control due to inability to obtain IV access.

Her past medical history includes Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Asthma, Hypertension, Migraines, and Obesity. She has undergone several surgical procedures in the past, including a laparoscopic appendectomy and cholecystectomy (both for endometriosis), laparoscopic fulguration of endometriosis twice, and a laparoscopic left ovarian de-torsion/cystectomy. She has allergies to Compazine, IV Dye, Iodine Containing Contrast Media, Augmentin, and morphine. Her family history is not on file.

Upon physical examination, she was found to be in no acute distress, with regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft, obese abdomen that was diffusely tender, maximally in the supra-pubic and right lower quadrant. She had active bowel sounds and 1+ edema bilaterally. Her bimanual exam revealed cervical motion tenderness and right adnexal tenderness. 

A pelvic ultrasound revealed an enlarged right adnexal cyst measuring 5.9 cm, a normal-sized left ovary with no identifiable venous or arterial waveforms, and a fibroid uterus with normal endometrium. Cultures were negative for Gonorrhea and Chlamydia.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy which resulted in a right cystectomy for a simple cyst and endometrioma. Post-surgery, her pain remained unchanged in terms of quality, location, and severity. She was actively requesting all her pain medicines around the clock and would also ask for more pain medicines in between dosages. She was receiving IV dilaudid and toradol. She also complained of nausea and reported vomiting. For her nausea, she was receiving IV zofran and phenergan. She was also placed on intravenous protonix. 

Her medications upon admission included Metoprolol, Metformin, Advair, Albuterol, Tompamax, Singulair 20, Imitrex, Toradol, Phenergan, and Percocet. Her blood sugars were followed while in hospital and she was placed on an insulin sliding scale while off her metformin. She was discharged on hospital day 4 with bearable pain, ambulating and voiding without difficulty. She was also set up with an outpatient chronic pain appointment. Her discharge diagnoses were Endometrioma and Chronic pain.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to nausea and a two-day history of an unspecified condition. She reported having a fever at home, but none was documented in the emergency department. Her past medical history includes Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Asthma, Hypertension, Migraines, and Obesity. She has undergone several surgical procedures in the past, including a laparoscopic appendectomy and cholecystectomy, both performed due to endometriosis. She also had two laparoscopic fulgurations of endometriosis and a laparoscopic left ovarian de-torsion/cystectomy. There is no family history on file.

Upon physical examination, the patient was tearful, with a temperature of 99.3, heart rate of 96, blood pressure of 127/90, respiratory rate of 17, and oxygen saturation of 100% on room air. Her abdomen was soft but tender to deep palpation on the right side of the umbilicus, with no rebound or guarding. Incision sites appeared clean, dry, and intact, and her abdomen was not distended.

Lab results showed a glucose level of 194*, total CO2 of 19*, and lipase of 63*, all of which are abnormal. Her Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC) was 37.3*, and she had an elevated eosinophil count of 4.3*. Other lab results, including WBC, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, RDW, and PLT count, were within normal ranges.

During her hospital stay, the patient refused to attempt oral intake of medicine or antiemetics and expressed a desire to go home to take oral medications there. She was given Zofran and Phenergan, as well as one dose each of IV Dilaudid and Toradol, which resulted in some improvement. The patient was discharged home with her previous oral medications and antiemetics.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Metoprolol 100mg twice daily, Metformin 1000mg twice daily, Advair once daily, Albuterol every four hours as needed, Tompamax 100mg daily, Singulair 20mg daily, Imitrex 6mg as needed for headaches, Toradol 40mg orally every eight hours as needed, Phenergan 25mg every six hours as needed for nausea, and Percocet as needed.

The patient was discharged with diagnoses of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was instructed to continue taking her medications and anti-nausea medications until her appointment with the clinic.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), stage II Breast Cancer post resection and chemotherapy, Cervical Cancer, stable meningioma, and chronic pain syndrome, presented with progressive weakness, worsening headaches, blurry vision, photophobia, and generalized weakness. She also reported dyspnea on exertion and chest tightness when climbing stairs, intermittent tingling in her feet for the past two months, and new upper and lower back pain for the past month. She has a history of chronic bilateral bone pain. The patient denied dizziness, left hypochondrium pain, and chest pain. She reported drinking one glass of wine upon hearing of her aunt's death. 

In the Emergency Department, her vitals were stable but her blood pressure dipped to 84/49, improving to 108/64 after 1L of Normal Saline. On examination, she had right horizontal nystagmus, word finding difficulty, and slightly slurred speech. Her alcohol level was 202. A head CT showed no hemorrhage and a stable cranial vertex lesion, likely a meningioma. Her chest X-ray was negative and her EKG was unchanged from prior. She received thiamine 100mg IV and was admitted for management of altered mental status. 

Her past medical history also includes anxiety, left parotid pleomorphic adenoma post resection, migraines, and osteoporosis. Her family history includes her mother with arthritis and her father with a hearing disorder. She has two healthy siblings and no children. There is no family history of headaches. 

Her lab results showed a Chloride level of 110* and RBC count of 3.87* and MCH of 32.7*. Her brain MRI showed a stable size of meningioma, extending through the dura into the right side of the sagittal sinus, which is patent. A non-contrast head CT showed no acute intracranial hemorrhage and a stable 1.7 cm hyperdense lesion at the cranial vertex, likely a meningioma. 

Upon admission, she was on Letrozole, Ativan, MS Contin, Oxycodone, Compazine, Topiramate, Zoledronic acid infusion, Vitamin C, Calcium + Vit D, and Rolaids. 

Her headache was likely exacerbated by alcohol intake and the social stressor of her aunt's death. Her altered mental status was likely due to alcohol intoxication. Her brain MRI showed a stable meningioma with no change from prior. Her breast and cervical cancers are in remission. Her depression was likely exacerbated by her aunt's death. She was discharged in a stable condition with a diagnosis of acute on chronic headache.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of depression, two spontaneous abortions, two therapeutic abortions, and a single vaginal delivery, was admitted due to heavy vaginal bleeding. She was five weeks postpartum and had experienced normal vaginal bleeding until the week of admission, when she had three large gushes of blood. The first two instances were minor, but the third involved heavy, flowing blood and clots, causing dizziness and lightheadedness. She was initially seen at an outside hospital where her hematocrit was found to be 22%. An ultrasound suggested retained products of conception. Her bleeding stabilized on its own and she was transferred for further management. It is noteworthy that after her vaginal delivery five weeks prior, she required manual removal of the placenta. Her hematocrit was 20.8% at the time of discharge from the hospital. She had refused blood transfusion at that time and had not been taking her prescribed iron.

Upon admission, her medications included prenatal vitamins and ibuprofen. Her physical examination was largely unremarkable, with a small amount of dried blood on the perineum and a moderately stained pad. Her uterus was small, anteverted, mobile, and non-tender. The external os was approximately fingertip dilated, with no blood in the vault. 

Lab results showed a glucose level of 95, urea nitrogen of 10, creatinine of 0.8, sodium of 139, potassium of 3.9, and chloride of 110. Her total CO2 was 22 and anion gap was 11. Her white blood cell count was 9.2, red blood cell count was 2.46, hemoglobin was 6.9, hematocrit was 20.5, and platelet count was 369. Her neutrophils were 61.8, monocytes were 5.6, eosinophils were 4.0, and basophils were 0.5. Her PTT was 23.4. A transvaginal ultrasound showed a hyperechoic heterogeneous endometrium measuring up to 2.2cm with increased color flow, most compatible with retained products of conception.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a dilation and curettage (D&C) procedure to remove the retained products of conception. The surgery was uneventful and she recovered well, being discharged four hours later in stable condition. She was afebrile, had minimal vaginal bleeding, was able to eat regular food, had adequate pain control with oral medications, and was ambulating and urinating without difficulty. Her discharge diagnosis included retained products of conception and the D&C procedure.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Osteogenesis Imperfecta and over 200 fractures, ADHD, and tobacco use, was admitted due to shortness of breath. He reported intermittent mid-back and shoulder pain, which worsened with certain movements, particularly raising his arms over his head. He also reported difficulty taking deep breaths, which he attributed to muscle issues rather than pain. Initial vitals in the ED showed tachypnea, and physical examination revealed multiple deformities due to Osteogenesis Imperfecta, poor air movement in the lungs bilaterally, and tenderness in the mid-back. The patient was given 15mg IV toradol and IV morphine for pain, which he reported did not help, and levofloxacin 250mg IV for presumptive pneumonia.

Lab results on admission showed abnormal MCHC (30.8) and glucose (108), and PTT (49.5). A CT scan revealed a nonobstructive kidney stone. Imaging showed decreased aeration of the left lung, which could indicate atelectasis or pneumonia, and a dysmorphic skeleton producing restrictive chest wall physiology. A TTE suggested a bicuspid aortic valve, but the image quality was poor due to the patient's body habitus. 

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced respiratory distress and was unable to tolerate sitting up. He reported improved breathing when lying flat on his back. He was treated for community-acquired pneumonia with a 7-day course of levofloxacin. He also had an episode of Gauiac positive emesis and was found to have esophageal reflux. He was started on a PPI and his Hct remained stable over the last days of his hospital stay. The patient also complained of back pain, which could be due to a fracture, pneumonia, a kidney stone, or constipation. His pain was manageable but not gone at the time of discharge. He was discharged on lidocaine patch and tramadol. 

The patient also had a 2mm non-obstructing kidney stone and hematuria on his UA. He was started on IV fluids on admission and given flomax. He was discharged on home oxygen, nebulizer treatments, a bowel regimen of Miralax, senna, and colace, tramadol for pain control, flomax, a lidocaine patch, and omeprazole. 

The patient's family history does not include CAD. His medications upon admission included methylphenidate (Concerta) 36 mg q.a.m. His code status was changed during this admission to DNR/DNI.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Osteogenesis Imperfecta and dwarfism, was admitted to the hospital following a fall from his wheelchair. He has had over 200 fractures in the past and also has a history of ADHD and tobacco use. There is no family history of CAD. Upon admission, the patient complained of left arm and leg pain, a headache, and some sternal pain. He was not on any blood thinning medications. 

The physical examination revealed ecchymosis with abrasion in the right periorbital region, tenderness to palpation over the right anterior sternum and the midshaft forearm on the left, and a laceration to the right supraorbital region. The patient was in no acute distress and his speech was fluent. His strength and sensation were grossly intact in all extremities. 

The patient's blood work showed abnormal RBC counts of 4.39, 4.23, and 4.32, Hgb of 13.5, RDWSD of 53.1, 54.4, and 55.0, Neuts of 82.9, Lymphs of 8.0, Eos of 0.1, AbsNeut of 11.04, AbsLymp of 1.07, AbsMono of 1.08, AbsEos of 0.01, PTT of 43.9, Creat of 0.4 and 0.3, K of 5.4, and HCO3 of 17. 

CT scans revealed a complex fracture of the frontal bone, right lateral orbital wall and right orbital roof fracture, multiple severe hypodensities in the periventricular, subcortical, and deep white matter, pansinus mucosal thickening, mild anterolisthesis of C2 on C3, and a non-displaced fracture involving the mid shaft radius. X-rays showed a fracture involving the mandible and frontal bone fracture. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent external fixation of bilateral mandible fractures including left body fracture and para-symphyseal mandibular fracture via reconstruction plate and screws. Post-operative course was stable and the patient was started on clear liquids and advanced to full liquids. His vital signs were stable and he was afebrile. He was voiding without difficulty. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with left non-displaced midshaft radius fracture, multiple frontal bone fracture, right lateral, inf, sup orbital wall fracture, right orbital roof fracture, right sphenoid wing fracture, left mandibular body fracture acute, sphenoid bone, left middle cranial fossa, and right mandibular condyle fracture. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to get out of bed with assistance to chair or wheelchair. 

The patient was on Concerta and Vit D upon admission.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted following a fall from his wheelchair at home, which resulted in a mild traumatic brain injury (TBI). He was found by his caretaker 24 hours after the fall. He did not recall hitting his head and denied loss of consciousness. A CT scan of the torso revealed right rib fractures from the third to the sixth ribs, which were age-indeterminate. Further evaluation at the hospital showed a left parietal epidural hematoma.

The patient's past medical history includes an appendectomy, arachnoid cyst, attentional problem, falls with fractures, non-depressed skull fracture, osteogenesis imperfecta with over 200 fractures in the past, cerebellar hemisphere encephalomalacia, and ADHD. He also has a history of tobacco use. 

Upon admission, the patient was alert and oriented, with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 15. His physical examination was largely normal, with intact sensation to light touch in all four extremities. His pupils were equally round and reactive to light, and his extraocular movements were intact bilaterally without nystagmus. His facial strength and sensation were intact and symmetric, and his hearing was intact to voice. His motor examination was limited due to his inability to participate in a formal motor examination, but he was able to move all extremities antigravity to command.

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced hematemesis and was started on Pantaprazole. He also had guiac positive stools, but no interventions were recommended. His hemoglobin and hematocrit levels dropped, possibly due to the hematemesis and guiac positive stools, but trended up later. Upon discharge, his hemoglobin and hematocrit were 9.4 and 29.6, respectively. 

The patient's medication upon admission was Concerta 54mg PO daily. His discharge diagnoses included left parietal epidural hematoma, rib fractures, and anemia. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His activity status was out of bed with assistance to chair or wheelchair.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of osteogenesis imperfecta, dwarfism, and multiple falls, was admitted following a mechanical fall from his wheelchair. The fall resulted in bilateral rib fractures and a left femur fracture. The patient has a history of over 200 fractures due to his osteogenesis imperfecta. His past medical history also includes an appendectomy, arachnoid cyst, attentional problems, non-depressed skull fracture, and cerebellar hemisphere encephalomalacia. He also has a history of ADHD and tobacco use. There is no known family history of any conditions.

Upon admission, the patient appeared in mild discomfort. He was tachycardic and tachypneic with mild labored breathing, but no respiratory distress. His abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender. There was no hip tenderness bilaterally, and his pelvis was stable. However, there was tenderness in his left knee. 

CT scans revealed a fracture of the distal left femur, with evaluation limited due to severe osteopenia. There were also cortical defects and step-off in the right lateral mid ribs and left mid and lower ribs, indicative of acute non-displaced fractures. No pneumothorax, pulmonary contusion, or pelvic fracture was detected. Lab results showed a total CO2 level of 21* and an anion gap of 22*, both of which are abnormal. 

The patient was evaluated by the orthopedic service, who determined that he was not a surgical candidate due to diffuse osteopenia. Instead, they recommended ACE wrapping for bone healing, a method that has worked well for him in the past. His pain was much improved upon discharge, and he was otherwise stable. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Senna and Docusate for constipation, Vitamin D, and Methylphenidate 54mg daily. His discharge diagnosis was multiple rib fractures and left femur fracture. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. He was discharged home with service.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a right-handed female with no known previous medical history, was transferred from an outside hospital due to experiencing left-sided numbness. She reported a history of anxiety but no other known medical conditions. She had not seen a doctor for several years. Five days prior to admission, she developed pain in her left heel that radiated upwards, accompanied by a tingling sensation and a feeling of pins and needles from the heel up to the hip. This sensation persisted in her entire left leg. She denied any weakness or headaches. On the day of admission, she woke up with numbness and tingling in her entire left arm and face, but no weakness. She reported a brief, non-throbbing headache around noon. She denied any visual difficulty, hearing changes, difficulty speaking, language problems, memory difficulty, difficulty swallowing, vertigo, unsteady gait, weakness, or falls. She also denied any fever, weight loss, appetite changes, chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath, cough, wheeze, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, fecal incontinence, dysuria, nocturia, urinary incontinence, muscle or joint pain, hot/cold intolerance, polyuria, polydipsia, easy bruising, depression, anxiety, stress, or psychotic symptoms. She has no family history of strokes or migraines.

Upon physical examination, she was found to be lying in bed, awake and alert, with normal affect. Her temperature was 99.4, blood pressure 147/71, heart rate 86, and oxygen saturation 100%. Her neurological examination was normal, with no evidence of apraxia or neglect. She reported hyperesthesia to cold sensation and pinprick on the left arm only. Her reflexes were normal and her gait was narrow-based and steady. 

Her blood work showed abnormal RBC count of 4.09 and 4.19, Hgb of 11.4 and 11.9, and Hct of 34.2 and 35.2. Her MRI showed no evidence of stroke or flow-limiting stenosis. Her secondary stroke risk factors were assessed and her LDL and HbA1c were pending at the time of discharge. Her HbA1c was 5.5. 

During her hospital stay, her symptoms resolved and she had a normal exam at discharge. She was diagnosed with left-sided parasthesias with no found underlying organic etiology. She was discharged home with instructions to follow up with her primary care physician and to seek immediate medical attention if she experiences any sudden onset of weakness or numbness on one side of her body. She was not on any medications upon admission and no changes were made to her medications during her stay.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of COPD, fibromyalgia, recent C-spine fusion, active smoking, fibromyalgia, recent fall and fib fractures, and prior spontaneous pneumothoraces with thoracotomy and bleb removal, was admitted due to worsening shortness of breath, fatigue, difficulty swallowing, coughing after eating, and a 10 lb weight loss. He was found to have a hydropneumothorax on CT. A chest tube was placed to address this issue. 

His past medical history also includes chronic back pain, lumbosacral radiculopathy, lung and thyroid nodules, insomnia, BPH, and knee arthroscopy. His family history includes lung cancer in his grandfather, lymphoma in his mother, and pancreatic cancer in his uncle. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Acetaminophen, Albuterol Inhaler, DULoxetine, Finasteride, Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, Omeprazole, OxyCODONE, Pregabalin, Tamsulosin, Tiotropium Bromide, Zolpidem Tartrate, Diclofenac Sodium, and Nicotine Patch. 

Physical examination revealed diminished right lung sounds and wheezing. Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.86), HGB (10.1), HCT (32.8), MCHC (30.8), and GLUCOSE (101). Pleural fluid analysis also showed abnormal values for polys (55), lymphs (15), monos (18), basos (3), mesotheli (3), macrophag (3), and other (3). 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with suspected oropharyngeal dysphagia, which was most remarkable for observed intermittent signs and symptoms of aspiration with thin liquids. This was concerning for pharyngeal residue/weakness, possible UES dysfunction/obstruction, and/or laryngeal penetration/aspiration. He underwent a video swallow study which confirmed these findings. 

The patient was started on levofloxacin for aspiration pneumonia seen on chest x-ray during this admission. At the time of discharge, he was on a regular diet with nectar thick liquids. He was voiding appropriately and ambulating independently. His discharge diagnoses were hemopneumothorax, dysphagia, and aspiration pneumonia.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a past medical history of pneumonia, hydropneumothorax, COPD, fibromyalgia, recent C-spine fusion, active smoking, fibromyalgia, recent fall and fib fractures, and two prior spontaneous pneumothoraces with thoracotomy and bleb removal, presented with sudden onset shortness of breath. He reported that this started two nights ago after eating pizza and has had issues with certain foods. He also reported cough, fevers, chills, and weakness over the last day and is currently producing sputum. He denies any chest pain, nausea, vomiting, constipation, or diarrhea. He is an active smoker but desires to quit. 

In the ED, he initially required a non-rebreather and had a fever up to 100.7. His labs showed a lactate of 2.3 that improved to 1.6 with intervention, a mildly elevated WBC to 11.3, and mildly low Hgb to 10.7. His CXR showed bilateral lower lobe infiltrates, atelectasis, or aspiration. He received levofloxacin, duloxetine 60 mg, pregabalin 150 mg, finasteride 5 mg, oxycodone 15 mg, nicotine patch, x2, acetaminophen, and albuterol. 

His family history includes lung cancer in his grandfather, lymphoma in his mother, and pancreatic cancer in his uncle. His physical exam was generally normal, with decreased breath sounds at bases with crackles and skin breakdown and eccymosis on his left arm with open skin wounds. 

His hospital course included a 1.7 cm left thyroid nodule observed on CT neck, follow up with Speech and Swallow as an outpatient, and outstanding labs including strep pneumo urine antigen, MRSA swab, and blood cultures. He was given a prescription for Chantix for smoking cessation and his zolpidem was decreased to 5mg. 

His discharge diagnosis was aspiration pneumonia, with secondary diagnoses of COPD, fibromyalgia, BPH, and GERD. His discharge condition was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent. His discharge instructions included continuing to take all of his medications as directed, following up with all the appointments scheduled with his doctor, and using Chantix to help quit smoking.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a right-handed male, was admitted to the neurosurgery service for an elective posterior lami C3-4 and fusion C2-5 due to a history of cervical myelopathy. He has been experiencing left-sided neck pain radiating to the left shoulder, along with numbness and tingling in both fingers for several years. An MRI of his cervical spine revealed moderate multilevel degenerative changes, most severe at C3-4, with mild cord indentation and increased intracord signal intensity. His past medical history includes COPD, partial RUL lobectomy, two spontaneous pneumothoraxes, neuropathy, tobacco use, arthroscopy of bilateral knees, and shoulder surgery. He has no significant family history.

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, cooperative, and normocephalic. He had no dysarthria and his extremities were warm and well-perfused. There were no rashes or concerning lesions on his skin. He had kyphoscoliosis of the skull and spine. His muscle bulk was decreased in the intrinsic muscles of the feet. His upper and lower extremities showed varying degrees of strength, with some weakness noted. His reflexes were rated 3 at the biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally, 1+ at the right patella, 3 at the left patella, 1 at the right achilles, and 2 at the left achilles. Sensory examination revealed decreased appreciation of pinprick in a patchy distribution in the distal right lower extremity and in the entire left upper extremity. His coordination was intact, and his gait was quick with a narrow base, normal length stride, and symmetric arm swing, although he had trouble with toe walk.

Lab results showed an LD(LDH) level of 271, RBC count of 4.49, and HGB level of 13.6, all of which were abnormal. His WBC count was 10.6. The patient's medications upon admission included Albuterol Inhaler, CloniDINE, Doxepin HCl, Fluticasone Propionate (nasal and inhaler), Morphine SR, OxycoDONE, Pregabalin, Tamsulosin, Tiotropium Bromide, and Oxymetazoline.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a posterior lami C3-4 and fusion C2-5 procedure, which he tolerated well. Post-operatively, he demonstrated daily improvement in his neuro exam, with full strength except for left-sided upper extremity weakness. He had no clonus but did have left-sided paresthesia and no proprioception in his left hand, leading to numbness. His pain was well controlled, and his diet was slowly advanced. He was stable from a cardiovascular, hematological, and pulmonary standpoint, and his temperature was closely monitored for signs of infection. His wound dressing was changed twice daily due to leakage from a hematoma, but there was no erythema or induration. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), recent pneumonia, spinal surgery with chronic pain and left-sided sensory loss, presented with abdominal pain and swelling. He reported a bulge in his abdomen after a bout of severe coughing, which was followed by swelling and severe pain. He also mentioned a small bruise on his abdomen from his cane. He denied any fevers, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, or vomiting. He also reported not passing flatus or having a bowel movement since the pain began. 

His past medical history includes chronic back pain with high narcotic pain requirement, lumbosacral radiculopathy, peripheral neuropathy, cervical radiculitis, COPD, former smoker, allergic rhinitis, hip pain, osteoarthritis, lung nodules (multiple, stable), thyroid nodule (multiple, benign), fibromyalgia, prostatitis, hand pain, foot pain, orchitis, and insomnia. His family history includes a mother with lymphoma, a sister with coronary artery disease, an uncle with pancreatic cancer, a son with type 1 diabetes, and a maternal grandfather with lung cancer.

Upon physical examination, a bruise and a tender protrusion were noted on his left abdomen. His vitals were stable. A CT scan revealed a large intramuscular hematoma in the left rectus abdominus measuring approximately 7.9 x 5.3 x 11.4 cm with evidence of active bleeding. There was also mild loss of height in the multiple lower thoracic vertebral bodies likely chronic, secondary to degenerative disease, and patchy opacity in bilateral lung bases, right greater than left, likely representing aspiration and/or pneumonia.

During his hospital stay, his hematocrit was 37, INR 1.0, and WBC 12. Interventional radiology was consulted for possible embolization but recommended conservative management and monitoring given his clinical and laboratory stability. His hematocrit and hemoglobin levels were monitored closely over the following days and remained stable. His pain improved and his diet was advanced, which he tolerated without issue. On the day of discharge, his hematocrit was stable, and he was tolerating a regular diet and ambulating without difficulty.

His medications upon admission included albuterol sulfate, Ventolin HFA, Symbicort, Voltaren, finasteride, levofloxacin, lidocaine, nystatin, omeprazole, oxycodone, prednisone, Lyrica, tamsulosin, Spiriva with HandiHaler, zolpidem, and aspirin (which he had not taken in months). 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of hematoma of the rectus abdominus. He was tolerating a regular diet and ambulating at baseline. He was instructed to call his doctor or return to the Emergency Department for any new or worsening symptoms.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted for post laminectomy cervical kyphosis. He had previously undergone a posterior cervical decompression and fusion, which unfortunately resulted in a spinal cord injury and subsequent hemiplegia. He continues to experience left-sided weakness and dysfunction, as well as some right hand dysfunction from a neurological standpoint. His prior fusion appears to be healed, but he has significant post-laminectomy kyphosis perched at the caudal edge of his fusion. This is causing prominence of the spinous processes that are bothersome to him clinically. The kyphosis in the lower cervical and upper thoracic spine appears to be relatively rigid with significant degeneration of the C5-C6, C6-C7 and C7-T1 disks. MRI demonstrates myelomalacia with spinal cord contusions consistent with a spinal cord injury. 

The patient underwent an anterior cervical discectomy and fusion C5/6, C6/7, C7/T1. The surgery was without complication and the patient was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. Postoperative pain was controlled with oral and IV pain medication. Diet was advanced as tolerated. He was voiding independently. Physical therapy and Occupational therapy were consulted for mobilization OOB to ambulate and ADL's. 

The patient has a past medical history of chronic back pain with high narcotic pain requirement, lumbosacral radiculopathy, peripheral neuropathy, cervical radiculitis, COPD, former smoker, allergic rhinitis, hip pain, osteoarthritis, lung nodules, multiple, stable, thyroid nodule, multiple, benign, fibromyalgia, prostatitis, hand pain, foot pain, orchitis, and insomnia. His family history includes a mother with lymphoma, a sister with CAD, an uncle with pancreatic cancer, a son with diabetes type 1, and a maternal grandfather with lung cancer.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Albuterol Inhaler, Symbicort, DULoxetine, Finasteride, Omeprazole, Pregabalin, Tamsulosin, Tiotropium Bromide, Zolpidem Tartrate, and OxyCODONE. The patient has allergies to Penicillins, naproxen, Methadone, and Pneumococcal Vaccine.

Abnormal lab results include a Hgb of 13.0* and a calcHCT of 39. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of cervical kyphosis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The male patient was admitted to the orthopedics service with the chief complaint of post-laminectomy kyphosis. He had previously undergone a laminectomy and fusion, which was complicated by intraoperative spinal cord injury. Over time, many aspects of his neurological injury recovered, but he developed significant instability with post-laminectomy kyphosis. Despite a prolonged and multimodal course of conservative care, he did not experience significant or lasting relief. He elected to undergo a two-stage operation to improve his cervical, thoracic alignment and functional status. The major surgical procedure performed during this admission was a C2-T2 osteotomy fusion.

The patient's past medical history includes chronic back pain with high narcotic pain requirement, lumbosacral radiculopathy, peripheral neuropathy, cervical radiculitis, COPD, former smoker, allergic rhinitis, hip pain, osteoarthritis, lung nodules (multiple, stable), thyroid nodule (multiple, benign), fibromyalgia, prostatitis, hand pain, foot pain, orchitis, and insomnia. His family history includes a mother with lymphoma, a sister with CAD, an uncle with pancreatic cancer, a son with diabetes type 1, and a maternal grandfather with lung cancer.

Upon physical examination, the patient's sensory and motor functions were assessed. The patient's right upper extremity showed normal sensory and motor function, while the left upper extremity showed slightly reduced motor function.

Abnormal lab results included low RBC count (3.30*), low hemoglobin (9.6*), low hematocrit (30.3*), low MCHC (31.7*), and high RDWSD (48.3*). These abnormalities persisted in subsequent blood tests. Additionally, the patient's glucose level was elevated at 129* and anion gap was low at 9*.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent the aforementioned surgical procedure without complications. Postoperative pain was managed with oral and IV pain medication. The patient was non-compliant with bracing and dysphagia assessment recommendations. He was evaluated for dysphagia, but refused further investigation and accepted the risk of aspiration. He tolerated a regular diet without any episodes of hypoxia or aspiration.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Zolpidem Tartrate, Flovent, Albuterol Inh, Finasteride, Omeprazole, Oxycodone, Pregabalin, Floma, and Spireva. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of post-laminectomy kyphosis and cervical spinal cord injury from prior surgery. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), fibromyalgia, recent C-spine fusion, active smoking, and fibromyalgia. He presented with complaints of cough and shortness of breath, which began about 10 days after his neck surgery. A chest X-ray showed pneumonia, and he was treated with Levaquin. He was admitted to the medicine service after experiencing severe shortness of breath and was treated with supplemental oxygen and antibiotics. His home medications include Symbicort, Spiriva, and an albuterol HFA. He has not kept up to date with pneumonia vaccinations due to an adverse reaction to his last pneumococcal vaccine.

The patient's past medical history includes COPD, chronic back pain, lumbosacral radiculopathy, active smoking, lung nodules, thyroid nodules, fibromyalgia, insomnia, C-spine fusion, and Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH). His family history includes lung cancer in his grandfather, lymphoma in his mother, and pancreatic cancer in his uncle.

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam showed diminished air exchange bilaterally with left lower rhonchi and left-sided tactile fremitus. His discharge exam showed faint crackles at the bases, otherwise clear, no wheezing, and comfortable breathing. 

Abnormal lab results upon admission included elevated WBC, low RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCHC, and RDWSD, and abnormal Neuts, Lymphs, Monos, Eos, AbsNeut, and AbsEos. Discharge labs showed low RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCHC, RDWSD, and low Phos. 

Imaging showed new left lower lobe consolidation compatible with pneumonia. There was no evidence of deep venous thrombosis in the left lower extremity veins. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for pneumonia and possible COPD exacerbation. He was also treated with prednisone for possible component of Acute Exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD). His sputum culture grew strep pneumonia resistant to erythromycin. He was discharged home to complete a 5-day course of azithromycin and prednisone with close follow-up with his primary care physician. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Acetaminophen, Tiotropium Bromide, Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, OxyCODONE, Pregabalin, Omeprazole, Albuterol Inhaler, Finasteride, Voltaren, Tamsulosin, DULoxetine, and Zolpidem Tartrate. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of pneumonia, possible COPD exacerbation, and possible aspiration. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was advised to quit smoking and to follow up for a video swallow evaluation due to possible aspiration related to his recent neck surgery.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a history of osteogenesis, paraplegia, chronic constipation requiring disimpaction, and recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs).
