He presented with fevers, dysuria, and hematuria. He had been treated for a suspected UTI with cefpodoxime after a urine culture revealed E. coli sensitive to multiple cephalosporins. Despite taking the antibiotic, the patient reported ongoing fevers, dysuria, and hematuria. He reported no other localizing symptoms and was able to have bowel movements. 

His past medical history includes multiple surgeries and fusions due to osteogenesis, hydrocephalus with gait instability and urinary incontinence, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), depression, coronary artery disease (CAD) with a positive stress MIBI, constipation with past admissions for disempactions, cervical vertebral fracture in childhood with residual neuropathy, glaucoma, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, and recurrent UTIs. His mother died of a myocardial infarction.

Upon physical examination, the patient had a fever and was tachycardic. He had multiple bony deformities and a catheter in place draining amber urine. His lab results showed elevated white blood cell count (11.3), low red blood cell count (4.09), low hemoglobin (12.8), low hematocrit (37.5), high neutrophils (89.0), low lymphocytes (7.2), high glucose (129), and high potassium (5.4). His urine showed large amounts of blood and small amounts of leukocytes. A urine culture revealed over 100,000 organisms/ml of E. coli, which was resistant to ampicillin and ciprofloxacin but sensitive to several other antibiotics.

During his hospital stay, the patient was found to have Clostridium difficile colitis with associated diarrhea. His diarrhea and fevers abated with treatment with oral metronidazole. He required intravenous fluids and electrolyte repletions transiently. He also reported a history of depression with occasional suicidality, but denied current suicidal ideation. A psychiatry consultation recommended resumption of outpatient psychiatric care.

Upon admission, the patient was taking aspirin, metoprolol, protonix, trazodone, simvastatin, senna, colace, miralax, cosopt, travatan, and alphagan. His primary discharge diagnosis was Clostridium difficile colitis, with secondary diagnoses of osteogenesis and chronic depressive disorder. His condition was stable upon discharge.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a past medical history of Osteogenesis Imperfecta, Coronary Artery Disease (CAD), Dementia, severe hydrocephalus and aqueductal stenosis, Depression, Bipolar Disorder, Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD), Hypercholesterolemia, Hypertension, and recurrent Urinary Tract Infections (UTIs). He has had multiple surgeries and fusions due to Osteogenesis Imperfecta, including bilateral fusions, and is wheelchair-bound. He has also had a cervical vertebral fracture in childhood with residual neuropathy. He has refused a shunt for his hydrocephalus in the past. He has a history of constipation with previous admissions for disimpactions. His mother died of a myocardial infarction.

The patient was admitted due to sudden weakness in his right arm. He has a baseline spastic left hemiplegia from a cervical spine injury and both his legs are fused. He reported numbness and tingling in his right arm and leg, which worsened with movement or touch. There was no trauma or fall reported. His physical examination revealed a very dysmorphic and small stature, with long bone deformities and fixed legs. His neurological examination showed that he was awake, alert, and cooperative, but did not know the month. His right arm was initially not antigravity proximally but showed sustained antigravity at the Deltoid for a few seconds, which was weaker than his baseline.

Lab results showed an abnormal glucose level of 108, and abnormal red blood cell count (3.94), hemoglobin (12.6), hematocrit (37.8), and mean corpuscular hemoglobin (32.1). His white blood cell count dropped to 2.9 but stabilized at 3.9. A CT angiogram of the head and neck did not show any major vascular occlusions but showed a severe stenosis of the cervical spine to 1mm. An MRI confirmed the critical stenosis. The neurosurgical team recommended surgery, but the patient opted against it. He was placed in a soft collar as a hard collar was not possible due to his anatomy. He also had a febrile diarrheal illness/emesis upon presentation, which improved, and cultures were normal. His strength improved, although he continued to have weakness upon discharge. The etiology was thought to be worsening cervical stenosis or a recurrence of cervical symptoms in the setting of a gastrointestinal illness.

Upon admission, the patient was on Zyprexa 5mg HS, KCL 20mg QAM, Lamictal 25 QAM, Arricept 5mg daily, Nexium 40 daily, Cosopt Gtt BID, Senna, Colace, and ASA 325. He was discharged to rehab in a stable condition.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the neurosurgery service due to severe spinal stenosis. His past medical history is significant for osteogenesis imperfecta, cervical spine fracture, aqueductal stenosis, hydrocephalus, and dementia. He has undergone multiple surgeries and fusions for osteogenesis imperfecta and is wheelchair-bound. He has also refused a shunt for his hydrocephalus, which has resulted in gait instability and urinary incontinence. Other conditions include GERD, depression, CAD with a small reversible inferolateral defect, constipation with past admissions for disempactions, a cervical vertebral fracture from a childhood fall, glaucoma, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, and recurrent UTIs. 

The patient reported a month of gradually worsening right arm weakness, followed by an acute episode of right arm weakness earlier in the month. He was previously evaluated by our service and surgery was discussed, but he refused any intervention at that time. He was discharged to rehab and later convinced by his PCP to pursue surgery. He was admitted from his nursing home for possible pre-operative evaluation. However, upon presentation, he denied any progression of his right arm weakness or any other symptoms. 

The patient's physical exam upon presentation and discharge were largely similar, with the patient being alert, attentive, and cooperative. He was oriented to person, place, and year, and his sensation was intact to light touch, with right greater than left, which he stated was his baseline. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count at 3.78, Hgb at 11.9, Hct at 34.5, and glucose at 146. Creatinine was also low at 0.3. 

The patient was evaluated by a spine specialist for possible neurosurgical operative intervention. However, based on the patient's history, imaging, and physical exam, it was determined that operative intervention would not be offered at this time. The patient was discharged back to an extended care facility in stable condition. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Zyprexa, KCL, Lamictal, Cosopt, Senna, Colace, and ASA. His family history includes a mother who died of an MI at an undisclosed age, and a father with no medical problems.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to neck pain and arm paresthesias. He has a history of osteogenesis imperfecta, cervical spine fracture, aqueductal stenosis, hydrocephalus, and dementia. He requires assistance with a wheelchair and has a chronic spinal cord injury. He reported experiencing sharp pain in the back of his neck that radiated to both shoulders, along with numbness in his arms and weakness in his left arm. He was initially discharged from the emergency department (ED) after pain control, with a plan for an MRI as an outpatient. However, he returned to the ED later in the day with worsening pain and was admitted for pain control. A neurology review of the MRI revealed chronic cord compression, unchanged from previous.

His past medical history includes multiple surgeries and fusions due to osteogenesis imperfecta, hydrocephalus with gait instability and urinary incontinence (he has refused a shunt in the past), GERD, depression, CAD with a small reversible inferolateral defect, constipation with past admissions for disempactions, a cervical vertebral fracture in childhood with residual neuropathy, glaucoma, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, and recurrent UTIs. His mother died of a myocardial infarction.

The physical exam revealed a small male, awake, alert, and in no apparent distress. His vital signs were stable. Neurological examination showed diminished sensation to light touch in the left upper extremity and hyperreflexia in the left upper extremity. 

The MRI of the cervical spine showed severe kyphosis of the mid cervical spine, predominantly at C3-C4 level with extreme narrowing of the spinal canal and compression of the spinal cord. There was increased signal within the spinal cord at this level. There was also compression of C5 vertebra and markedly increased thoracic kyphosis. The spinal cord below C3-4 level was small in size, indicative of atrophic changes. There was low signal on T1- weighted images in C7 vertebra and on the inferior aspect of T2 vertebra, indicating marrow edema due to mild compressions. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's pain was initially controlled with morphine and then transitioned to oral medication (percocet). He was fitted with a soft cervical collar for routine use and a stiff plastic collar for higher risk activities. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Brimonidine, Donepezil, Dorzolamide-Timolol, Latanoprost, Pantoprazole, Quetiapine, Aspirin, Colace, and Senna. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of myelopathy, small cervical compression fracture, and osteogenesis imperfecta. His condition was stable at the time of discharge. He was instructed to use his soft cervical collar as tolerated to protect his neck during day to day activities and his plastic cervical collar during activities which require more movement and transfers.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of osteogenesis imperfecta, cervical spine fracture, aqueductal stenosis, hydrocephalus, dementia, and complete dependence on others for activities of daily living, was admitted due to dehydration and suspected neglect. He was brought in after calling his brother for help, expressing concerns about his caregiver's (identified as his "wife" or girlfriend) inadequate care and threats of harm. The patient reported not being fed or given enough to drink, and expressed feelings of depression. He also reported long-term constipation, infrequent stable bright red blood per rectum, a history of hemorrhoids, frequent nausea, and pain from a recently broken cervical vertebra. He is a current smoker with a >20 pack year smoking history. 

Upon examination, the patient was found to be soiled and foul-smelling, and was suspected to be dehydrated. His vital signs were 96.8 113/84 72 12 97%RA. His past medical history includes multiple surgeries and fusions due to osteogenesis imperfecta, hydrocephalus with gait instability and urinary incontinence, GERD, depression, CAD, constipation, cervical vertebral fracture in childhood, glaucoma, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, and recurrent UTIs. His mother died of a myocardial infarction. 

Physical examination revealed dry oral mucosa with oral thrush, tachycardia, bilateral wet crackles in the lungs, and contracted limbs with no edema. Lab results showed abnormal neutrophils at 47.6* and eosinophils at 7.5*. X-ray of the foot showed severe diffuse osteopenia and a gracile appearance to the bones, consistent with osteogenesis imperfecta, but no obvious acute fracture. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's dehydration was treated with oral fluids, and his nutrition improved with the recommendation of Ensure. Social services were consulted due to concerns of neglect and abuse. The patient was found to have two bruises on the right side between the clavicle and the nipple and one abrasion and one bruise on the left side below the clavicle. The patient was transferred to a long-term care facility for further care. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Brimonidine, Donepezil, Dorzolamide-Timolol, Latanoprost, Pantoprazole, Quetiapine, Aspirin, Colace, Senna, and Oxycodone-Acetaminophen. His primary diagnosis upon discharge was dehydration and neglect, with secondary diagnoses of osteogenesis imperfecta and depression. His condition was stable upon discharge.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to progressive right knee pain. His past medical history includes hypertension (HTN). He underwent a right total knee arthroplasty after conservative management failed to alleviate his symptoms. 

Upon discharge, his physical examination was generally normal with stable vital signs, alert and oriented mental status, clear bilateral lung sounds, regular heart rhythm, and a right lower extremity incision that was clean, dry, and intact. 

Several abnormal lab results were noted during his stay. His red blood cell (RBC) count was low at 4.40, 3.37, 3.35, and 3.60 on different occasions. His hemoglobin (Hgb) levels were also low at 13.9, 11.1, 10.8, and 11.4. His hematocrit (Hct) was low at 40.3, 30.9, 30.8, 30.9, and 32.6. His mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) was high at 33.0 and 32.3. His mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) was high at 35.4 and 35.9.

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced dyspnea on exertion and desaturations to the 80's when off of oxygen on post-operative day 3. A computed tomography angiogram (CTA) revealed a right lower lobe pulmonary embolism (PE). He was subsequently placed on coumadin with a therapeutic lovenox bridge. His hospital course was otherwise uneventful, with well-controlled pain, stable labs, and vital signs. 

Upon discharge, the patient was prescribed several medications including oxycodone for pain, pantoprazole, hexavitamin, senna for constipation, magnesium hydroxide, docusate sodium, nifedipine, hydrochlorothiazide, atenolol, atorvastatin, acetaminophen, enoxaparin, and warfarin. 

The patient was diagnosed with right knee osteoarthritis and a pulmonary embolism. He was discharged in stable condition to extended care. His discharge instructions included weight-bearing as tolerated on the right leg, keeping the incision clean and dry, and taking all medications as prescribed. He was also advised to seek medical attention if he noticed any increased redness, swelling, drainage, temperature >101.4, or difficulty breathing.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a past medical history of B cell CLL stage 0, chronic recurrent prostatitis, Coccidiomycosis, bilateral inguinal hernia repair, and bilateral shoulder surgery for impingement, was admitted due to fevers and chills. The symptoms began 6 days prior to admission and were accompanied by mild headache, photophobia, and eye pain. The patient also reported chronic dysuria and penile discharge. A bone marrow biopsy was performed during his stay. 

Upon admission, the patient's WBC was found to be low (~3500 per patient). An MRI of the brain showed no meningeal change. An echocardiogram showed very mild mitral regurgitation with no vegetation seen. A peripheral blood smear showed no PMNs, no RBC inclusions, lymphocytes with small granules along with slight atypia of lymphocytes and monocytes. A CXR showed a nodule in the left lung compatible with a previous lesion. Blood cultures, urine cultures, and EBV culture were all negative. The patient was started on imipenem for acute prostatitis. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with breast cancer and a father with lung cancer. The patient's allergies include ciprofloxacin and Ertapenem. Upon admission, the patient was taking NexIUM (esomeprazole magnesium) and CeleBREX (celecoxib) for pain.

Abnormal lab results include WBC 1.14, Hgb 13.2, HCT 39.2, platelets 98, PSA 10, fibrinogen 229, RBC-4.11*, Hgb-12.7*, Hct-36.9*, Atyps-3*, Metas-1*, Glucose-124*, ALT-67*, AST-102*, LD(LDH)-270*, LD(___)-282*, LD(LDH)-286*, b2micro-4.0*, IgG-563*, and IgA-50*. 

The patient's febrile neutropenia resolved during his stay. His bone marrow biopsy showed L shift, approx 20% involvement of CLL/SLL. The patient also had transaminitis, untreated history of pulmonary involvement, chronic prostatitis with likely acute flare, and chronic shoulder pain. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of Chronic lymphocytic leukemia.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male with a past medical history of B cell CLL stage 0, chronic recurrent prostatitis, coccidiomycosis, bilateral inguinal hernia repair, and bilateral shoulder surgery for impingement. He was recently hospitalized for neutropenic fever and prostatitis, and was discharged on ertapenem. He presented with abdominal pain, hoarse voice, sore throat, prolonged somnolence, weakness, and occasional confusion. His physical exam was notable for perianal erythema and mild tenderness over the right upper quadrant of the abdomen. 

His lab results showed normal CBC, CHEM7, and lactate. However, his glucose was elevated at 119, and his ALT, AST, and LD(LDH) were also elevated at 147, 152, and 263 respectively. His CK(CPK) was also elevated at 26. His urine analysis was unremarkable. 

The patient's abdominal discomfort and LFT abnormalities were attributed to mild drug-induced liver injury secondary to recent exposure to carbapenem/ertapenem. His symptoms and AST/ALT levels improved overnight after holding ertapenem. He also had symptoms consistent with a prostatitis flare, although his physical examination and urine analysis did not show striking evidence of inflammation. His perianal erythema and sore throat were thought to be fungal in nature due to recent broad-spectrum antibiotics. He was started on Nystatin swish and swallow and miconazole powder to the perianal area with improvement in symptoms. His neutropenia has resolved, and the etiology is likely viral given the clinical course and normal bone marrow. HSV6 is a potential cause, but the PCR is pending at the time of discharge.

His medications upon admission included Nexium for GERD, Lorazepam for nausea, Nystatin Cream for fungal dermatitis, Oxycodone for pain, Ertapenem for prostatitis, and Celebrex for pain. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with breast cancer and a father with lung cancer. He was discharged with a primary diagnosis of transaminitis and abdominal pain, and a secondary diagnosis of chronic prostatitis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of pancreatitis due to severe hypertriglyceridemia, presented with epigastric pain radiating to her shoulders and back, accompanied by increased nausea, vomiting, and bloating. She was initially diagnosed with pancreatitis and hepatitis, with lab results showing lipase at 3500 and AST at 700. A CT scan revealed worsening pancreatitis with necrosis of the pancreas body/tail, and an MRCP showed narrowing of the distal common bile duct. Despite being made NPO, placed on aztreonam, and given IVF rehydration, her condition did not improve. She developed a fever, her WBC count dropped from 7 to 2.4, and her H&H and platelet counts also decreased. She was in moderate DKA with blood glucose in the 400s, which normalized after being put on an insulin drip. Aztreonam was stopped due to concerns of causing pancytopenia, and subcutaneous heparin was discontinued due to concerns of HIT. 

Her past medical history includes type 1 diabetes, hypothyroidism, GERD, lupus, depression, portal hypertension, pancytopenia, elevated triglycerides, and chronic pancreatitis with a history of necrotizing pancreatitis from high triglycerides (TGs > 5000). She has no known family history of any conditions. 

Upon physical examination, she was found to be alert and oriented, with no scleral icterus, moist mucous membranes, unlabored breathing, and a slightly distended and tender abdomen. 

Lab results showed several abnormalities: PO2-41*, UREA N-5*, CREAT-0.3*, SODIUM-133, POTASSIUM-5.6*, TOTAL CO2-21*, ALT(SGPT)-284*, AST(SGOT)-343*, ALK PHOS-345*, TOT BILI-3.2*, DIR BILI-0.9*, INDIR BIL-2.3, LIPASE-183*, ALBUMIN-2.9*, CALCIUM-7.8*, PHOSPHATE-1.6*, TRIGLYCER-2259*, WBC-2.9*, RBC-2.81*, HGB-7.6*, HCT-23.5*, MCH-37.3*, and PLT COUNT-54*. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated with a dilaudid PCA, which was increased for better pain control and then transitioned to IV dilaudid PRN and then to PO Dilaudid. She was initially NPO for bowel rest due to her acute pancreatitis, but was transitioned to clears and then to a regular low fat diet, which she tolerated well. Her respiratory status was monitored, and she was encouraged to use pulmonary toilet and IS. She was found to have a urinary tract infection and was discharged on Bactrim for three days. Her hemodynamics were stable, but she experienced pancytopenia and was evaluated by the heme-onc. Her sugars and triglyceride levels were significantly elevated, and she was evaluated by specialists who gave recommendations for insulin sliding scale adjustments. 

Upon discharge, her condition was stable, she was ambulating and voiding independently, and tolerating a regular low fat diet. She was diagnosed with pancreatitis.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the Orthopaedic service following a motorcycle accident near his home. He sustained an open fracture to his right tibia and fibula, which was confirmed by a portable x-ray at an outside hospital. He was unable to walk and was transferred to our facility via EMS. Prior to transfer, he was given 2 grams of Ancef. He has no known allergies except to Ceclor. 

Upon admission, his vital signs were stable and he was alert but somnolent. He had a 3 cm laceration on the anteromedial aspect of his mid tibia that probed to the bone and a 1 cm laceration on the proximal anterolateral tibia that did not probe to bone. He had decreased ankle movement due to pain but was able to move all toes and had intact sensation. His compartments were soft. 

His blood work showed a low hematocrit of 23.8, low RBC count of 3.27, low hemoglobin of 7.4, low MCV of 70, low MCH of 22.7, and high RDW of 18.8. 

He underwent irrigation and debridement (I&D) and open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of his right tibia and fibula. Postoperatively, his pain was managed with a PCA and he was transitioned to oral pain medications as tolerated. He was given perioperative antibiotics and Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. His diet was advanced without complications and he made steady progress with physical therapy. 

Upon discharge, his vital signs were stable, he was afebrile, tolerating a regular diet, voiding spontaneously, and his pain was well controlled. His extremities were neurovascularly intact distally and all incisions were clean, dry, and intact without evidence of infection, hematoma, or seroma. 

He was discharged home in stable condition with instructions to follow up with his primary care physician and orthopaedic surgeon. He was advised to return to the emergency department or notify his doctor if he experienced severe pain not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers >101.5, shaking chills, redness or drainage at the incision site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns. He was also given detailed instructions on wound care and medication management. 

The patient has no known past medical history or family history of note. He was not on any medications upon admission.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the Orthopaedic Trauma Service for repair of a nonunion in his right tibia and fibula. This condition was a result of a previous open tib-fib fracture that was treated with an IM nail. Despite the treatment, the patient experienced persistent pain at the fracture site and along his fibula. The decision was made to proceed with operative repair of the nonunion due to these concerns and persistent symptoms with no signs of healing. 

The patient underwent a major surgical procedure which included the repair of the right tibia nonunion with an iliac crest autograft and an open reduction and internal fixation/repair of the right fibula nonunion with an autograft. The procedure was uncomplicated and the patient was transferred to the PACU in stable condition. Post-operatively, the patient's pain was managed with a PCA and later transitioned to oral pain medications once he was able to tolerate oral intake. The patient's diet was advanced without difficulty and he made steady progress.

The patient's lab results showed several abnormal findings. His WBC count was elevated at 14.8. His Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, and RDW were all abnormal at 11.3, 35.4, 67, 21.4, and 19.4 respectively. His glucose level was also elevated at 107. His Na and Cl levels were abnormal at 131 and 91 respectively. 

The patient was put on partial weight bearing status for his right lower extremity. He received unspecified antibiotics and Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. The incision sites for both the right lower extremity and the iliac crest autograft were clean, dry, and intact with no signs of erythema or drainage. The patient was discharged in stable condition with instructions for follow-up care. 

The patient had no medications upon admission. He has allergies to Ceclor and Ancef. His family history is not specified. His past medical history is also not specified. His physical exam showed no abnormalities. His discharge diagnosis was a nonunion of the right tibia and fibula.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a past medical history of hypertension and glaucoma. She was admitted due to multiple pelvic fractures sustained from a mechanical fall at home. She reported severe right pelvis pain immediately after the fall but denied any head strike or loss of consciousness. Initial vital signs were stable and labs showed a white blood cell count of 15.5 and hemoglobin/hematocrit of 9.6/32.4. A plain film pelvis showed non-displaced fractures of inferior pubic rami, left superior pubic ramus, and a minimally displaced fracture of the right sacrum. The orthopedics team recommended pain control, weight-bearing as tolerated, and a follow-up in the Orthopedics Clinic in a week. The patient was admitted to Medicine for pain control.

During her stay, the patient denied any pain and reported that morphine worked well for her. She denied any fevers, chills, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, and urinary symptoms. Her admission and discharge physical exams were largely unremarkable. 

Lab results showed elevated WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, Neuts, Lymphs, Glucose, UreaN, and Iron. There was also a decrease in calTIBC and Ferritn. Imaging showed non-displaced fractures of the inferior pubic rami bilaterally, non-displaced fracture of the left superior pubic ramus, minimally displaced fracture of the right sacrum, and a small amount of blood along the pelvic sidewalls, anterior to the bladder, and in the presacral space. There was also thickening of the cecum and adjacent lymphadenopathy, which is concerning for malignancy.

The patient's leukocytosis was resolving, likely due to the pelvic fracture. Her anemia was unclear, but there was concern for acute blood loss anemia due to her multiple pelvic fractures or iron-deficiency anemia from probable colon cancer. Her hypertension was managed with home lisinopril and amiloride, and her glaucoma with home timolol and latanoprost. 

The patient was discharged to an extended care facility for rehabilitation. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive but required assistance or aid for ambulation. Her medications upon admission were Amiloride HCl 5 mg, Lisinopril 20 mg, Latanoprost 0.005% Ophth. Soln. 1 DROP BOTH EYES HS, and Timolol Maleate 0.5% 1 DROP BOTH EYES DAILY.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the Vascular Surgical Service for evaluation of a restenotic left SFA stent. His medical history includes hypertension, type II diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, a positive PPD with a normal chest x-ray in the past that was not treated, alcohol abuse, and peripheral vascular disease of the lower extremities. He had previously undergone left SFA angioplasty and stenting. His family history includes a father with hypertension and diabetes, but no history of neurologic disease, including stroke.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including amlodipine, plavix, gabapentin, glipizide, hydralazine, HCTZ, novolog, labetalol, lisinopril, metformin, mirtazapine, oxycodone, protonix, KCL, flomax, tylenol, aspirin, vit B12, colace, folate, and MVI.

During his stay, the patient underwent LLE laser in-stent SFA stenosis & peroneal artery and PTA of native SFA, SFA stent stenosis and peroneal, which went well without complication. Post-operation, the patient was hemodynamically stable. His groin was inspected frequently for signs of a hematoma. He was given hydralazine for hypertension. Routine post-op labs revealed a high K level of 6.1, which was managed with calcium gluconate and insulin with dextrose, bringing his K level down to 5.2.

Other abnormal lab results included Hct-22.1, RBC-2.63, Hgb-7.9, Hct-25.9, MCV-99, MCHC-30.5, RDW-17.1, Neuts-73.1, UreaN-31, and UreaN-32. 

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. He was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled. His discharge diagnosis was peripheral vascular disease.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a right-handed male with a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes, cervical spondylosis with myelopathy, peripheral vascular disease, and alcohol abuse, was admitted due to progressively worsening weakness and numbness over the last 2 weeks. He had previously experienced difficulty standing about a year ago, which was around the time he was hospitalized for vascular disease of the lower extremities. He underwent left SFA angioplasty and stenting at that time. His weakness was most pronounced in the distal lower extremities, but he was able to walk independently. The weakness persisted for about 3 weeks after the hospitalization, but gradually resolved and his strength returned to baseline. 

Eight months ago, he began experiencing numbness in his feet that gradually progressed upwards to his hips bilaterally. Six months ago, he was admitted to the hospital for hypertension and back pain. He underwent a lumbar puncture, but the nature of the back pain and the results of the lumbar puncture are unclear. After discharge, he was unable to stand for prolonged periods and could only walk short distances with the help of a cane. His daughter reported that his strength and ability to stand and walk improved somewhat over the next five months, but he never returned to his baseline. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Lisinopril, Gabapentin, Plavix, HCTZ, Amlodipine, Toprol XL, and Metformin. His physical exam revealed a posterior cervical spine incision without erythema or drainage, and staples in place. He had left shoulder pain with PROM and decreased AROM in the left shoulder compared to the right. He also had some weakness in his right leg and difficulty gripping items in his hands. 

Lab results showed several abnormal findings including an ESR of 83, %HbA1c of 6.5, CRP of 14.0, calTIBC of 224, TRF of 172, RBC of 3.62, Hgb of 10.8, Hct of 30.9, and Glucose of 153. An MRI of his cervical spine showed severe stenosis with cord signal changes and evidence of cord compression. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent a Posterior C4-6 laminectomies, C3-7 PISF procedure. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the ortho spine service and medicine was consulted for management of his hypertension. He was started on Hydralazine PO in addition to Hydralazine IV for breakthrough with moderate control of his pressures. He was then transitioned to PO Labetolol for improved control with SBP into the 160s. His hospital course was otherwise unremarkable and he was discharged in a stable condition. His discharge diagnosis was cervical myelopathy. 

The patient's father had hypertension and diabetes, but there is no family history of neurologic disease, including stroke.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, type II diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, positive PPD with a normal chest x-ray in the past (not treated), alcohol abuse, and peripheral vascular disease of the lower extremities, was admitted due to bilateral heel ulcers. He had previously undergone left SFA angioplasty and stenting. The patient has been experiencing progressive lower and upper extremity weakness, which led to an MRI revealing cord compression. This resulted in cervical laminectomies involving C4 through C6 and fusion of the same levels. Post-operation, the patient has been unable to regain the ability to walk and can only stand for five minutes at a time. His right upper strength has returned to normal, but his left upper extremity remains weak. 

The patient's prolonged recovery and preoperative weakness have led to the development of bilateral heel decubiti. He has no symptoms of rest pain and no signs of local or systemic infection. An arteriogram was carried out to evaluate the arterial system of his lower extremities. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an arteriogram of the right lower extremity, angioplasty of the right SFA, right anterior tibial artery, and peroneal artery balloon angioplasty. He remained hemodynamically stable throughout the procedure and was transferred to the PACU for further stabilization and monitoring. 

Lab results showed an elevated UreaN level at 23* and a low Hct level at 27.6*. The patient's medications upon admission included Gabapentin, Hydralazine, Hydrochlorothiazide, Labetalol, Lisinopril, Mirtazapine, Oxycodone, Pantoprazole, Potassium Chloride, Tamsulosin, Folic Acid, Multivitamin, Senna, and Thiamine. 

The patient's family history includes a father with hypertension and diabetes, but no known history of neurologic disease, including stroke. Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with peripheral vascular disease, hypertension, DMII, and dyslipidemia. He was discharged to a rehabilitation facility in stable condition.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of meningioma resection, transient ischemic attack, seizures, hypertension, hypercholesteremia, hypothyroidism, right spermatocele, congenital toe webbing, eye surgery, bilateral carpal tunnel disease, and a left knee replacement, was admitted for neurosurgical evaluation after presenting with subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) found on a CT scan. The patient's wife reported that he was acting normally in the morning but was unable to speak when she called him later in the day. He was transferred to the hospital for further evaluation. Neurosurgery determined that an intervention would not be helpful, and his CT imaging showed appropriate evolution of the bleed.

Upon admission, the patient was found to have expressive aphasia and dysarthria. His physical exam was generally unremarkable, with normal vital signs and no apparent distress. However, his mental status exam was limited due to his aphasia. 

Lab results showed several abnormalities, including elevated white blood cell count (16.5*), low red blood cell count (3.75*), low hemoglobin (11.9*), low hematocrit (33.3*), elevated mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (35.9*), elevated glucose (128*), elevated urea nitrogen (24*), low calcium (7.9*), and elevated phenytoin (26.4*). 

Imaging studies revealed extensive areas of intraparenchymal, subarachnoid, and subdural hemorrhages, mild mass effect on the left lateral ventricle, and mild shift of the midline structures to the right side. There was no evidence of aneurysm, but significant atherosclerotic disease was noted in the intracranial arteries. The patient's echocardiogram showed normal global left ventricular function with no vegetation identified. 

During his hospital stay, the patient developed a fever and leukocytosis, which were treated with Ciprofloxacin for a urinary tract infection. Despite extensive work-up, the cause of the fever and leukocytosis was not identified. The patient was also found to have transaminitis of unclear etiology, hyponatremia, and thyroid nodules. His medications upon admission included Aggrenox, Lipitor, HCTZ, Enalapril, Finasteride, Synthroid, Trileptal, and Dilantin. 

The patient was discharged to extended care with a primary diagnosis of bilateral subarachnoid hemorrhage, left subdural hematoma, and fever, and a secondary diagnosis of seizure disorder and hypertension. He was neurologically and hemodynamically stable at the time of discharge.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a right-handed male, was admitted to the neurology service with a chief complaint of code stroke. He has a past medical history of left-sided hemorrhage while on Aggrenox, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a seizure disorder following tumor removal. He also has a history of transient ischemic attack (TIA), hypothyroidism, right spermatocele, congenital toe webbing, eye surgery, bilateral carpal tunnel disease, and left knee replacement. The patient's family history is non-contributory.

On the day of admission, the patient was found unable to speak while in the bathroom. His wife reported that he had no signs of convulsion or tongue biting. The patient had a similar episode in the past where he suddenly lost his ability to speak without focal weakness and was found to have multiple intracranial hemorrhages (ICH) including left temporal lobe, inferior frontal lobes, and superior right frontal lobe plus diffuse subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) and subdural hemorrhage (SDH). He was evaluated for underlying vascular malformation which was negative. The patient also has a history of seizures following left fronto-temporal meningioma removal and is maintained on medication. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake and alert but showed poor comprehension. He was able to read some phrases but was unable to name any objects. His cranial nerves were intact, and he showed normal bulk and tone bilaterally. Sensation was intact to light touch and pinprick. Reflexes were symmetric throughout. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for white blood cell count (11.7*), red blood cell count (4.29*), hemoglobin (12.3*), and hematocrit (36.2*). MRI/MRA showed signal abnormality in the posterior superior parietal lobes, extensive encephalomalacia in the anterior/inferior frontal and anterior temporal lobes, small chronic infarctions in the left corona radiata and inferior right cerebellar hemisphere, and extensive atherosclerosis in the anterior circulation. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was evaluated for possible acute stroke vs seizure. His MRI and MRA did not show acute infarction, but there was some area of posterior superior parietal lobes in a symmetric signal abnormality seen on DWI. His vessel imaging also showed multiple areas of atherosclerotic disease. His symptoms were not felt to be due to stroke but were likely due to a seizure and post-ictal state. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Enalapril 10 BID, Amlodipine 2.5 daily, Levothyroxine 112mcg daily, Lipitor 20 daily, and a multivitamin. His discharge diagnosis included seizure, history of meningioma, and history of OCH/SAH/SDH. His condition at discharge was stable, with limited speech and difficulty with 2 step commands.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a right-handed male, was admitted to the neurology service due to an episode of speech arrest and right-sided droop. His medical history includes a left-sided hemorrhage while on Aggrenox, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a seizure disorder following tumor removal. He also had a recent seizure with speech arrest. The patient has a history of a left fronto-temporal meningioma, which was removed, and he experienced a seizure afterwards. He has been maintained on Trileptal for his seizure disorder. He also has a history of a CNS bleed, which resulted in multiple intracranial hemorrhages. Other past medical conditions include transient ischemic attack (TIA), hypertension, hypercholesteremia, hypothyroidism, right spermatocele, congenital toe webbing, eye surgery, and carpal tunnel disease. He has undergone a left knee replacement and meningioma resection. His family history is non-contributory.

During his admission, the patient was alert with no signs of meningismus or carotid bruits. His neurological examination showed speech arrest, but he was able to mobilize all four limbs symmetrically. His deep tendon reflexes were normal and he showed no signs of dysmetria or dysdiadochokinesia. 

Lab results showed elevated levels of white blood cells (WBC-11.8*), red blood cells (RBC-4.46*), hemoglobin (Hgb-13.0*), hematocrit (Hct-37.0*), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC-35.1*), glucose (Glucose-147*), urea nitrogen (UreaN-21* and 27*), and lactate (Lactate-2.3*). 

Imaging and EEG results showed no acute intracranial process but stable multifocal areas of encephalomalacia. EEGs showed rare isolated left anterior temporal sharps and sharp and slow wave complexes, as well as persistent focal left anterior temporal slowing. The overall background was also mildly encephalopathic. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was initially worked up for stroke due to the sudden onset of speech arrest. However, he rapidly improved and was diagnosed with a seizure episode. He was admitted to neurology general service with long-term monitoring for his seizures. His usual dose of Trileptal was increased to 750 mg BID with close monitoring of his sodium and white cell count. His neurological status rapidly improved to his baseline. 

Upon admission, the patient was on OCX 600 bid, LT4 112 mcg qd, Enalapril 10 bid, Amlodipine 2.5 mg qd, Lipitor 20 qhs, MVI, and calcium citrate.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the hospital following a seizure episode. His medical history includes left-sided hemorrhage while on aggrenox, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a seizure disorder that developed after a tumor removal. He also has a history of strokes, grand mal seizures, meningioma post-removal, intracranial hemorrhage, hypertension, hypercholesteremia, hypothyroidism, right spermatocele, congenital toe webbing, eye surgery, bilateral, and carpal tunnel disease. He had a knee replacement on his left knee. 

The patient's wife noticed a sudden seizure lasting for a few minutes, which was similar to his typical seizure episodes that started after his stroke. He was brought to the emergency department (ED) for seizure-like activity and right-sided weakness. In the ED, his vitals were T 97.8 R 81 BP 115/69 RR 18 95 % RA. He showed dramatic improvement and was close to his baseline on admission. He was unsteady on his feet and required physical therapy assessment and potential placement. 

Upon admission, the patient was on enalapril 10 mg PO BID, amlodipine 2.5 mg PO qD, oxcarbazepine 600 PO BID, Synthroid, Ca+VitD, Multivitamins, Atorvastatin 20 mg PO qD, and Levothyroxine 125 mcg Po qD. 

The patient's lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC-11.6*, MCHC-35.5*, Glucose-125*, and UreaN-29*. A head CT scan showed no acute intracranial process but revealed multiple areas of encephalomalacia in the bifrontal and temporal lobes. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was evaluated by neurology for a typical seizure and showed rapid improvement. CT head was not concerning for an acute process. Infectious work-up did not reveal any concerning etiologies. The episodes were likely small breakthrough seizures. He also had microscopic hematuria, likely due to foley trauma, which resolved by discharge. His hypertension was managed with enalapril 10 mg PO BID, amlodipine 2.5 mg PO qD, and his hypothyroidism was managed with levothyroxine 125 mcg PO qD. His hyperlipidemia was managed with lipitor 20 mg Po qD. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a seizure, history of stroke, hypertension, and hyperthyroidism. His mental status was sometimes confused, but he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of quadriplegia, hypothyroidism, gastroparesis, and low testosterone levels, was admitted due to concerns of osteomyelitis of the sacrum and coccyx. This was suggested by an MRI scan and was thought to be secondary to a chronic sacral decubitus ulcer. The patient had a wound culture that tested positive for MRSA and an elevated sed rate of 72. He had been on Bactrim for 10 days, which was completed two weeks prior to admission. He reported a reduced appetite and weight loss over the last two weeks. 

A bone biopsy was performed during his stay. The MRI showed enhancing marrow edema involving the S3-S4 segments of the sacrum and the coccyx with posterior cortical disruption. There was also non-mass-like enhancing edema in the subcutaneous soft tissues and gluteal muscles, which was compatible with the patient's known chronic coccygeal decubitus ulcer with chronic infection. 

Initial labs were remarkable for ESR 2, CRP: 1.5, lactate 0.9, Cr 0.4, K 3.9, WBC 6. The patient was given 1 gram IV vancomycin and Zosyn IV 4.5 g. Blood and urine cultures were sent. The sacral decubitus ulcer was probed to the bone. 

The patient's physical exam was generally unremarkable, with the exception of a sacral decubitus clean looking ulcer with a ~4mm sinus tract that tracks ~2.5cm deep. 

Abnormal lab results included RBC-4.48*#, Hgb-13.2*#, Hct-40.5#, RBC-4.25*, Hgb-12.8*, Hct-38.4*, and Glucose-103*. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Methadone, ClonazePAM, amphetamine-dextroamphetamine, Propantheline Bromide, Diazepam, Collagenase Ointment, Testim, Docusate Sodium, Bisacodyl, and OxycoDONE. 

The patient's discharge diagnosis was osteomyelitis, with secondary diagnoses of quadriplegia and chronic pain. The patient was discharged home with service.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of paraplegia due to a broken neck, neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent catheterization, recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs), hypothyroidism, gastroparesis, and low testosterone levels, presented with lower abdominal pain. He reported several days of cloudy and foul-smelling urine, poor appetite, and general malaise. He had a recent episode of sore throat treated with oral augmentin. His initial vitals were stable and labs showed a white blood cell count of 7.1, normal electrolytes, and urinalysis with large leukocytes, bacteria, and 39 white blood cells. No imaging was performed. He was given ceftriaxone and 1L normal saline. Upon arrival to the floor, he reported feeling significantly better with no fever, chills, nausea, or flank pain.

The patient's urine culture showed Enterobacter cloacae complex >100,000 cfu/mL, which was resistant to several antibiotics including amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, cefazolin, cefoxitin, cefpodoxime, cefuroxime (oral and IV), ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, nitrofurantoin, and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole. It was sensitive to ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, gentamicin, tetracycline, and tobramycin. Admission labs showed a glucose level of 129, and urinalysis showed urobilinogen of 2, RBC of 20, and WBC of 39. Discharge labs showed a decrease in RBC to 4.04, Hgb to 12.1, Hct to 38.0, and MCHC to 31.8.

The patient was diagnosed with a UTI and started on ceftriaxone based on the urine culture. He declined a PICC line and opted to present to the clinic for daily infusions. He also experienced hypotension, which was attributed to autonomic dysfunction from his spinal cord injury. He had mild transaminitis of unclear cause. His chronic issues included chronic pain, for which he was taking diazepam, oxycodone, and methadone, neurogenic bladder, for which he was taking propantheline bromide, and sleep disorder, for which he was taking mirtazapine and amphetamine-dextroamphetamine. He was also on testosterone, methenamine hippurate, amoxicillin-clavulanic acid, vitamin D, ascorbic acid, and fluticasone propionate nasal spray. Upon discharge, he was prescribed the same medications, with the addition of ceftriaxone. His discharge diagnoses included a UTI, paraplegia, neurogenic bladder, and chronic pain.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of paraplegia due to a C6 injury, gastroparesis, neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent catheterization, recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs), chronic pain syndrome, polysubstance use disorder, dysthymic disorder, osteomyelitis, hyperlipidemia, peptic ulcer disease, vitamin D deficiency, and testicular hypofunction, presented to the emergency department (ED) with complaints of dysuria and fatigue. He reported fevers, malaise, and urine growing multi-drug resistant organisms. He had been previously treated with Augmentin for a UTI, but his symptoms did not improve, leading to a switch to ciprofloxacin. However, he only took three doses due to joint pain. His urine culture grew Klebsiella pneumoniae and Providencia rettgeri, which were resistant to multiple drugs but sensitive to ceftazidime. 

Upon admission, his vital signs were stable, and his labs showed a white blood cell count of 7.9, hemoglobin of 14.8, platelet count of 210, sodium of 137, potassium of 5.9, chloride of 102, bicarbonate of 19, BUN of 11, creatinine of 0.6, and anion gap of 16. His urine studies showed large leukocytes, small blood, negative nitrites, 4 red blood cells, more than 182 white blood cells, few bacteria, and 1 epithelial cell. He was given IV ceftazidime 2g, PO clonazepam 0.5mg, and oxycodone 15mg. 

The patient's father has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, and his mother has a history of cancer. His physical exam upon admission was unremarkable, and his discharge physical exam showed stable vital signs and no significant findings. 

His admission labs showed neutrophilia, lymphopenia, and eosinopenia. His potassium and bicarbonate levels were elevated. His urine studies showed the presence of blood, large leukocytes, and elevated red and white blood cell counts. His lactate level was initially elevated but normalized subsequently. His discharge labs showed a decrease in red blood cell count, hemoglobin, and hematocrit. His urine culture showed growth of Providencia rettgeri and Escherichia coli, both of which were sensitive to ertapenem. His blood cultures were negative for growth, except for one that grew coagulase-negative Staphylococcus, which was considered a contaminant. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was treated for a complicated UTI, hypotension likely due to sepsis and autonomic dysfunction, and dilutional anemia. His chronic pain syndrome was managed, and he was educated on checking his urine pH more frequently to ensure the effectiveness of his home methenamine. He was discharged home with instructions to continue his medications, attend follow-up appointments, and check his urine pH weekly. His discharge medications included ceftazidime, ascorbic acid, amphetamine-dextroamphetamine, bisacodyl, clonazepam, docusate sodium, fluticasone propionate, methadone, methenamine hippurate, mirtazapine, oxycodone, testosterone gel, and vitamin D. He was also prescribed surgical lubricant packets and urine dipstick test strips for home use.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the orthopaedics department due to pain in his right leg and left ankle. He had been involved in a motor vehicle collision, which resulted in immediate pain in his right and left lower extremities and an inability to walk. He denied loss of consciousness and premonitory symptoms. His review of systems was otherwise at baseline. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His past medical history is unremarkable and his family history is non-contributory.

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, and not in acute distress. His right lower extremity was wrapped and in an air cast boot. His vital signs were within normal limits.

The patient underwent several surgical procedures including intramedullary nailing for a right tibial shaft fracture, open reduction and internal fixation for a medial malleolar fracture, examination under anesthesia with external rotation stress, and closed treatment with manipulation for a fibular shaft fracture. 

Imaging of the shoulder showed no fractures, dislocations, degenerative changes, lytic or sclerotic bone lesions, radiopaque foreign bodies, or soft tissue calcifications. The visualized portions of the lungs were clear. Imaging of the tibia and fibula showed progressive placement of an intramedullary nail with proximal and distal transfixing screws in the tibia. Tibial length appeared to be restored and the fracture alignment appeared near anatomic. Additional screws were placed at the medial malleolus. A comminuted fracture of the fibula was also noted.

Lab results showed elevated neutrophils (88.9*), lymphocytes (5.2*), and white blood cell count (17.4*). Glucose (106*) and urea nitrogen (24*) levels were also elevated. 

The patient was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. He was given perioperative antibiotics and anticoagulation per routine. His home medications, Albuterol sulfate and Omeprazole, were continued throughout his hospitalization. 

Upon discharge, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, his pain was well controlled with oral medications, his incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and he was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. He was touch down weight bearing with an air cast boot on his right lower extremity. His discharge diagnoses were right tibial shaft and fibular shaft fracture, and right medial malleolar fracture. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a past medical history of Type 2 Diabetes, Hypertension, Peripheral Vascular Disease, Hypothyroidism (post partial thyroidectomy, thyroid nodule), and a history of Bell's palsy. She has undergone a cholecystectomy and hysterectomy in the past. She presented with dizziness, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. She also experienced an allergic reaction to dye, resulting in transient hypotension. The patient reported feeling dizzy at her dentist's office the morning prior to admission, which she described as both vertiginous and presyncopal. She also noted her vision seemed "white", although she was successfully able to drive a car. In the emergency department, she developed a fever of 102.9, nausea, vomiting, and watery diarrhea. She also had a series of allergic reactions to medications including Zofran, CT contrast dye, and Ceftriaxone. 

The patient's father had a myocardial infarction and her mother had open heart surgery and heart cancer. Her sister has a heart condition and there is a history of diabetes and thyroid disease on her mother's side of the family. 

Upon admission, her vitals were 98.6 126/65 110 18 96%RA. Her physical exam was largely unremarkable. Her labs showed elevated glucose at 223, total CO2 at 17, calcium at 7.0, phosphate at 1.9, magnesium at 1.5, and WBC at 5.1. Her RBC was low at 3.97, HGB at 11.1, and HCT at 33.7. Her lactate was elevated at 3.3 but trended down after IVF. Her WBC was elevated at 13.6 but trended down to 5.9. She became hypoNa at 131 after IVF. 

Her chest X-ray showed patchy opacity, likely atelectasis. Her CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a moderate amount of liquid stool without evidence of obstruction or intra-abdominal abscess, and a moderate-sized hiatal hernia. She had mild pruritis after administration of IV contrast, possibly a contrast reaction. 

Her discharge labs showed low RBC at 3.89, Hgb at 11.0, Hct at 33.0, and glucose at 62. Her calcium was low at 7.0 and phosphate at 1.2. 

Her primary diagnosis upon discharge was acute gastroenteritis. Her secondary diagnoses were hypertension and diabetes mellitus. Her discharge medications included cilostazol, clopidogrel, Lantus Solostar, HumaLOG, Levothyroxine Sodium, Lisinopril, and Neutra-Phos.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a female with a history of hypertension, hypothyroidism, type 2 diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, shoulder pain, back pain, knee pain, Bell's palsy, arthritis, smoking, hysterectomy with ovaries left in, thyroid nodule post right thyroid lobectomy and isthus excision, peripheral vascular disease post left hallux amputation and left SFA recanalization and stenting to treat left hallux gangrene, dermatofibroma, and abnormal pap smear. She has a significant allergy history including penicillins, sulfa antibiotics, ciprofloxacin, Mavik, Diovan, adhesive tape, aspirin, Nifedipine, Cyclobenzaprine, chlorthalidone, pseudocholesteine deficiency, simvastatin, IV dye, Zofran, ceftriaxone, Crestor, Decadron, Solu-Cortef, gabapentin, Benadryl, and latex.

She presented with asymptomatic hypotension, intermittent vomiting for 3 weeks, and was found to have a CT scan concerning for metastatic malignancy. She also reported a 10lb weight loss due to decreased oral intake and carb intake, back and hip pain in her right sacral area, fatigue over the past several weeks, and chronic nausea/vomiting/diarrhea that worsened a few weeks ago. She also reported chronic mild dry cough, but no hemoptysis. She denied any fevers, chills, abdominal pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, swelling, or urinary symptoms. Overnight, she had some dizziness with standing and her blood pressure dropped to 94/58 from 132/75. She was given 1L of IV fluids and also had diarrhea overnight.

Her family history is significant for an aunt on her father's side who recently had a stroke and is receiving comfort care. Her father had a heart attack and blood clots. Her mother had "heart cancer" and melanoma of the eye and skin.

Upon admission, her labs were notable for a new microcytic anemia (Hgb 10.1 from 13.5 baseline), increased creatinine (Cr 1.8 from 1.1 baseline), and hyponatremia (Na 126 from 136). CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast revealed new extensive retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy concerning for malignancy, a 4.2 cm retroperitoneal mass that may represent a partially calcified lymph node, increased size of 3.0 cm right adnexal soft tissue lesion adjacent to suture line from prior hysterectomy that may represent the right ovary or enlarging adnexal mass, new irregular lytic lesion in the right ischium/posterior right acetabulum, numerous new pulmonary nodules in the bilateral lung bases suggesting metastatic malignancy. She received 1L NS, lorazepam 0.5, and metoclopramide.

She underwent an EBUS with biopsy. The patient was found to be orthostatic. An a.m. cortisol was low. She underwent a cosyntropin stimulation test, with appropriate response in cortisol. This diagnosed her with secondary adrenal insufficiency, possibly due to pituitary compression. Given her history of steroid allergy, she was trialed on methylprednisolone. She tolerated the trial well. She was discharged on 4 mg of methylprednisolone. Her home lisinopril and chlorthalidone were held at discharge.

Her discharge diagnosis included concern for metastatic disease and secondary adrenal insufficiency. Her discharge medications included methylprednisolone 4 mg PO DAILY, cilostazol 100 mg Oral BID, Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY, HumaLOG (insulin lispro) 100 unit/mL Subcutaneous BEFORE MEALS as directed per sliding scale, Lantus Solostar (insulin glargine) 100 unit/mL (3 mL) Subcutaneous QHS 30 units qhs, Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO DAILY, Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO 1X/WEEK, Tacrolimus 0.1 % topical BID. Her Lisinopril, Chlorthalidone, and Cyclobenzaprine were held at discharge.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to an infection in her left toe ulcer. She has a history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM), Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) with multiple angioplasties and a left Superficial Femoral Artery (SFA) stent, and a metastatic neuroendocrine tumor with brain, lung, lymph node, and bone metastases. She is on palliative everolimus and octreotide. She also had a previous amputation of her left big toe. 

During her stay, a bedside debridement of her left foot ulcer was performed. The podiatry team did not recommend any further operative intervention. The patient was seen by the infectious disease team due to her numerous drug allergies, and they recommended linezolid. She was advised to follow up with podiatry in a week and to continue daily betadine dressings to the left toe. 

Her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.88), Hgb (10.2), Hct (31.3), MCV (81), and Glucose (111). Upon discharge, her labs still showed abnormal values for Hgb (10.4), Hct (31.9), MCV (80), MCH (25.9), and Glucose (134). 

The patient's medication upon admission included cilostazol, clopidogrel, insulin lispro, levothyroxine sodium, lisinopril, tacrolimus, insulin glargine, methylprednisolone, diphenoxylate-atropine, everolimus, and loperamide. 

The patient has a significant number of allergies including penicillins, sulfa, ciprofloxacin, Mavik, Diovan, adhesive tape, aspirin, nifedipine, cyclobenzaprine, chlorthalidone, pseudocholesteine deficiency, simvastatin, iodinated contrast media, Zofran, ceftriaxone, Crestor, Decadron, Solu-Cortef, gabapentin, Benadryl, latex, and Gadavist. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of foot ulcer and cellulitis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. However, she required assistance or aid for ambulation. She was advised to continue her antibiotics for a total of 10 days and to follow up with the podiatrists.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of metastatic neuro-endocrine (carcinoid) tumor, type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) complicated by a recent toe ulcer/cellulitis, and multiple drug allergies, presented to the emergency department (ED) with malaise. She reported feeling extremely fatigued and had a hard time getting out of bed. She also reported some loose stools that self-resolved. She denied experiencing headache, neck stiffness, cough, vomiting, abdominal pain, or dysuria. She was hypotensive but given IVF and remained normotensive so was discharged. However, her malaise/fatigue continued so her son brought her back to the ED. 

Initial vitals in the ED were 98.1 115 110/62 10 95% RA. T max 102.6. WBC 11.6, Hgb 11.5, plt 231, AST 55, Lipase 87, CK 118, CHEM w/ Cr of 1.1, lactate 1.0, Mg 1.5. UA was negative for infection. Trop <0.01. CXR showed no acute process. CT Head showed no acute intracranial process, partially visualized right parietal mass with surrounding edema not substantially changed compared to prior MR, and previously seen enhancing right hypothalamic lesion. EKG showed sinus rhythm, low voltage, precordial leads, anteroseptal infarct, old. No significant change compared with previous ECG. 

During her time in the ED, her TMax increased to 102, and she became encephalopathic. She was broadened to bacterial meningitis dosing and admitted for further care. During the time that she was febrile, she was hypoxic to 88%RA, but CXR was unchanged. 

The patient has a past oncologic history of a well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor (carcinoid tumor) confirmed by MRI brain, which showed suspicion of brain metastases. She has undergone radiation for 4 brain metastases with SRS and cyber knife and has been on octreotide and daily everolimus. Her past medical history also includes arthritis, Bell's palsy, cigarette smoking, gastrointestinal issues, hypertension, multiple medication allergies, right axillary lymphadenopathy, s/p carpal tunnel surgery, s/p cholecystectomy, s/p hysterectomy, s/p ulnar nerve reconstruction, thyroid nodule, peripheral vascular disease, back pain, dermatofibroma, mole, leg pain, rash, peripheral vascular disease, drug allergy, 90-day prescriptions, gynecologic issues, back pain, knee pain, brain metastases, and h/o chickenpox. 

Her family history includes heart disease, transient ischemic attack, eye loss, diabetes type I in her mother, myocardial infarction in her father, valve prolapse, hearing loss in her sister, sudden cardiac death in another relative, pneumonia, obesity in her daughter, blood pressure fluctuations, gout in her son, Crohn's disease, hearing problems in her granddaughters, myocardial infarction in another relative, PTSD, depression, obesity, stress in another granddaughter, and stroke in her aunt. 

On physical exam, she was found to have erythema of the left toe, erythema surrounding the neck with superficial desquamation at the right/lower margin, which the patient notes is chronic. Her left toe looked stable to her. 

Her admission labs showed abnormal findings including WBC 11.3, MCV 80, RDW 16.0, AST 55, Lipase 87, Glucose 134, Chloride 95, and Lactate 2.1. Her interval labs showed abnormal findings including RBC 3.53, Hgb 9.2, Hct 29.5, MCHC 31.2, RDW 15.8, Neuts 78.5, Lymphs 10.5, Eos 0.7, AbsNeut 6.93, AbsLymp 0.93, K 3.4, Calcium 8.0, and Phos 2.0. Her discharge labs showed abnormal findings including WBC 12.5, RBC 3.52, Hgb 9.2, Hct 28.5, MCV 81, RDW 16.5, Glucose 231, Calcium 8.2, and Phos 1.3. 

Her imaging results showed no acute intracranial process, partially visualized right parietal mass with surrounding edema not substantially changed compared to prior MR, previously seen enhancing right hypothalamic lesion, no acute cardiopulmonary process
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, history of chickenpox, and previous surgeries including cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction, was admitted due to a gangrenous left foot hallux. She reported dropping a dish on her foot several months prior, which led to darkened discoloration and worsening pain. She was given a one-week course of Clindamycin, which improved the erythema at the base of her left hallux. She denies any fever, chills, nausea, or vomiting and does not use any ambulatory devices.

Upon physical examination, her left hallux appeared gangrenous without any bogginess or fluctuance. There was localized erythema at the base of the left hallux. No tenderness to palpation or malodor was noted. Her vital signs were stable with a temperature of 98.2, blood pressure of 172/114, heart rate of 110, respiratory rate of 18, and oxygen saturation of 95% on room air.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a left digit amputation and lower left extremity angioplasty. Radiology reports showed severe end-stage osteoarthritic changes involving the first MTP joint, distal SFA versus popliteal disease on the left, and likely tibial disease. There was no radiographic evidence for osteomyelitis. Post-amputation, there was extensive soft tissue abnormality but no fracture or subluxation.

Lab results showed an elevated RDW of 16.0 and glucose of 139 upon admission. At discharge, her glucose had risen to 301, her RDW was 15.8, and her Vancomycin level was 8.6. 

Her home medications, including amlodipine, atorvastatin, lantus, levothyroxine, and lisinopril, were continued during her hospital stay. She was discharged with instructions to remain partial weight-bearing to her left heel in a surgical shoe, physical therapy, two weeks of post-operative antibiotics, and daily dressing changes.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for elective angioplasty to address stenosis in her left lower extremity, specifically in her prior SFA stent. This was due to a non-healing ulcer on her left toe. Prior to the procedure, she underwent a duplex which showed patent distal SFA stent with progressive diffuse stenosis within the stent, and a drop in the left ankle-brachial index to 0.62. 

Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, a thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, a history of chickenpox, and right axillary pain. She has also undergone cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction. She has a history of Bell's palsy. 

The patient has allergies to penicillins, sulfa antibiotics, ciprofloxacin, Mavik, Diovan, adhesive tape, aspirin, nifedipine, cyclobenzaprine, chlorthalidone, and has a pseudocholesteine deficiency. 

Upon admission, she was on lantus 58 qam, lisinopril 40', levothyroxine 25' M through F, 50' on weekends, and chlorthalidone 12.5 every other day. 

Her physical exam upon discharge showed normal vital signs, with no organomegaly or other abnormalities. Her pulses were palpable and dopplerable on the left, and palpable but not dopplerable on the right. 

Lab results showed a glucose level of 330* and a chloride level of 95*, both of which are abnormal. Other lab results, including WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, Plt, PTT, UreaN, Creat, Na, K, HCO3, AnGap, Calcium, Phos, and Mg were all within normal ranges. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent angioplasty of the left distal SFA stent in-stent stenosis and popliteal artery. Postoperatively, she was stable with no issues, and her creatinine level was 0.7, which was at her baseline. She was discharged home in good condition with instructions to follow up in one month's time with a duplex of her left lower extremity. 

Upon discharge, her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was able to ambulate independently. Her discharge diagnosis was a left toe ulcer with distal SFA instent stenosis, post stent and popliteal angioplasty.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to bilateral lower extremity claudication. She underwent a percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) of bilateral popliteals. The procedure was well-tolerated and she was transferred to the PACU in stable condition. She remained in bedrest for two hours, her diet was advanced, and she was restarted on her aspirin and clopidogrel therapy. She had no new events overnight and was discharged in stable condition.

Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, history of chickenpox, and right axillary pain. She has also undergone cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction. She has a history of Bell's palsy. She has no known family history of any significant diseases.

The patient has multiple allergies including penicillins, sulfa, ciprofloxacin, Mavik, Diovan, adhesive tape, aspirin, nifedipine, cyclobenzaprine, chlorthalidone, pseudocholesteine deficiency, and simvastatin. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including acetaminophen, chlorthalidone, clopidogrel, docusate sodium, levothyroxine sodium, glargine insulin, nicotine lozenge, and lisinopril. 

Her physical examination on the day of discharge was generally unremarkable. She was alert and oriented, with regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft, non-tender, non-distended abdomen. There was no evidence of hematoma or bruising in both groins and her pulses were palpable. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of bilateral lower extremity claudication and diabetes. Her discharge condition was stable, with clear and coherent mental status, and she was ambulatory and independent.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of kidney stones, presented with a chief complaint of a swollen left knee and a fever. He reported waking up with a markedly swollen and tender knee, which he could barely flex. He also reported a fever of 100.1 and 101.8 on two separate occasions. The patient had previously injured the same knee in a softball game but did not seek medical attention at the time. He reported no recent trauma to the knee, no scrapes or broken skin, and no recent illnesses. He was seen by his primary care physician and sent to the emergency department (ED). 

In the ED, the patient's joint was aspirated, and no organisms were seen. He was discharged with Keflex. However, the culture grew Gram-positive cocci (GPCs), and he was called back to the ED. By this time, he had taken several doses of Keflex, and his knee was clinically improved with less pain and improved range of motion. He was evaluated by orthopedics in the ED, who recommended intravenous Vancomycin for possible septic bursitis. 

The patient's physical exam revealed erythematous and warm left knee joint with mild discomfort on passive range of motion. His temperature was 100.1, blood pressure 128/60, pulse 88, respiratory rate 18, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for joint fluid WBC (2500), HCT (4), POLYS (66), BANDS (1), SED rate (63), and CRP (188.6). The joint fluid culture was positive for methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA). Knee films showed extensive soft tissue swelling, but no acute fracture or dislocation was identified. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was initially treated with Keflex, which resulted in resolution of erythema. He was returned to the hospital once joint cultures showed small growth of MSSA. Orthopedics believed the condition to be septic bursitis and not septic arthritis, given the patient's adequate range of motion. The patient was placed on IV Vancomycin while awaiting sensitivities, and he showed continued improvement in his knee and remained afebrile. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Keflex. The patient's family history is unknown as he is adopted. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of septic bursitis in good condition.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to lung nodules. His chief complaint was a cough that he developed a while back. A Chest CT revealed a 5mm RML nodule, which has been monitored yearly. Bilateral pulmonary nodules were also documented. A CT-guided biopsy of a LLL nodule was non-diagnostic. The patient also underwent a CT of the abdomen and pelvis, which showed liver lesions and some other lesions of unclear etiology. The differential included infection, neoplasm, and vasculitis. A colonoscopy was performed and found to be normal. The patient's pulmonary symptoms include cough and some mid-chest pressure when he feels more congestion. He denies shortness of breath, dyspnea on exertion, weight loss, recent upper respiratory infection/pneumonia, foreign travel, etc. He smoked for a short time about several years ago but none since. He works every day in a family business and is exposed to lots of dust, sewerage, etc.

The patient's past medical history includes kidney stones, RML lung nodule, and MRSA in the left knee and nose. He has also undergone bilateral hernia repairs. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His family history is unknown as he is adopted.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a VATS RML wedge resection x 2 and RLL wedge resection x 1. Post-operation, he was stable, oxygenating well on room air, and ambulating independently. His chest X-ray showed no pneumothorax or consolidation. His chest tube was removed as the post-pull film showed no pneumothorax. His pain was well controlled on oral pain medications. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking over-the-counter cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) and a daily multivitamin. Abnormal lab results included a glucose level of 127* and a creatinine level of 1.3*. His chest X-ray prior to chest tube removal showed no appreciable pneumothorax, right pleural drainage tube in place, no appreciable pleural effusion, moderately extensive atelectasis and biopsy artifact persisting in the right mid and lower lung zones, and moderate atelectasis developed on the left. Left and right upper lobes were clear, and the cardiomediastinal silhouette was unremarkable. 

The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and ambulatory independently. His discharge diagnosis was lung nodules.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of spontaneous abortion followed by dilation and curettage (D&C), hypothyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, possible gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and vasovagal syncope in childhood, presented with progressive sensory changes. She reported odd paresthesias to light touch over her lower legs and feet, which later extended to include her thighs, waist, and up to nipple level. She denied any abnormality at rest and was only aware of the difference when she touched her skin. An urgent MRI scan was arranged, which showed several enhancing lesions in the dorsal cord, as well as several lesions that did not enhance. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with normal muscle bulk and tone throughout. She reported an odd sensation to light touch, which included the legs and extended up the trunk to the level of ~T4 bilaterally. She believed that the right side was perhaps more affected. 

Admission labs showed an elevated absolute neutrophil count (7.46*) and glucose level (112*). Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed 1 red blood cell (RBC*). Imaging revealed multiple T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions throughout the cervical and thoracic spinal cord, suggestive of demyelinating disease. There were also scattered white matter lesions in the bilateral cerebral hemispheres and the right cerebellar hemisphere. 

The patient's family history includes a father with significant hypertension and cholesterol, a mother with coronary artery disease (CAD) and Graves' disease, and a brother who is alive and well. The patient's only medication upon admission was Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY for her hypothyroidism. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's symptoms improved. She denied bladder or urinary incontinence. Sensation in the bilateral lower extremities was improved, although there were still residual changes to light touch sensation in legs and torso. Using McDonald criteria, the patient was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis (MS). She was started on a 3-day course of 1g of IV methylprednisolone. Several tests were sent prior to discharge to expedite workup. 

Upon discharge, the patient's mental status was clear and coherent. She was alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. Her neuro exam showed decreased light touch (mixed with dysesthesia) in the medial legs and bilateral torso (below ~T4 on the right and below ~T6 on the left).
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted following an accident where she was struck by an automobile while crossing the street. She lost consciousness and woke up in the ambulance.
