She was then transported to the hospital for further management. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She has allergies to Penicillins and Tetracycline. 

The patient underwent an Open Reduction and Internal Fixation (ORIF) of a mandible fracture. Postoperatively, she was initially sleepy, likely due to the anesthesia and narcotics. She had difficulties navigating stairs, which was a concern as her home has 35 stairs. However, she was eventually cleared for home by Physical Therapy with services. 

Lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 137, a low potassium level of 3.0, and a low total CO2 level of 20. Her Hematocrit (HCT) was low at 35.3 and Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) was also low at 80. Her lymphocyte count was high at 43.9. 

A head CT scan revealed no acute intracranial process, a left frontal/supra-orbital scalp soft tissue hematoma without skull fracture, and sinus disease. A mandibular series did not clearly identify a right mandibular ramus fracture on the plain films. 

During her hospital stay, she was evaluated by Oral Maxillo Facial Surgery for her mandible fracture. She was given intraoperative antibiotics. She was instructed on a full liquid and soft diet and advised to increase her protein intake. She was provided with wire cutters for her wired shut jaw and scheduled for a follow-up in the clinic the following week. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis included a mandible fracture, lip and facial lacerations, and knee abrasions. She was hemodynamically stable, tolerating a regular diet, and her pain was adequately controlled. She was discharged home with service.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for an elective right total knee replacement due to progressive right knee pain with activity. His past medical history includes osteoarthritis, a tonsillectomy, knee arthroscopy, and prostate cancer treated with radiation therapy. He has an allergy to NSAIDs. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was closely monitored and remained hemodynamically stable. He tolerated the knee replacement procedure well and there were no complications. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the PACU and floor for further recovery. His pain was controlled and he remained hemodynamically stable. However, he required a transfusion due to expected postoperative anemia. He made progress with physical therapy to improve his strength and mobility. 

Upon physical examination at discharge, the patient was afebrile with all vital signs stable. He was alert and oriented, with no acute distress. His right lower extremity was partially weight bearing. The incision was intact with no swelling, erythema, or drainage. The dressing was clean, dry, and intact. Extensor/flexor hallicus longus was intact, sensation was intact to light touch, neurovascular was intact distally, capillary refill was brisk, and 2+ pulses were present. 

The patient was not on any medications upon admission. His discharge diagnosis was osteoarthritis and his condition at discharge was stable. He was discharged home with service.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for an elective L4-S1 lumbar fusion due to lumbar spondylosis. His chief complaint upon admission was right leg pain. His past medical history includes hypercholesterolemia and a right upper lobe opacity on CT. He has no known allergies to drugs. 

The patient underwent a major surgical procedure, specifically an L4-S1 posterior lumbar interbody fusion with pedicle screws and bone marrow aspirate. Post-operatively, his pain was initially managed with a PCA, which was later discontinued in favor of oral agents. He was able to void independently after his Foley was discontinued. Physical therapy evaluated him and cleared him for home. His surgical wound was covered with steri strips and was clean, dry, and intact upon discharge. 

Post-operative X-rays of the lumbar spine showed proper alignment with no evidence of hardware complication. There was mild dextroconvex scoliosis with its apex at L4. No fractures were demonstrated. Soft tissue changes dorsally over the operative site likely reflect recent surgical changes with soft tissue gas noted. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's drain was discontinued and there were no issues with drainage. His maximum temperature over a 24-hour period was recorded and a urinalysis was ordered, which came back negative. He was ambulating independently at the time of discharge. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Oxycodone as needed. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His activity status was independent and ambulatory. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of lumbar spondylosis. His discharge instructions included wound care, activity restrictions, pain management, and dietary recommendations. He was advised to call his surgeon immediately if he experienced any increasing pain, weakness, numbness, tingling in his extremities, signs of infection at the wound site, fever, or changes in bowel or bladder habits. He was also instructed to call the office for urine culture results and to continue using his incentive spirometer.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a male of undisclosed age who was admitted due to severe back and leg pain that had been worsening over the course of 18 months. His pain was so severe that it was poorly controlled with IV opioids and was affecting his ability to work. He had previously tried conservative management for his pain with little relief. He was scheduled for surgical repair of a ruptured L4-L5 disc in the coming weeks. The patient denied having diabetes mellitus, intravenous drug use, recent back procedures, urinary incontinance, urinary retention, or fecal incontinance. His past medical history includes gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). He has no known drug allergies and was not on any medications upon admission. His social history includes rare alcohol consumption and smoking, but no illicit drug use.

Physical examination revealed the patient was alert and oriented, but in severe pain. Sensory and motor examinations were normal. His upper extremities were clean and intact with no signs of tenderness, deformity, erythema, edema, induration, or ecchymosis. His arms and forearm compartments were soft and he had no pain with passive motion. His radial pulses were 2+.

During his hospital stay, the patient was considered for surgery for his herniated lumbar disc. However, due to surgical emergencies, the operation was delayed. In the meantime, the patient's pain was managed with IV decadron, and oral oxycontin and oxycodone. As the patient became comfortable and was able to ambulate on oral pain medications, he was discharged home to follow up on his scheduled operation day the following week.

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a herniated lumbar disc. His condition was good and he was instructed not to do any heavy lifting or significant twisting/bending. His discharge medications included acetaminophen, immediate release oxycodone, and extended release oxycodone.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient is a female who was admitted due to worsening depressive symptoms and suicidal ideation. She has a history of depressive episodes but no previous psychiatric admissions or suicide attempts. She reported persistent thoughts of suicide over the last few weeks, along with symptoms of major depression such as loss of interest in activities, inability to see or invest in the future, lack of appetite, trouble concentrating, hopelessness, and a generalized feeling of being "lost." She also has a history of sexual trauma. The patient reported a three-month worsening of depressive symptoms, including anhedonia, poor concentration, low motivation, fatigue, diminished appetite, and suicidal ideation. She denied having any supports in the community and cited school and financial stressors as contributing to her worsening mood. 

The patient has a past medical history of asthma and Achilles tendonitis. She has no known drug allergies but is allergic to alcohol. She is not on any medications. She denies any family psychiatric history. 

On physical examination, the patient was well-groomed and appeared younger than her stated age. Her neurological examination was normal. Her mental status examination revealed a restricted, anxious affect, linear thought process, and fair judgement. She had some active and passive suicidal ideation but denied any hallucinations. Her insight was limited. 

Lab results showed an abnormal MCHC of 30.5 and RDWSD of 47.1. Her eosinophil count was also low at 0.7. All other lab results were within normal limits. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was placed on 15-minute checks status and remained unit restricted. She was diagnosed with an unspecified depressive disorder. The patient was provided with individual and group therapy focusing on meditation, self-awareness, and coping skills. She showed improvement in mood and was future-oriented. She denied suicidal ideation and demonstrated fair insight and good judgment. 

The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of unspecified depressive disorder. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was in a good mood congruent with euthymic, reactive affect, and denied suicidal ideation. She was advised to follow up with all outpatient appointments and to avoid abusing alcohol and any drugs.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, was referred for ERCP due to a biliary tumor. He was recently diagnosed with cholangiocarcinoma when he presented with jaundice and pruritis. He has a 3 cm complex hilar biliary stricture and has undergone two ERCPs. He was scheduled for a curative Whipple procedure, which was aborted due to the discovery of multiple peritoneal implants. This was a surprise to the patient and his family as previous CT scans did not show this. The patient has regained some weight since the placement of a plastic stent during ERCP. His initial jaundice has resolved. 

The patient underwent major surgical procedures including ERCP with metal stent placement, percutaneous cholecystostomy tube placement, and PICC placement. He reported pain at the site of the laparoscopy wounds and in his shoulder after the procedure. He also reported a weight loss of 7 lbs over the past month. 

The patient's family history includes his maternal grandfather and grandmother who died from heart disease. His uncle had diabetes and died of heart disease, and another uncle died of lung cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) 5 mg PO Q4H for pain and Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q6H for anxiety. 

Abnormal lab results include a WBC count of 16.8, RBC count of 4.49, MCH of 32.1, and AlkPhos of 319. The patient's blood culture showed the presence of Escherichia Coli. 

The patient's ERCP showed a complex tight 3cm hilar stricture. The right anterior and posterior intrahepatics were visualized, but the left intrahepatic duct could not be seen. A sphincterotomy was performed and two 8cm by 8mm uncovered metal stents were successfully placed in the right anterior and posterior intrahepatic ducts. 

CT scan showed diffuse intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation, central hepatic vascular stenosis and effacement with a central poorly defined hepatic lesion suggestive of cholangioCA/ Klatskin tumor. Other diagnostics showed a complex stricture at the bifurcation, a 14 x 21 mm mass in the CHD, and multiple areas of nodularity lateral to the falciform ligament. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by acute cholangitis and E. coli septicemia, as well as portal vein thrombosis and evidence of liver infarct. His condition upon discharge was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male of unspecified age, was admitted due to increasing right upper quadrant pain. He has a history of cholangiocarcinoma, for which he has had multiple stents placed to relieve biliary obstruction. He has also undergone a laparotomy which revealed peritoneal metastases, ruling out a previous plan for a Whipple procedure. He has been receiving palliative chemotherapy with gemcitabine and cisplatin, with the last infusion two weeks prior to this admission. He presented with fevers, worsening right upper quadrant pain, and increasing icterus. He also reported that his stools have looked whiter, and urine appears darker. He has been managing his pain with minimal oxycodone, but reported that the pain is increasing beyond his baseline. He denies shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, dizziness, lightheadedness, diarrhea, numbness, tingling, and dysuria. 

During this admission, he underwent an ERCP with placement of 2 metal stents. A CT scan of his abdomen revealed no progression of his tumor; however, laboratory assessment revealed leukocytosis and a rising alkaline phosphatase (>600). He also developed acute fever with subsequent epigastric abdominal pain. Blood cultures returned positive for GNR, despite being on Unasyn. He was changed to Zosyn/Cipro and a CT was performed, showing dilatation of his left hepatic lobe biliary system, and possibly early abscess formation. A PTC was placed, with improvement in his symptoms. Nearly pan-resistant E. coli was grown, and he was transitioned to Imipenem. His blood cultures were cleared and a PICC line was placed. He was discharged on Meropenem 1g IV q8 for at least 2 weeks. 

The patient's family history includes his maternal grandfather and grandmother dying from heart disease. His uncle had diabetes and died of heart disease. Another uncle died of lung cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were stable and he was in no apparent distress. His abdomen was soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant, with bourborygmi noted. His extremities were warm and well perfused with no edema. His discharge physical was largely unremarkable, with mild tenderness to palpation in the right upper quadrant, and a right-sided PICC in place. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (15.9), RBC (3.55), Hgb (11.8), Hct (35.4), MCV (100), MCH (33.2), RDW (16.6), Neuts (78.0), PTT (37.5), Glucose (101), ALT (58), AST (50), AlkPhos (661), TotBili (3.5), and Albumin (3.3). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.41), Hgb (11.2), Hct (34.3), MCV (101), MCH (32.8), RDW (15.7), AlkPhos (522), and Phos (5.1). 

Imaging showed intrahepatic mass focused at the porta hepatis, consistent with known cholangiocarcinoma, unchanged chronic occlusion of the left portal vein with cavernous transformation, and stable positioning of intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. ERCP showed obstruction of 2 stents in the biliary tree with evidence of pus behind the stents. Two metal stents were placed to relieve obstruction and biliary duct aspirate was sent for culture. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Lorazepam, OxycoDONE, Docusate Sodium, Senna, and Enoxaparin Sodium.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Stage IV Cholangiocarcinoma, presented with fevers and worsening right upper quadrant pain. He reported a significant increase in abdominal pain unresponsive to his usual dose of oxycodone 5mg, which he had been taking once daily. He also reported associated nausea but denied vomiting. He had a history of cholangitis and had been treated for the same in the past. His ERCP showed obstruction of 2 stents in the biliary tree with evidence of pus behind the stents. He was discharged on a 10-day course of meropenem for resistant E.Coli and augmentin for enterococcus. 

Upon admission, the patient was febrile with a temperature of 103.7. His labs showed a normal WBC and lactate, but an obstructive pattern on LFTs. His CT scan showed no intra-abdominal process and the biliary stents appeared patent. He was hypotensive despite receiving 3L NS, leading to his admission to the ICU. 

The patient's past medical history includes a diagnosis of cholangio CA after presenting with a 20 lb weight loss, jaundice, and pruritis. He had undergone ERCP with plastic stent placement and had atypical cells on cytology. He had also undergone a vasectomy. His family history includes his maternal grandfather and grandmother dying from heart disease, an uncle with diabetes who died of heart disease, and another uncle who died of lung cancer. 

On physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with no acute distress. He had tenderness in the epigastrium and RUQ, but no peritoneal signs. His labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, Neuts, Lymphs, Monos, Glucose, ALT, AST, AlkPhos, and TotBili. His LLE U/S showed no evidence of deep venous thrombosis in the left lower extremity. His CXR showed no acute cardiopulmonary process. His CT A/P showed improved bile duct dilation compared to the prior study and continued nonvisualization of the left portal vein and narrowing of the main portal vein due to an ill-defined mass. His ERCP showed 2 metal stents placed in the biliary duct were partially occluded from the food debris. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with acute cholangitis with bile obstruction and metastatic cholangiocarcinoma to liver/peritoneum. He was treated with antibiotics per most recent culture data showing resistant E coli and enterococcus. He underwent ERCP and PTC, with a PTBD left in place in the R anterior biliary system. He was also diagnosed with portal vein thrombosis/liver infarct and was on Lovenox for the same. 

His medications upon admission included Docusate Sodium, Enoxaparin Sodium, Lorazepam, OxycoDONE, Senna, Ondansetron, and Prochlorperazine.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of stage IV cholangiocarcinoma and portal vein thrombosis, was admitted for evaluation of abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fevers. He reported increasing abdominal pain, nausea, decreased oral intake, fevers, and diarrhea over the past two days. He denied any chest pain, dyspnea, vomiting, or dysuria. His last chemotherapy treatment was three days prior to admission. 

Upon admission, his vitals were recorded and labs showed a WBC count of 11.1K with 87% neutrophils, bicarbonate of 18, ALT of 57, AST 46, AP 218, total bilirubin 0.4. Lactate was 1.1. Urinalysis showed 3 WBC, with no bacteria, 6 RBC, small blood. He was administered ciprofloxacin and metronidazole, as well as hydromorphone 2 mg IV x 2. Blood cultures x 2 and urine culture were sent. CT A/P preliminarily showed a liver lesion with narrowing of the left portal vein and focal narrowing of the main portal vein, similar to prior, and stable biliary stents. The small and large bowel loops were without evidence of obstruction, but fluid-filled suggesting possible gastroenteritis. 

The patient has a history of cholangiocarcinoma, for which he initially presented with diarrhea and jaundice. He underwent two ERCP procedures, during which biliary stents were placed. Cytology was initially inconclusive but on follow up study showed adenocarcinoma. He was referred for surgical exploration and planned resection, but the procedure was aborted as peritoneal metastases were identified intraoperatively. Biopsy confirmed the finding of metastatic adenocarcinoma morphologically consistent with pancreaticobiliary primary. His postoperative course was complicated by E. coli bacteremia.

The patient's mother has hypertension. His father died with NASH cirrhosis. His brother has no health concerns. He has three children, a son with irritable bowel syndrome and a daughter with a disease. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent PTBD replacement, ERCP, and PICC placement. His abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fever were evaluated with a differential diagnosis of gastroenteritis, cholangitis, biliary infection, and abscess. His CT scan showed no evidence of a biliary process, but fluid-filled intestines were observed. Stool studies, including C. Diff, were negative. His condition initially improved on cipro/flagyl, but worsened after PTBD replacement. This was thought to be secondary to instrumentation and spread of infectious source/post-procedure cholangitis. These symptoms resolved within 24 hours of the procedure. Bile cultures during this admission grew 2 strains of E.coli, including one very resistant strain, and sparse enterococcus. Vancomycin was started given concern for PTBD site infection. Blood cultures from admission remain negative. 

The patient's creatinine level increased to 1.6 from 1.0, likely due to decreased oral intake and large output from the biliary drain that is connected to the bowel. This improved to 1.2 with IVF and capping of his PTBD. His vancomycin dose was adjusted according to his renal function. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with MDR E. coli Cholangitis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. He was instructed to continue taking IV antibiotics at home, specifically Vancomycin through a certain date and Zosyn through another date.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Stage IV cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to fever and abdominal pain. He has been treated with gemcitabine/cisplatin and has had three hospitalizations for cholangitis caused by resistant E. coli bacteremia in the past year. He reported constant right upper quadrant (RUQ) abdominal pain, fevers up to 103.8'F, paler stools, and darker urine. His labs showed an elevated alkaline phosphatase (AP) and hemoglobin (Hgb) of 7.6, down from 9.9 during a prior hospitalization. A liver ultrasound showed some biliary radicals but no abscesses or fluid collections. 

The patient's past medical history includes two ERCP procedures where biliary stents were placed. Cytology initially was inconclusive but later showed adenocarcinoma. He was referred for surgical exploration and planned resection, but the procedure was aborted due to the identification of peritoneal metastases intraoperatively. His postoperative course was complicated by E. coli bacteremia. He also has a history of portal vein thrombosis. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with hypertension, a father who died from NASH cirrhosis, and a brother with no health concerns. He has three children, one of whom has irritable bowel syndrome and another with an unspecified disease. 

During his hospital stay, a percutaneous cholangiogram was performed with replacement of the internal-external bile drain. His labs showed several abnormalities including a low RBC count of 2.11, low Hgb of 7.2, high MCV of 103, high MCH of 33.9, high RDW of 16.5, high ALT of 47, high AST of 49, high LDH of 350, high AP of 319, and low albumin of 3.4. His urine test showed 8 RBCs and 3 hyaline casts. His discharge labs showed a continued low RBC count of 2.64, low Hgb of 8.9, high MCV of 102, high MCH of 33.6, high RDW of 16.4, and high AP of 240. 

Imaging showed intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation in the liver, a thickened and collapsed gallbladder, and a CBD stent. No definite fluid collections were identified. A biliary cath check showed an obstructed existing right percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage catheter with mildly dilated intrahepatic bile ducts and an occluded stent. The catheter was successfully exchanged. 

Microbiology results showed mixed bacterial flora in the bile and at the biliary drainage site, including Enterococcus sp., Klebsiella oxytoca, and Escherichia coli. No anaerobes were isolated. Urine and blood cultures showed no growth. 

The patient was diagnosed with recurrent cholangitis and was treated with Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5g IV every 8 hours. His anemia was likely due to chronic inflammation, with macrocytosis caused by reticulocytosis. His portal vein thrombosis was managed with enoxaparin. His cholangiocarcinoma is being managed by his oncologist. 

Upon discharge, the patient was instructed to continue Piperacillin-Tazobactam until a specified date and to recheck CBC, AST, ALT, AP, and total bilirubin on a specified date. His discharge medications included Piperacillin-Tazobactam, Ursodiol, Docusate Sodium, Ondansetron, Senna, Enoxaparin Sodium, Lorazepam, Oxycodone, and Prochlorperazine.
label: training_controls

note_summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to abdominal pain. He has a history of stage IV cholangiocarcinoma and is currently on gemcitabine/cisplatin. He was previously admitted with cholangitis and treated with zosyn. During that admission, a cholangiogram showed a clogged biliary drain which was replaced. The patient reported that his pain never fully resolved and has worsened over the past few days, with associated fevers reaching 101.5 at home and nausea without vomiting. 

The patient has a past medical history of portal vein thrombosis. His oncologic history includes initial presentation with diarrhea, followed by jaundice, which led to medical evaluation. He underwent two ERCP procedures where biliary stents were placed. Cytology was initially inconclusive but follow-up study showed adenocarcinoma. He was referred for surgical exploration and planned resection, but the procedure was aborted due to the identification of peritoneal metastases. His postoperative course was complicated by E. coli bacteremia.

The patient's family history includes a mother with hypertension, a father who died from NASH cirrhosis, and a brother with no health concerns. He has three children, one of whom has irritable bowel syndrome and another with an unspecified disease.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Ursodiol, Docusate Sodium, Ondansetron, Senna, Enoxaparin Sodium, Lorazepam, OxycoDONE, and Prochlorperazine. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an IR guided bile duct change catheter procedure due to his abdominal pain. The procedure was successful and the patient's bile duct catheter was working and not obstructed. His liver function tests were stable. He was given dilaudid 1 mg iv x 2 and admitted to OMED. 

Lab results showed several abnormal findings including glucose at 104*, ALK PHOS at 173*, WBC at 12.1*#, RBC at 2.99*, HGB at 10.2*, HCT at 31.4*, MCV at 105*, MCH at 34.0*, PLT COUNT at 118*#, and AlkPhos at 221*. 

The patient's discharge diagnosis included cholangitis, bile duct cancer, and cholangiocarcinoma. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. He was discharged home with service.
label: training_controls
